name: [24]7.ai
summary: Enterprise customer engagement AI provider [24]7.ai suffered a major 2017-2018 data breach affecting client payment data, faced lawsuits, and underwent significant workforce restructuring despite remaining privately operating.
lesson: A security failure at a customer-engagement vendor handling sensitive payment data can trigger years of reputational and financial damage even without an outright shutdown.
source: Reuters and SEC filing coverage of the [24]7.ai 2017-2018 data breach

name: 22seven
summary: South African personal finance and budgeting app was discontinued by owner Old Mutual in 2023 after over a decade of operation failed to produce a standalone profitable business.
lesson: Personal finance aggregator apps often struggle to monetize directly, making them vulnerable to being cut once a parent reassesses ROI.

name: 23andMe
summary: Consumer genomics company shut down its therapeutics division after failing to produce a viable pipeline, then filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy amid a data breach scandal.
lesson: A massive proprietary genetic dataset is a research asset, not automatically a drug discovery business.

name: 24 Hour Fitness
funding: Private equity owned (Forstmann Little, later AEA/Fitness Capital)
summary: Gym chain 24 Hour Fitness filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2020, closing over 100 clubs after pandemic shutdowns devastated revenue under a heavy debt load.
lesson: Highly leveraged, capital-intensive physical fitness chains have little buffer against a sudden demand shock.

name: 2U Inc.
summary: Online program management company that powered university degrees and acquired edX filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after years of stock decline and unsustainable university revenue-share contracts.
lesson: Revenue-share contracts with universities can become structurally unprofitable as the market matures.

name: 38 Studios
summary: Curt Schilling's studio shipped Kingdoms of Amalur: Reckoning but collapsed under debt from a $75M Rhode Island state-backed loan before its planned MMO ever shipped.
lesson: Betting a studio's survival on an unshipped MMO while carrying huge state-backed debt is a fatal combination.

name: 3D Robotics
summary: Drone manufacturer that abandoned consumer hardware after massive losses in 2015-2016, pivoted to construction/mapping software (Site Scan), and eventually wound down that business as well.
lesson: Competing on drone hardware against low-cost incumbents is a losing battle; pivoting to software after burning through hardware capital rarely leaves enough runway to succeed.
source: TechCrunch, IEEE Spectrum coverage of 3D Robotics history

name: 3D Robotics (3DR)
summary: 3D Robotics exited consumer and agricultural drone hardware manufacturing in 2015 after being outcompeted by Chinese manufacturer DJI, pivoting to enterprise drone software instead.
lesson: Hardware startups competing against a vertically integrated low-cost manufacturer need a clear differentiation beyond the airframe itself.

name: 3DO Company
summary: Trip Hawkins' console-and-publishing venture never recovered from the costly failure of its 3DO Interactive Multiplayer hardware, eventually filing for bankruptcy.
lesson: Launching proprietary game hardware against entrenched console makers requires far more capital than most startups can raise.

name: 4C Insights
summary: Social and TV ad-tech platform 4C Insights shut down in 2019, selling some assets, after failing to achieve profitability in the competitive cross-channel ad measurement space.
lesson: Cross-channel measurement platforms need a clear cost advantage or proprietary data to outlast better-funded rivals.
source: AdExchanger, Chicago Tribune coverage of 4C Insights shutdown 2019

name: 51Talk
summary: China-based online English tutoring company that IPO'd in 2016 but saw its stock collapse over 95% after the 2021 tutoring crackdown forced a pivot to overseas markets.
lesson: Diversifying geographic exposure early can matter more than domestic market share.

name: 5miles
summary: Local classifieds and secondhand marketplace app raised heavy funding but was shut down around 2019 after failing to compete with letgo and OfferUp.
lesson: Heavily funded but undifferentiated marketplace apps struggle once category leaders consolidate share.

name: 6 River Systems
summary: Warehouse collaborative-robot maker (Chuck) was acquired by Shopify for $450M in 2019, then shut down in 2023 after Shopify exited the logistics business and sold its fulfillment assets to Flexport.
lesson: A robotics unit's fate can hinge entirely on its parent company's own strategic pivots, independent of its own performance.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Shopify's logistics exit and 6 River Systems shutdown, 2023

name: 8base
summary: GraphQL backend-as-a-service and low-code platform that shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Backend-as-a-service platforms compete directly with well-funded hyperscaler offerings (Firebase, AWS Amplify), which raises the bar for differentiation.

name: 8fit
summary: Workout and nutrition app 8fit was shut down in 2023 after its parent company failed to find a sustainable path in the crowded fitness app market.
lesson: Even a well-funded fitness app under a media conglomerate can be discontinued if it can't differentiate from larger competitors.

name: 8i
summary: 8i, a volumetric video capture startup for VR/AR holographic content, wound down operations around 2020 after failing to reach sustainable commercial scale despite high-profile partnerships.
lesson: Volumetric capture technology required expensive studio infrastructure that outran demand for holographic VR/AR content.
source: Road to VR reporting on 8i wind-down, 2020

name: 8tracks
summary: Independent playlist-sharing streaming radio service that operated for 17 years before shutting down in 2023 citing an unsustainable business amid streaming giant competition.
lesson: Niche streaming services face relentless margin pressure once the market consolidates around a few well-capitalized giants.

name: A123 Systems
summary: MIT spinout A123 built lithium-iron-phosphate batteries for EVs and grid storage but filed for bankruptcy in 2012 after costly recalls and thin automotive margins; assets were sold to Wanxiang.
lesson: Being first to market with new battery chemistry doesn't protect against quality failures and margin pressure from automotive customers.

name: Aaptiv
summary: Audio-based workout app Aaptiv shut down in 2023 after failing to differentiate against free and video-based fitness content competitors.
lesson: An audio-only fitness content model struggled to retain subscribers once video-based and free alternatives proliferated.

name: AAX (Atom Asset Exchange)
summary: Hong Kong-based crypto exchange suspended withdrawals in November 2022 and was later found to have misused customer funds, ceasing operations amid the post-FTX confidence crisis.
lesson: A crisis of confidence across the industry exposes exchanges that were already commingling or misusing customer funds.
source: AAX withdrawal suspension announcements and subsequent investigations, 2022-2023

name: ABL Space Systems
summary: Small-launch vehicle developer lost its RS1 rocket in a January 2023 launch failure and lost a second vehicle in a July 2024 static-fire fire, leading to two rounds of layoffs and a strategic pivot away from commercial launch toward missile-defense interceptor work.
lesson: Consecutive vehicle losses in a capital-starved small-launch market can force a pivot away from the original business entirely.

name: Abound Solar
summary: Thin-film cadmium telluride solar panel maker that shut down after being unable to compete with falling silicon panel prices from China.
lesson: A cost advantage in thin-film solar evaporated once conventional silicon panel prices fell faster than expected.

name: Abstract
summary: Design version-control platform aiming to be 'Git for designers' that shut down in 2023 as design workflows consolidated around Figma's built-in collaboration.
lesson: Point solutions solving one workflow problem are at risk when platform incumbents absorb that functionality natively.

name: Abundant Robotics
summary: Apple-picking robotics startup Abundant Robotics, spun out of SRI International, ceased operations in late 2021 after struggling to commercialize its harvesting robots at scale.
lesson: Field-robotics products must handle far more variability than lab conditions before they can be commercially deployed profitably.

name: Acclaim Entertainment
summary: Aggressive licensing deals and mediocre movie tie-in games buried the publisher in debt until it liquidated in 2004.
lesson: Betting the business on movie-licensed games is a low-margin, high-risk strategy.

name: Achaogen
summary: Won FDA approval for antibiotic Zemdri in 2018 but filed for bankruptcy within a year because hospitals rarely used novel antibiotics, generating almost no revenue.
lesson: Antibiotics face a broken market where clinical need doesn't translate into reimbursed volume.

name: Acorns
summary: Micro-investing and banking app's planned SPAC merger, valuing it near $2.2 billion, was called off in 2022 amid a market downturn for fintech valuations.
lesson: A collapsed public-listing plan can strand a fintech's growth strategy even if the underlying product keeps operating.

name: Adconion Media Group
summary: Global ad network Adconion Media Group had several executives criminally charged by the US Department of Justice for wire fraud related to hijacking IP address blocks for ad inventory, severely damaging the company before its assets were sold off.
lesson: Ad-tech supply-side fraud, even at the executive level, can permanently taint a company's ability to operate.
source: DOJ press release, AdExchanger coverage of Adconion IP hijacking case 2018

name: Adly
summary: Celebrity and influencer marketing platform Adly, an early mover in influencer advertising, shut down around 2016 after failing to build a scalable, repeatable business model.
lesson: Being an early mover in a new advertising category isn't enough without a repeatable, scalable sales motion.
source: AdWeek, Digiday reporting on Adly wind-down

name: Adobe Advertising Cloud
summary: Adobe shut down its Advertising Cloud DSP business in 2023, exiting the ad-buying market entirely after years of trying to compete with Google and The Trade Desk, and laid off the associated team.
lesson: Even a large enterprise software company can fail to break into ad-tech if it can't match scale players' data and reach.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday coverage of Adobe Advertising Cloud shutdown 2023

name: Aereo
summary: Aereo let subscribers stream local broadcast TV via tiny remote antennas, bypassing retransmission fees. The Supreme Court ruled this violated copyright law, forcing immediate shutdown.
lesson: Building a media business on an untested legal loophole is a bet on courts, not customers.

name: AeroFarms
summary: Vertical farming pioneer AeroFarms filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2023 after failing to reach profitability despite over a decade of operation and heavy investment.
lesson: Novel growing technology alone cannot overcome persistently negative unit economics.

name: AeroVironment agricultural drone unit
summary: AeroVironment's agricultural imaging drone product line was discontinued around 2021 as the company refocused entirely on defense-sector drone contracts.
lesson: Diversified drone manufacturers often exit lower-margin agricultural applications when higher-margin defense contracts offer a better return on R&D.
source: Industry press, AeroVironment product line coverage

name: Africrypt
summary: South African crypto investment platform vanished in 2021 after its teenage founders allegedly disappeared with an estimated $3.6 billion in client Bitcoin, one of the largest alleged crypto frauds ever.
lesson: Platforms promising outsized trading returns with opaque strategies and no independent custody are classic fraud red flags.

name: AgBiome
summary: Biological crop-protection company AgBiome laid off most of its staff in 2023 and ceased core operations in 2024 after failing to commercialize its microbial discovery platform at scale.
lesson: Ag-biologicals discovery platforms require very long development timelines that can outlast the patience of venture funding cycles.

name: AgEagle Aerial Systems
summary: Agricultural and enterprise drone maker built via a string of acquisitions (senseFly, MicaSense, senseFly) has posted years of losses and repeated restructurings, with its stock down over 95% from its 2021 peak.
lesson: Rolling up multiple drone-hardware acquisitions doesn't create synergy if the combined business still can't reach profitability.
source: AgEagle SEC filings and market coverage of stock decline

name: AgriProtein
summary: AgriProtein was an early leader in insect-based protein for animal feed, building large-scale fly farms, but it filed for bankruptcy in 2019 after a large planned funding round collapsed.
lesson: Scaling capital-intensive novel-protein infrastructure ahead of secured, committed funding leaves a company exposed to a single failed raise.

name: Airobotics
summary: Israeli maker of automated 'drone-in-a-box' industrial inspection systems went public via SPAC in 2022 but wound down commercial operations later that year amid mounting losses.
lesson: A SPAC listing provides capital but not a fix for an underlying business that cannot reach profitable unit economics.
source: Globes / Calcalist coverage of Airobotics wind-down, 2022

name: Airspace Systems
summary: Counter-drone (anti-drone interception) startup raised significant venture capital but shut down around 2020 as the counter-UAS market proved smaller and more regulation-bound than expected.
lesson: Security markets tied to government procurement cycles can move far slower than a startup's venture-funded runway allows.
source: Trade press coverage of counter-drone startups, 2020

name: Airspan Networks
summary: 5G and Open RAN hardware vendor filed prepackaged Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2024 after Open RAN adoption grew far slower than expected.
lesson: Building capacity for a market inflection point that arrives years late can bankrupt a company waiting for it.

name: Airware
summary: Drone operating-system and analytics company serving construction, mining, and insurance that shut down in 2018 after pivoting away from hardware failed to find a sustainable business.
lesson: Pivoting from hardware to software mid-flight can strand a company without a clear competitive moat in either market.
source: TechCrunch, The Verge coverage of Airware shutdown

name: Akili Interactive
summary: Maker of EndeavorRx, the first FDA-cleared video-game-based digital therapeutic for ADHD, went public via SPAC but struggled commercially and sold its core assets in 2024 after running low on cash.
lesson: FDA clearance alone doesn't guarantee payer coverage or consumer willingness to pay.

name: Akudo
summary: Indian neobank for teenagers and young adults shut down in 2023 after failing to raise a follow-on round in a cooling funding environment.
lesson: Youth-focused neobanking is a crowded niche where undifferentiated players cannot outlast a funding winter.

name: Al Jazeera America
summary: Qatar-backed Al Jazeera's U.S. cable and digital news channel shut down after less than three years due to low ratings and an inability to secure widespread cable distribution.
lesson: Heavy capital investment cannot overcome a structurally difficult distribution environment for a new entrant news brand.

name: Alameda Research
summary: Sam Bankman-Fried's crypto trading firm collapsed alongside FTX in November 2022 after it emerged the firm had been using billions in FTX customer deposits to cover trading losses.
lesson: A trading firm with privileged, undisclosed access to an affiliated exchange's customer funds is a fraud waiting to be discovered.

name: Algorithmia
summary: Machine learning model deployment and MLOps platform that was acquired for parts by DataRobot after struggling to monetize ahead of the MLOps market maturing.
lesson: Being early to MLOps meant burning cash years before enterprise budgets and workflows caught up to the category.

name: Allbirds
summary: Sustainable footwear/apparel D2C brand's stock collapsed over 95% from IPO highs by 2025, with the company disclosing 'substantial doubt' about its ability to continue as a going concern before attempting a speculative pivot to AI infrastructure in 2026.
lesson: A single hero product and premium pricing rarely sustain growth once competitors copy the design and category hype fades.
source: Seeking Alpha, Forbes, Investing.com coverage of Allbirds 2025-2026 financial distress

name: Allied Esports Entertainment
funding: Nasdaq listing via reverse merger with Black Ridge Acquisition Corp
summary: The Nasdaq-listed owner of the World Poker Tour and Esports Arena venues saw its stock collapse after its SPAC-style listing as venue and event revenue badly underperformed projections.
lesson: Physical esports venues carry heavy fixed costs that don't scale down gracefully when attendance disappoints.

name: AllThingsD
summary: Kara Swisher and Walt Mossberg's influential tech-news site under The Wall Street Journal ended in 2013 after a contract dispute with Dow Jones, prompting the founders to depart and launch Re/code.
lesson: A media brand's value is often inseparable from its star journalists, who can walk and rebuild elsewhere when contract terms sour.

name: Alooma
summary: Israeli data-pipeline/ETL startup acquired by Google Cloud in 2019; Google discontinued the standalone product around 2022 in favor of its own Data Fusion offering.
lesson: Being acquired by a cloud hyperscaler often means the acquired product gets absorbed and eventually replaced by the parent's native tooling.
source: Google Cloud Alooma acquisition and later sunset notices

name: AltaRock Energy
summary: AltaRock Energy pursued enhanced geothermal systems using engineered fracturing to access deep heat, but its flagship Newberry Volcano project was abandoned after inducing seismic concerns, and the company wound down operations.
lesson: Enhanced geothermal fracturing techniques carry induced-seismicity risks that can halt a project regardless of the underlying resource potential.

name: AltspaceVR
funding: $15M raised prior to acquisition by Microsoft in 2017
summary: AltspaceVR, an early social VR platform acquired by Microsoft in 2017, was permanently shut down by Microsoft in 2023 as the company consolidated its mixed-reality strategy around Mesh and HoloLens.
lesson: Being acquired does not guarantee longevity; a parent company's strategic pivot can still shut down a previously acquired social VR product.

name: Amazon Care
summary: Amazon's in-house telehealth and home-visit service for employees and enterprise clients was shut down in 2022 as Amazon pivoted its healthcare strategy toward acquiring One Medical.
lesson: Even deep-pocketed corporate backers will kill a healthcare bet that doesn't show a path to scale.

name: Amazon Fire Phone
summary: Amazon's foray into smartphones with a 3D display flopped commercially, forcing a $170M inventory write-down and discontinuation within a year.
lesson: Gimmicky hardware features don't substitute for a compelling reason to switch platforms.

name: American Apparel
summary: Vertically-integrated 'Made in USA' fashion retailer filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy twice (2015 and 2016) amid founder scandal, debt, and sales declines, and fully liquidated its US operations in 2017.
lesson: Founder misconduct scandals compound financial distress and can accelerate an already-troubled retailer into liquidation.
source: Wall Street Journal, Reuters coverage of American Apparel bankruptcies 2015-2017

name: Amigo Loans
summary: UK guarantor loan provider entered insolvency proceedings in 2024 after a wave of mis-selling compensation claims overwhelmed the business.
lesson: Guarantor and high-cost consumer lending is especially exposed to regulator-driven redress schemes.

name: Amiigo
summary: Crowdfunded fitness tracker startup Amiigo failed to deliver its product to backers and shut down in 2014 after running out of funding.
lesson: Crowdfunding campaign success does not guarantee sufficient capital or engineering capacity to actually manufacture a hardware product.

name: Amity
summary: Customer success management platform Amity faded from the market and was acquired by Community.com in 2022, discontinuing its original product line.
lesson: In a maturing SaaS category, mid-sized players without a clear wedge get acquired for talent or customer lists rather than scaling independently.
source: Industry press coverage of Amity's 2022 acquisition

name: Amyris
summary: Synthetic biology company Amyris originally aimed to produce renewable diesel and jet fuel from engineered yeast but pivoted repeatedly toward consumer products before filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023.
lesson: Repeated strategic pivots away from a founding energy mission can signal an unresolved core unit-economics problem rather than solve it.

name: Analytical Space
summary: Satellite data-relay and optical downlink startup ceased operations in 2022 after failing to raise additional funding to scale its relay constellation.
lesson: Data-relay infrastructure businesses need scale to be profitable, but scale requires capital that dries up quickly once growth stalls.

name: Anki
summary: Maker of consumer robotics toys shut down abruptly after failing to close a final funding round, despite selling over 1.5 million robots.
lesson: Consumer robotics toy margins can be too thin to sustain the R&D costs of building lovable hardware.

name: Anorak
summary: UK digital life insurance advice platform that used algorithms to recommend coverage amounts ceased operations in 2022 after struggling to build a large enough distribution channel.
lesson: Life insurance advice tools need a strong distribution partnership or paid acquisition engine to reach meaningful scale.

name: Answer Financial
summary: Online insurance shopping and comparison subsidiary majority-owned by Allstate was sold to Confie Seguros in 2016 after failing to become a durable stand-alone growth engine for its parent.
lesson: Insurance comparison sites owned by a single carrier struggle to compete with independent, carrier-agnostic aggregators.

name: Antenna Software
summary: Early enterprise mobile app platform that failed to transition its licensing model to the cloud era and was absorbed into Verivo Software.
lesson: Legacy enterprise software vendors that fail to re-architect for cloud delivery become acquisition targets rather than survivors.

name: Anybots
summary: Silicon Valley telepresence robot maker (QB) failed to find a scalable enterprise market for its self-balancing telepresence robots and effectively ceased operations around 2015.
lesson: Telepresence robotics needed video-conferencing infrastructure and workplace habits that had not yet matured when Anybots launched.
source: Trade press coverage of Anybots wind-down

name: AnyPresence
summary: Enterprise mobile backend and app-platform startup that ceased independent operations in 2016 after failing to scale against larger MBaaS competitors.
lesson: Enterprise mobile backend platforms needed either massive scale or a defensible niche to survive the MBaaS shakeout of the mid-2010s.
source: Industry reporting on mobile backend-as-a-service consolidation

name: Appcelerator
summary: Cross-platform mobile app development platform acquired by Axway in 2016; the product was declared end-of-life around 2020 as Axway shifted strategy.
lesson: Mobile-first no-code/low-code platforms from the early smartphone era struggled to keep up once native and web-based alternatives matured.

name: AppFog
summary: PaaS startup acquired by CenturyLink in 2013 and then quietly shut down as the PaaS market consolidated around Heroku and AWS.
lesson: Early PaaS entrants without durable differentiation got squeezed once telecom and cloud giants entered the space.

name: AppHarvest
summary: Kentucky indoor tomato-growing company went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in January 2024 after years of losses and construction cost overruns.
lesson: Capital-intensive indoor farming requires proven unit economics before scaling to multiple facilities.

name: AppMachine
summary: Dutch no-code app builder acquired by GoDaddy in 2015 to power its website/app bundle; GoDaddy discontinued the product in 2018.
lesson: A strategic acquisition to bundle into an acquirer's product line often has a limited shelf life once strategy shifts again.
source: GoDaddy AppMachine acquisition and later discontinuation coverage

name: AppMakr
summary: One of the earliest DIY mobile app builders, it quietly shut down around 2018 after losing ground to more modern no-code platforms.
lesson: First-mover advantage in no-code tooling erodes quickly if the platform fails to modernize its underlying technology.
source: Industry retrospectives on early app-builder platforms

name: Aptera Motors (original)
summary: The original Aptera Motors, developer of an ultra-efficient three-wheeled vehicle, shut down in 2011 after failing to secure a Department of Energy loan and running out of cash before production.
lesson: Relying on a single large government loan as the bridge to production leaves a company extremely exposed to political and bureaucratic risk.

name: Aptonomy
summary: Autonomous drone security startup merged with Scout Security in 2018 after failing to independently scale its automated aerial surveillance product for commercial real estate.
lesson: Early autonomous-drone security startups often need to merge or be absorbed before regulation and enterprise demand mature enough to sustain them.
source: Trade press coverage of Aptonomy-Scout Security merger, 2018

name: Apttus
summary: The quote-to-cash and CLM unicorn carried a heavy debt load and was recapitalized by Thoma Bravo in 2020, re-emerging as Conga after existing equity was largely wiped out.
lesson: Debt-heavy growth financing can force a painful recapitalization even for a large, revenue-generating company.

name: aQuantive
funding: public company, acquired by Microsoft for $6.3B in 2007
summary: Microsoft acquired the digital marketing conglomerate aQuantive for $6.3 billion in 2007 to compete with Google, then wrote off the entire value of the deal in 2012 after failing to integrate or monetize it.
lesson: Overpaying for a strategic acquisition without a clear integration plan can destroy the value entirely.
source: New York Times, Reuters coverage of Microsoft aQuantive writedown 2012

name: Aquion Energy
summary: Maker of saltwater grid-storage batteries backed by Bill Gates and Kleiner Perkins that filed for bankruptcy after failing to reach profitable scale.
lesson: Novel battery chemistries need a credible path to cost parity with lithium-ion before scaling factories.

name: Argo AI
summary: Self-driving AI startup backed by Ford and Volkswagen shut down after both automakers concluded fully autonomous robotaxi tech was too far from commercial viability.
lesson: Deep-pocketed corporate backers can still walk away when a capital-intensive AI bet's timeline stretches too long.

name: Arkane Austin
summary: Microsoft closed the Redfall developer after the live-service vampire shooter launched to poor reviews and low player counts, misjudging the studio's strengths.
lesson: Pushing a single-player-focused studio into an unfamiliar live-service genre can produce a launch failure that costs the studio its existence.

name: Arrival
summary: UK-based EV van maker Arrival, which promised low-cost micro-factories, filed for bankruptcy in 2024 after repeated restructurings failed to bring any vehicle to volume production.
lesson: An unproven manufacturing innovation narrative can attract huge SPAC-era valuations without de-risking the actual production process.

name: Arrivo
summary: Hyperloop-adjacent high-speed ground transportation startup founded by a former Hyperloop One executive that shut down in 2018 after failing to secure a route deal in Colorado.
lesson: Infrastructure-dependent transportation startups need a committed government partner before spending down capital on engineering.

name: Ashley Madison
summary: Dating site marketed toward extramarital affairs; suffered a catastrophic 2015 hack that exposed over 30 million users' personal data, triggering lawsuits and lasting brand damage.
lesson: A business model built on user secrecy makes a data breach existential rather than merely embarrassing.

name: Aspiration
summary: Sustainability-focused neobank collapsed in 2024 amid mounting debts, and a co-founder was later charged criminally over carbon-offset fraud tied to the company's climate deals.
lesson: A mission-driven brand does not substitute for verifiable unit economics, and fraud allegations can unwind a neobank overnight.

name: Assembly OSM
summary: Detroit-based modular/offsite home manufacturer that ceased manufacturing operations around 2023 amid funding and demand challenges.
lesson: Offsite manufacturing plants for affordable housing require steady, guaranteed order pipelines to stay solvent.
source: Detroit business press coverage of Assembly OSM

name: Assist
summary: Conversational commerce and chatbot-building startup Assist shut down in 2018 after businesses failed to adopt bot-based customer interactions at the pace investors expected.
lesson: Chatbot platforms that arrived ahead of reliable conversational AI struggled to prove ROI before capital ran out.

name: Assurance IQ
summary: Insurance comparison and sales platform sold to Prudential Financial for $2.35B in 2019 saw Prudential write down the business and shut major parts of it after underperforming projections.
lesson: A blockbuster acquisition price doesn't guarantee the acquired business model survives integration into a legacy insurer.

name: Astra Space
summary: Small satellite launch and spacecraft engine company came close to bankruptcy after repeated launch failures and cash shortfalls, ultimately taken private in 2024.
lesson: Rushing a minimum-viable rocket to market to hit investor timelines multiplies both technical and financial risk.

name: Astralis Group
funding: IPO on Nasdaq First North 2019, raised ~$14M
summary: The Danish CS:GO and League org went public on Nasdaq's First North Growth Market in 2019, then saw its share price collapse over 95% amid COVID event cancellations and declining competitive results before being delisted in 2023.
lesson: Taking an esports org public assumes recurring event and sponsorship revenue that can vanish overnight in a crisis.

name: Atheer
summary: Atheer, an enterprise AR software company for remote assistance and frontline worker training, shut down in 2022 after failing to reach profitability amid a crowded enterprise AR software market.
lesson: The enterprise AR software space became crowded enough that even well-funded early movers could not sustain differentiation and revenue.
source: AR Insider reporting on Atheer shutdown, 2022

name: Atlas Solutions
funding: acquired by Microsoft 2007 as part of aQuantive, sold to Facebook 2013
summary: Ad-serving platform Atlas was acquired by Facebook in 2013 to build cross-device ad measurement, but Facebook shut the product down in 2017 as it consolidated ad tools internally.
lesson: Acquired ad-tech products can be discontinued once the acquirer's internal roadmap changes, regardless of the product's quality.
source: Digiday, AdExchanger coverage of Atlas shutdown 2017

name: Atlas Wearables
summary: Fitness tracker startup Atlas Wearables shut down operations in 2017 after struggling to scale sales beyond its crowdfunding base.
lesson: Crowdfunded hardware traction often fails to translate into a sustainable retail sales channel.

name: Atria Senior Living (home care division)
summary: The licensed home care agency arm of major senior living operator Atria; it shut down its New York home care business in January 2025, laying off 161 workers to refocus entirely on core senior living communities.
lesson: Even large, well-capitalized senior living companies can find home care a margin-thin adjacency not worth the operational complexity.

name: Atrium
summary: Justin Kan's hybrid law-firm-plus-software startup aimed to serve startups' legal needs but shut down after failing to find a scalable model combining legal services with software.
lesson: Bundling a labor-intensive law firm with a software product does not automatically produce software-like margins.

name: Attabotics
summary: Canadian 3D warehouse robotics/goods-to-person storage startup that ceased operations and filed for insolvency in 2024 after failing to secure additional financing.
lesson: Novel warehouse hardware architectures require sustained capital far beyond initial pilots to reach profitable scale.

name: Audacy
summary: Satellite data-relay network startup building a constellation to provide near-continuous connectivity to other satellites shut down in 2022 after running out of cash before completing its constellation.
lesson: Infrastructure plays that require a full constellation before generating revenue are especially exposed to funding gaps.

name: Audiogalaxy
summary: Early peer-to-peer music-sharing service with a popular recommendation engine, forced to shut its file-sharing feature after an RIAA lawsuit.
lesson: Consumer-loved features built on unlicensed content are fragile against copyright enforcement.

name: Automat.ai
summary: Conversational AI marketing and support automation startup Automat.ai ceased operations around 2020 after failing to gain enough enterprise traction to raise further funding.
lesson: Early conversational AI vendors that depended on immature NLP struggled to deliver the accuracy enterprises required for customer-facing deployment.
source: Crunchbase and industry press records on Automat.ai's closure

name: Automate.io
funding: Undisclosed seed funding, acquired by Notion 2021
summary: iPaaS workflow automation tool acquired by Notion in 2021 and fully discontinued in 2023 as Notion built native automation instead.
lesson: An acquirer may value the team and IP more than the product itself, leading to eventual sunset.

name: Automatic Labs
summary: Maker of the Automatic car adapter for driving analytics shut down its consumer app and hardware in 2020 after failing to build a large enough subscriber base against free automaker apps.
lesson: Aftermarket connected-car hardware struggles once OEMs bundle equivalent connectivity features for free.

name: Avegant
funding: $41M+ raised, including a Kickstarter for the Glyph headset
summary: Avegant launched the Glyph, a consumer light-field VR/video headset via Kickstarter, but abandoned the consumer VR market by 2017 to pivot entirely to licensing its display technology to enterprise AR partners.
lesson: A consumer hardware crowdfunding success does not guarantee a sustainable consumer business, and pivoting to B2B licensing can be a survival move rather than growth.
source: Road to VR reporting on Avegant's pivot away from consumer VR, 2017

name: Avibra
summary: US app offering free micro life insurance coverage monetized through data and rewards partnerships shut down in 2022 after failing to convert its free user base into sufficient revenue.
lesson: Giving away insurance coverage as a growth hook still requires a credible monetization path to cover claims and operating costs.

name: Axiom
summary: The pioneering alternative legal services company underwent major layoffs, leadership turnover, and financial restructuring around 2023 after years of aggressive expansion.
lesson: Scaling a talent-intensive legal services model globally requires matching headcount growth tightly to actual client demand.

name: Axovant Sciences
summary: Bought Alzheimer's candidate intepirdine from GSK; the drug failed its phase 3 trial in 2017, and the company wound down its neuroscience pipeline and rebranded.
lesson: Licensing a deprioritized asset from a major pharma is often a signal, not a bargain.

name: Ayasdi
summary: An AI platform used for anti-money-laundering and regulatory compliance workflows ran into financial trouble after raising over $100M and sold its assets in a distressed 2021 deal.
lesson: Heavily-funded 'platform AI' companies can still fail if they never land a defensible, repeatable enterprise use case.

name: Azlo
summary: No-fee digital banking app for freelancers and small businesses, backed by BBVA, that shut down in 2021 after BBVA sold its US banking operations to PNC and discontinued the unprofitable unit.
lesson: A fintech dependent on a single incumbent-bank sponsor is exposed to that sponsor's own strategic pivots.

name: Azubu
summary: The esports-focused livestreaming platform tried to compete with Twitch through exclusive streamer deals but shut down its live-streaming service in 2016 after failing to build a large enough audience.
lesson: Exclusive streamer contracts can't overcome the network effects of an already-dominant streaming platform.

name: b8ta
summary: Retail-as-a-service startup that let D2C brands showcase products in physical stores shut down its US retail operations in 2023 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Bridging online D2C brands into physical retail carries real estate and staffing costs that are hard to offset.

name: Babel Finance
summary: Hong Kong-based crypto lender suspended withdrawals in June 2022 after suffering losses on client assets used in proprietary trading during the market downturn.
lesson: Using customer collateral for proprietary trading without clear risk limits turns a lending business into an undisclosed hedge fund.
source: Babel Finance statement on withdrawal suspension, 2022

name: Babylon Health
summary: UK-based AI-driven telehealth and symptom-checker company expanded aggressively into US value-based care contracts, burned through cash, and filed for bankruptcy/liquidation in 2023.
lesson: Scaling into risk-bearing healthcare contracts without disciplined unit economics can sink even well-funded tech.

name: Backand
summary: Low-code backend-as-a-service platform that ceased active operations around 2018 after failing to scale against larger BaaS competitors.
lesson: Thinly-capitalized backend-as-a-service startups have little room for error in a market dominated by well-funded incumbents.

name: BankMobile (Megalith SPAC deal)
summary: Digital banking platform's planned SPAC merger with Megalith Financial Acquisition Corp was terminated in 2021 after valuation and diligence disputes, derailing its public listing plan.
lesson: SPAC mergers can collapse late in the process, so companies should not treat a signed deal as a guaranteed exit.

name: Baobab Studios
summary: Baobab Studios, an award-winning VR animation studio behind titles like Invasion! and Asteroids!, significantly downsized and pivoted toward broader animation and AI-driven content after VR content funding and audiences failed to scale.
lesson: Prestige and industry awards for VR animated content did not translate into a large enough paying audience to sustain a dedicated VR-first studio.
source: Variety/Cartoon Brew reporting on Baobab Studios restructuring

name: Barkly
summary: Endpoint protection startup was acquired by Malwarebytes in 2020 for an undisclosed, reportedly modest sum after struggling to differentiate in the crowded EDR/EPP market.
lesson: Endpoint security is a scale game; smaller vendors without a distinct wedge often end up as tuck-in acquisitions rather than standalone winners.

name: Barstool Sportsbook
summary: Penn Entertainment's sports betting brand built on the Barstool Sports media property, abandoned and sold back to founder Dave Portnoy for $1 after struggling to get licensed in key states.
lesson: A controversial media personality's brand can be a liability when regulators must approve gaming licenses state by state.

name: Base CRM (Zendesk Sell)
summary: Sales CRM Base was acquired by Zendesk in 2018 and rebranded Zendesk Sell; Zendesk discontinued Sell entirely in 2024 to refocus on its core support product.
lesson: Bolt-on CRM acquisitions rarely achieve the platform synergy expected, and are often the first casualty when a parent company refocuses.
source: Zendesk's 2023 announcement of Sell product sunset

name: Basis (Basecoin)
summary: One of the best-funded algorithmic stablecoin projects shut down in December 2018 and returned investor funds after determining its bond-token mechanism would classify as an unregistered security under US law.
lesson: A promising token-economic design can be killed outright by securities law if the mechanism resembles a debt instrument.

name: Basis Cash
summary: Algorithmic stablecoin fork of the earlier Basis project lost its dollar peg and collapsed in 2021 as its seigniorage-share model failed to maintain demand during market downturns.
lesson: Seigniorage-style algorithmic stablecoins repeatedly failed across multiple projects, indicating a structural flaw rather than a one-off bug.
source: DeFi market post-mortems on algorithmic stablecoin failures, 2021-2022

name: Basis Science
summary: Made an early fitness/sleep tracking smartwatch, was acquired by Intel in 2014, then Intel recalled and permanently shut down the product in 2016 over an overheating hazard.
lesson: A safety recall inside a corporate parent with no long-term wearables strategy usually ends in the product's death, not a fix.

name: Batchbook
summary: Social CRM startup Batchbook shut down operations in 2017 after failing to scale against larger, better-funded CRM competitors.
lesson: Small CRM vendors without a wedge into a defensible niche get squeezed out as the category consolidates around a handful of platforms.

name: Bay Dynamics
summary: Cyber risk analytics vendor that had raised tens of millions quietly wound down operations around 2019 after failing to scale its enterprise risk-analytics platform against larger competitors.
lesson: Enterprise risk-analytics products with long sales cycles can burn through funding before reaching the scale needed to survive independently.

name: Bazaarvoice
summary: Ratings-and-reviews martech platform Bazaarvoice went public in 2012 but was hit by an SEC accounting investigation and a poorly integrated acquisition (PowerReviews), and was eventually taken private by Marlin Equity in 2018 at a steep discount to its IPO value.
lesson: Poorly integrated acquisitions combined with accounting missteps can permanently derail a public martech company's growth story.
source: SEC filings, Reuters coverage of Bazaarvoice take-private 2018

name: Beachbody (BODi)
funding: SPAC merger 2021, valued at $2.9B at listing
summary: Home fitness and nutrition company Beachbody went public via SPAC in 2021 but its stock collapsed over 95% amid subscriber declines and heavy losses, later rebranding as BODi.
lesson: A SPAC listing at an inflated valuation can rapidly destroy shareholder value if underlying subscriber growth doesn't materialize.

name: Beachmint
summary: Celebrity-endorsed fashion subscription commerce startup (JewelMint, ShoeMint, StyleMint) restructured and sold off its brands piecemeal after burning through funding without reaching sustainable unit economics.
lesson: Celebrity-branded subscription lines can drive initial buzz but rarely translate into durable repeat-purchase economics.

name: Beacon Power
summary: Flywheel energy storage company that filed for bankruptcy just weeks after Solyndra, another recipient of a DOE loan guarantee.
lesson: Being a pioneer in grid storage doesn't protect against running out of runway before the market matures.

name: Beal Aerospace Technologies
summary: Privately funded heavy-lift rocket developer built the world's largest privately financed rocket engine test stand, then shut down in 2000 citing government subsidies to competitors like ULA's predecessors as making private heavy-lift uneconomic.
lesson: A private venture competing directly against government-subsidized incumbents needs a cost or niche advantage that survives policy, not just engineering.

name: Beam Financial
summary: High-yield savings fintech that froze customer withdrawals in 2021 amid allegations its underlying investment strategy could not support advertised returns.
lesson: Yield promises that outpace what the underlying assets can generate are a red flag for depositors and regulators alike.

name: Beanstalk Farms
summary: Algorithmic stablecoin protocol lost $182M in April 2022 when an attacker used a flash loan to gain majority governance voting power and drained the protocol's treasury in a single transaction.
lesson: On-chain governance without safeguards against flash-loan voting power concentration is an open invitation to attack.

name: BearShare
summary: Peer-to-peer music file-sharing client sued by the RIAA, forced to pay a large settlement, and converted into a paid legal service before ultimately shutting down.
lesson: File-sharing networks built around copyrighted content face the same legal exposure regardless of technical architecture.
source: RIAA v. Free Peers settlement coverage, 2006

name: Beautycounter
funding: Sold majority stake to Carlyle Group for ~$1B in 2021
summary: Clean-beauty direct-sales brand terminated its entire consultant network in April 2024 after Carlyle Group, which bought a majority stake in 2021, decided to exit amid MLM-channel headwinds; founder Gregg Renfrew relaunched it as 'Counter' in 2025.
lesson: Direct-selling/MLM distribution channels can collapse quickly once recruitment economics turn negative, even for a well-known clean-beauty brand.
source: Business of Fashion, GeekWire, Direct Selling News coverage of Beautycounter shutdown 2024

name: Bebe Stores
summary: Women's fashion retailer closed all ~170 physical stores in 2017, unable to adapt its mall-based model to e-commerce and shifting consumer habits, continuing only as a licensed online brand.
lesson: Mall-anchored specialty retailers that delay e-commerce investment lose to digitally-native competitors.
source: Reuters, CNBC coverage of Bebe Stores store closures 2017

name: Because Animals
summary: Because Animals developed cultivated mouse-protein cat food as an alternative to conventional pet food but shut down in 2023 without reaching commercial launch.
lesson: Novel cultivated-protein categories for pets face the same capital and regulatory hurdles as human food with a smaller addressable market.

name: Beepi
summary: Peer-to-peer used-car marketplace Beepi shut down in 2017 after burning through venture funding on logistics and reconditioning costs it never brought under control.
lesson: Underestimating logistics costs in physical-goods marketplaces can erase the benefits of a lean digital front end.

name: Belly
summary: Chicago-based mobile loyalty and rewards-card platform backed by Andreessen Horowitz and NEA that failed to scale profitably, rebranded as Hatch Loyalty in 2017, and sold its product assets to Mobivity for just $3 million in 2018.
lesson: A loyalty platform's per-merchant revenue must exceed the cost of hardware and support, or growth simply accelerates cash burn.

name: Bench Accounting
summary: Online bookkeeping and tax platform for small businesses that abruptly shut down in December 2024, locking out customers from their financial records before its assets were acquired by Employer.com.
lesson: Abrupt shutdowns of financial recordkeeping services can cause disproportionate harm to small-business customers relative to the company's own losses.

name: Benson Hill
summary: Plant-breeding and food ingredient company Benson Hill, which went public via SPAC in 2022, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in July 2024 after mounting losses.
lesson: SPAC mergers can provide capital without fixing an underlying business model that isn't yet generating sustainable margins.
source: Reuters, Benson Hill Chapter 11 filing July 2024

name: Berkshire Grey
summary: Warehouse robotics and fulfillment automation company that went public via SPAC at a rich valuation, then was taken private by SoftBank at a fraction of that price.
lesson: A SPAC valuation built on future promise can collapse fast once real-world adoption lags.

name: Better Booch
summary: Better Booch was a California kombucha and functional-beverage producer that shut down its production facility in 2021 after failing to compete with larger national kombucha brands for retail shelf space.
lesson: Functional-beverage categories consolidate quickly around a few national brands, squeezing out regional producers.

name: Better Place
summary: Israeli EV battery-swap network builder burned through its funding building charging and swap infrastructure faster than EV adoption could support, filing for bankruptcy in 2013.
lesson: Building infrastructure ahead of demand in a nascent market can bankrupt a company before the market matures.
source: NYT and Fortune retrospectives on Better Place bankruptcy, 2013

name: Better.com
summary: Digital mortgage lender that grew rapidly during the pandemic refinancing boom saw its reputation and finances collapse after its CEO fired 900 employees over a Zoom call.
lesson: Scaling headcount to match a temporary rate-driven demand spike leaves a company badly overextended once rates rise.

name: Beyond Meat
summary: Plant-based meat pioneer that went public at a high valuation but has suffered years of declining sales, heavy losses, and a collapsed stock price as demand plateaued.
lesson: Early hype and a successful IPO don't guarantee that consumer demand for a sustainability-driven product category will keep growing.

name: Beyond the Rack
summary: Canadian flash-sale retail e-commerce site that expanded rapidly across North America ceased operations in 2016 after failing to reach profitability in the increasingly crowded flash-sale retail category.
lesson: Entering a retail category already crowded with well-funded competitors (Gilt, One Kings Lane, Rue La La) requires a clear differentiation to survive a shakeout.

name: Bigelow Aerospace
summary: Inflatable space habitat pioneer, which flew the BEAM module to the ISS, laid off its entire workforce in March 2020 citing the COVID-19 downturn and never resumed full operations.
lesson: A company reliant on a single deep-pocketed founder and a still-nascent commercial space-station market has no buffer against an external shock.

name: BIMA (Milvik)
summary: Mobile microinsurance provider serving emerging markets in Asia, Africa and Latin America underwent major restructuring and divested several country operations in the early 2020s after failing to reach profitability at scale.
lesson: Microinsurance's low premiums require enormous distribution scale before per-policy margins can cover acquisition and claims costs.

name: BIME Analytics
summary: French cloud BI platform acquired by Zendesk in 2015 to build Zendesk Explore; the standalone BIME product was discontinued in 2018.
lesson: An acquisition aimed at absorbing technology into a new product line often ends the acquired brand's independent life.
source: Zendesk BIME acquisition and Explore transition announcements

name: Biogen (Aduhelm)
summary: Alzheimer's drug Aduhelm received a controversial accelerated FDA approval over its own advisory committee's objections, then was rejected by insurers and withdrawn from market in 2024.
lesson: An approval secured against expert consensus does not guarantee physician adoption or payer reimbursement.

name: Birchbox
summary: Pioneer of the beauty subscription box model, Birchbox was sold for parts to investment firm Viking Global Investors in 2021 after years of down-rounds and failed sale attempts, well below its peak valuation.
lesson: A first-mover subscription concept can be commoditized quickly once retailers and rivals copy the model at lower cost.
source: TechCrunch, Business Insider coverage of Birchbox sale to Viking Global, 2021

name: Bird
summary: Electric scooter-sharing pioneer that scaled rapidly across hundreds of cities but never achieved sustainable unit economics; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in December 2023.
lesson: Rapid geographic expansion without solving vehicle durability and repair costs burns cash faster than growth can offset.

name: Birst
summary: Cloud BI platform acquired by Infor in 2017 for roughly $150M; the Birst brand was ultimately discontinued as Infor consolidated its analytics stack around 2022.
lesson: Raising very large sums for a BI platform doesn't guarantee an independent future if the eventual acquirer folds the brand into its own roadmap.
source: Infor Birst acquisition and later product consolidation

name: Bitconnect
summary: Crypto lending platform promising outsized guaranteed returns via a proprietary trading bot shut down after regulators issued cease-and-desist orders, revealing a Ponzi scheme worth over $2B in losses.
lesson: Guaranteed fixed returns with an opaque, unaudited strategy are the classic signature of a Ponzi scheme.

name: Bittrex
summary: Early major US crypto exchange shut down its US operations in 2023 after settling SEC charges for operating an unregistered exchange, and later wound down globally.
lesson: Operating in a regulatory gray zone for years can catch up with a company once regulators formalize enforcement priorities.
source: SEC press release on Bittrex settlement, 2023

name: Bizness Apps
summary: DIY mobile app builder for small businesses that shut down operations in 2019 as the market for generic white-label app builders shrank.
lesson: White-label no-code app builders targeting SMBs face brutal price competition and shrinking demand as native mobile web improves.

name: Bizongo
summary: Indian B2B packaging materials marketplace that underwent a steep valuation down round and major restructuring around 2023-2024 after a strategic pivot away from its original marketplace model.
lesson: Pivoting a marketplace's core business model mid-scale-up can strand both supplier relationships and investor confidence.
source: Indian tech press (Entrackr/Inc42) coverage of Bizongo's restructuring and down round

name: Blendle
summary: A Dutch 'iTunes for news' platform that let readers pay per article for premium journalism failed to reach scale and pivoted away from its original micropayment model before effectively shutting down.
lesson: Micropayments for individual news articles have repeatedly struggled against readers' preference for either free content or flat subscriptions.

name: Blinkbox Music
summary: UK music streaming service backed by retailer Tesco that shut down after less than two years as its parent retrenched from digital media investments.
lesson: Corporate-backed media ventures are vulnerable when the parent company's strategic priorities shift.

name: Blip.fm
summary: Twitter-integrated music discovery and DJ-style sharing service that failed to convert its early buzz into a lasting business and shut down in 2013.
lesson: Dependence on a third-party platform's API and audience graph creates fragile distribution that can collapse if that platform's dynamics change.

name: Blippar
summary: Augmented-reality marketing platform Blippar fell into administration in 2018 after burning through over $130M without finding a sustainable revenue model, and was later sold to new owners in a restructuring.
lesson: Flashy AR marketing technology can attract huge funding rounds while masking a lack of paying-customer demand.
source: TechCrunch, Financial Times coverage of Blippar administration 2018

name: Block.one (EOS)
summary: Raised a record $4 billion in a year-long ICO for the EOS blockchain but failed to deliver the promised dApp ecosystem or enterprise adoption, and later pivoted away from EOS development entirely.
lesson: Raising enormous capital does not substitute for shipping a product that developers and users actually want to build on.
source: SEC settlement with Block.one, 2019; EOS Network Foundation spin-off coverage

name: BlockFi
summary: Crypto lender filed for Chapter 11 after its exposure to FTX/Alameda, which had bailed it out earlier that year, became fatal.
lesson: Accepting a rescue loan from a counterparty that is itself fragile just delays and compounds the eventual failure.

name: Blokable
summary: Modular multifamily housing manufacturer that wound down operations around 2023-2024 after struggling to secure enough project volume to sustain its factory.
lesson: Modular housing manufacturers need long-term pipeline commitments from developers before committing to factory-scale capital investment.

name: BloomTech (Lambda School)
summary: Coding bootcamp built on income-share agreements that faced state regulatory actions and an FTC investigation over misleading job-placement statistics, shrinking dramatically.
lesson: Income-share-agreement business models invite regulatory scrutiny if outcome claims aren't rigorously verified.

name: Blooom
summary: Robo-advisor focused on optimizing employer-sponsored 401(k) accounts shut down its consumer service after failing to build a large enough subscriber base.
lesson: A low-dollar subscription robo-advisor needs massive scale to overcome thin per-user margins.

name: Blu Homes
summary: Prefabricated/modular green home manufacturer that struggled to scale factory production profitably and filed for bankruptcy in 2020.
lesson: Prefab housing economics depend on factory utilization; underused manufacturing capacity destroys unit economics quickly.
source: Builder Magazine, GreenBiz coverage of Blu Homes bankruptcy

name: Blue Apron
summary: Meal-kit subscription pioneer that IPO'd in 2017, struggled with customer acquisition costs and churn for years, and was acquired by Wonder Group in 2023 after its stock collapsed.
lesson: High customer acquisition costs paired with poor retention doom subscription commerce even with first-mover advantage.

name: bluebird bio
summary: Gene therapy pioneer won multiple approvals but pricing them at $2.8-3.1M each proved commercially unsustainable, forcing withdrawal from Europe and an 80%+ stock decline.
lesson: Curative one-time gene therapies still need a reimbursement pathway that health systems will actually pay for.

name: Bluecrew
summary: W-2 flexible staffing marketplace that was sold to Employbridge in 2022 in a deal reported as a distress sale after struggling to turn its worker-classification-compliant model into a profitable business.
lesson: Choosing a more compliance-friendly W-2 employment model can raise costs enough to erase the margin advantage of a staffing marketplace.
source: Staffing industry trade press coverage of the Bluecrew-Employbridge acquisition (2022)

name: Bluefly
summary: Early online discount fashion retailer filed for bankruptcy in 2018 after two decades of struggling to turn a sustainable profit against larger competitors.
lesson: Longevity alone doesn't protect a discount ecommerce retailer without a durable cost or sourcing advantage.

name: BlueNalu
summary: BlueNalu was a well-funded cultivated-seafood pioneer developing cell-cultured mahi-mahi and bluefin tuna, but slashed most of its staff in 2024 as cultivated-meat investment dried up before commercial-scale approval.
lesson: Cultivated seafood faces the same multi-year regulatory runway as cultivated meat, and funding cycles rarely match that timeline.

name: Bluenose Analytics
summary: Customer success analytics startup Bluenose Analytics quietly shut down and sold assets in 2017 after failing to differentiate in an increasingly crowded customer success software market.
lesson: Analytics-only customer success tools without workflow ownership struggle to retain customers who eventually consolidate onto a full platform.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Bluenose Analytics closure, 2017

name: Blueprint Robotics
summary: Baltimore-based panelized home-manufacturing company using robotic assembly lines that ceased operations and closed its factory in 2023.
lesson: Automating construction manufacturing does not help if end-market housing demand and pricing can't cover the factory's fixed costs.
source: Baltimore Business Journal coverage of Blueprint Robotics closure

name: Bluesmart
summary: Made GPS-enabled smart luggage with built-in batteries and locks, then collapsed in 2018 after major airlines banned checked bags with non-removable batteries, wiping out its core product.
lesson: A hardware business built on airline cooperation is one policy change away from being wiped out.

name: Bnext
summary: Spanish neobank targeting underbanked consumers ceased operations in 2023 after failing to raise further funding to sustain its growth.
lesson: Regional European neobanks competing against well-funded pan-European players like Revolut and N26 face a narrowing runway to differentiate.

name: Bó
summary: Digital-only bank launched by RBS (NatWest) in late 2019 to compete with Monzo and Starling; shut down just six months later in mid-2020 after failing to gain traction and to justify its cost amid COVID-19.
lesson: An incumbent bank spinning up a challenger brand still needs genuine differentiation, not just a new app skin.

name: Bodega (Stockwell)
summary: Automated micro-market vending-pantry startup founded by ex-Google engineers, widely criticized for its name and pitch to replace corner stores, rebranded as Stockwell.ai and ultimately shut down its retail vending business by 2023.
lesson: A retail concept that generates public backlash over its premise can struggle to build the retailer and landlord trust needed to scale placements.

name: Bollinger Motors
summary: Electric truck and commercial vehicle maker Bollinger Motors filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2025 after failing to secure additional financing to scale production of its B4 truck.
lesson: Niche commercial EV makers face the same capital intensity as consumer EV makers without comparable volume to spread fixed costs.
source: Automotive News, 2025, Bollinger Motors Chapter 11 filing

name: Bolt Financial
summary: One-click checkout SaaS reached an $11B valuation before layoffs and public controversy over founder claims about funding and metrics led to a collapse in its valuation.
lesson: Founder credibility problems can destroy enterprise sales momentum even when the core product works.

name: Bolt Threads
summary: Biotech materials company behind Mylo mushroom-leather that paused production after brand partners delayed commitments, and later wound down.
lesson: Relying on a handful of brand partners for a novel material's commercial validation is risky if they can quietly walk away.

name: Bombfell
summary: Menswear personal-styling subscription service shut down operations in 2019, unable to compete against better-funded rivals like Stitch Fix and Trunk Club in the crowded try-before-you-buy apparel space.
lesson: Under-capitalized entrants in a subscription-styling niche get squeezed out once larger players with logistics scale enter.
source: GeekWire, Racked coverage of Bombfell wind-down 2019

name: Bond Technologies
summary: Banking-as-a-service platform for embedded finance was sold to FIS in 2023 in a distressed sale after struggling to reach scale amid intensifying BaaS competition.
lesson: A crowded BaaS market with thin margins can force even well-funded infrastructure players into fire sales.

name: Bonobos
summary: Menswear D2C pioneer was acquired by Walmart for $310M in 2017 but never fit Walmart's e-commerce strategy; Walmart sold it to WHP Global and Express in 2023 for just $75M, roughly a 75% loss.
lesson: A D2C brand's culture and customer base can be a poor strategic fit inside a mass-market acquirer, destroying most of the deal's value.
source: CNBC, CNN Business, Fortune coverage of Walmart-Bonobos sale 2023

name: Boo.com
summary: Early online luxury fashion retailer spent $135M in 18 months on a simultaneous multi-country launch and an over-engineered website that loaded slowly for most users; it collapsed into receivership in May 2000, a symbol of dot-com-era excess.
lesson: Launching simultaneously across many markets before validating product-market fit and basic site performance multiplies the cost of failure.
source: Wikipedia, Museum of Failure, Smart Insights case study on Boo.com

name: Boss Key Productions
summary: Cliff Bleszinski's studio bet on the hero-shooter LawBreakers and battle royale Radical Heights, both of which failed to find an audience amid stiff competition.
lesson: Launching into an oversaturated genre without a clear differentiator invites getting crushed by bigger incumbents.

name: Bossa Nova Robotics
summary: Retail shelf-scanning robotics company whose flagship deployment with Walmart ended, after which the company ceased its retail-robotics operations.
lesson: Depending on a single anchor retail customer leaves a hardware startup dangerously exposed to one contract decision.

name: Bounce
summary: Indian dockless scooter-rental startup (formerly Metro Bikes) that shut down its core rental business in 2022 to pivot fully into electric-vehicle manufacturing.
lesson: Two-wheeler rental economics in price-sensitive markets pushed the company to abandon its original business entirely.

name: Bowery Farming
summary: Once the most valuable vertical farming startup in the US at a $2.3B valuation, Bowery Farming shut down and ceased operations in November 2024 after failing to find a buyer.
lesson: High private valuations do not guarantee a sustainable business model when core unit economics never turn positive.
source: The Information / TechCrunch, Bowery Farming shutdown Nov 2024

name: Bowflex (Nautilus Inc.)
summary: Home fitness equipment maker Nautilus Inc. (Bowflex) filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2024 after pandemic-era overproduction led to a sharp post-pandemic demand collapse and inventory glut.
lesson: Ramping manufacturing to meet a temporary pandemic demand spike left the company overexposed when demand normalized.

name: Boxbee
summary: On-demand storage and pickup logistics startup that shut down in 2016 after failing to scale its physical storage-and-delivery network profitably.
lesson: Physical-asset-heavy on-demand services need dense, repeatable routes to make unit economics work.

name: Boxed
summary: Bulk wholesale ecommerce retailer aimed at non-Costco-member shoppers went public via SPAC in 2022 and filed for bankruptcy in 2024, with assets acquired by rival Sam's Club owner.
lesson: Thin-margin bulk retail online struggles to outrun warehouse club incumbents with superior logistics scale.

name: Boxed.com
funding: ~$500M raised, went public via SPAC in 2021
summary: Bulk wholesale e-commerce marketplace for businesses and households that went public via SPAC then filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2024 after years of unprofitable growth and heavy cash burn.
lesson: A SPAC listing does not fix a fundamentally unprofitable bulk-goods logistics model.
source: Reuters/press coverage of Boxed's 2024 Chapter 11 filing

name: Braintrust
summary: Blockchain-token-based freelance talent marketplace whose BTRST token value collapsed and whose growth stalled well short of its ambitious projections after the broader crypto downturn.
lesson: Tying a marketplace's core incentive mechanism to a volatile token adds a second, unrelated failure mode on top of normal marketplace risk.
source: Crypto/tech press coverage of Braintrust's token and growth struggles

name: Branch Insurance
summary: Home and auto insurance startup offering instant bundled quotes ceased writing new policies and wound down operations in 2024 after failing to achieve sustainable underwriting margins.
lesson: Bundling home and auto for speed doesn't fix thin margins if claims costs outrun premium pricing.

name: Brandless
summary: D2C consumer goods retailer selling unbranded household products at flat $3 prices; shut down in 2020 after burning through massive SoftBank-led funding without reaching profitability.
lesson: A minimalist branding gimmick cannot substitute for a durable unit-economics advantage.

name: Breaker
summary: Social podcast discovery and listening app whose team was acquired by Twitter in 2020, after which the standalone app was shut down.
lesson: Building a standalone consumer app in a category dominated by platform-bundled players is extremely difficult to sustain independently.

name: Bridj
summary: On-demand shuttle bus startup piloted in Boston, Kansas City and Sydney that shut down in 2017 after failing to find a sustainable model and losing a major transit-agency partnership.
lesson: Public transit-agency partnerships can be a lifeline or a liability depending on how dependent the startup's revenue is on them.

name: Bright Automotive
summary: Plug-in hybrid delivery van maker Bright Automotive shut down in 2012 after the Department of Energy denied its loan application, cutting off its planned path to production.
lesson: Building a business plan around a specific government loan program creates single-point-of-failure risk.
source: New York Times, February 2012, Bright Automotive shutdown

name: Bright Health
summary: Tech-enabled health insurance startup expanded rapidly into ACA and Medicare Advantage markets nationwide, then exited nearly all insurance lines and sold its remaining businesses by 2024 after massive losses.
lesson: Underwriting risk in health insurance punishes growth-at-all-costs expansion into new geographies and plan types.

name: Bright Machines
summary: Software-defined manufacturing and robotics automation company underwent major layoffs and restructuring in 2023-2024 after failing to hit growth targets.
lesson: Selling flexible robotics-as-a-service to factories requires proving ROI fast enough to justify the capital needed to build the platform.

name: Britishvolt
summary: UK battery gigafactory startup that collapsed into administration after failing to secure enough funding to build its planned plant.
lesson: Announcing a gigafactory before securing full construction financing leaves a company exposed to any funding gap.

name: Brolly
summary: UK app that aggregated and managed a user's personal insurance policies in one place shut down in 2021 after failing to convert its user base into a profitable underwriting or brokerage business.
lesson: An insurance management app needs a genuine monetization engine behind the convenient interface, not just aggregation.

name: Bromium
summary: Endpoint isolation/micro-virtualization security vendor was acquired by HP Inc. in 2019 in a deal widely seen as underwhelming relative to the roughly $130M it had raised from investors.
lesson: Deep, hardware-adjacent security technology can be expensive to build and sell, and may not return venture-scale outcomes even with strong engineering.

name: BuildDirect
summary: Online marketplace for building materials (flooring, décor) that filed for creditor protection (CCAA) in Canada in 2019 after years of losses.
lesson: Commoditized building-materials e-commerce faces thin margins that heavy customer-acquisition spending can quickly turn unprofitable.
source: BetaKit, Vancouver Sun coverage of BuildDirect CCAA filing

name: Builder.ai
summary: AI-powered app-development platform once valued at $1.5B collapsed into insolvency amid revelations that much of its 'AI' output relied on human engineers.
lesson: Overstating AI automation capability to investors and customers eventually collapses under financial scrutiny.

name: Built.io
summary: API and backend integration platform acquired by Software AG in 2018; the standalone product line was discontinued shortly after as it was merged into Software AG's integration suite.
lesson: Integration-layer startups acquired for their technology are frequently merged and rebranded out of existence.
source: Software AG Built.io acquisition and integration coverage

name: Bulletin
summary: B2B wholesale marketplace connecting independent product brands with retail store space that shut down in 2020, citing the impossibility of continuing its retail-space model during the COVID-19 pandemic.
lesson: A marketplace tightly coupled to physical retail footprint is acutely exposed to sudden shocks that shut down in-person commerce.

name: Bunker
summary: US startup providing on-demand liability insurance and compliance tracking for freelancers and independent contractors ceased operations in 2023 after running out of funding.
lesson: Serving the gig economy's insurance needs is a large addressable market in theory but a thin-margin one in practice.

name: Burst Media
summary: Online ad network Burst Media wound down operations around 2014-2015 after failing to transition its display network model to the programmatic era, with remaining assets absorbed by other firms.
lesson: Ad networks built on manual sales models often fail to adapt fast enough to programmatic buying.
source: AdExchanger reporting on Burst Media wind-down

name: Buzzer
summary: Fan app letting users stream short 99-cent clips of live sporting events, backed by Michael Jordan, shut down after a licensing pivot failed to gain traction.
lesson: Micro-transaction clip streaming couldn't out-compete free social highlights already available from leagues and broadcasters.

name: BuzzFeed News
summary: BuzzFeed's Pulitzer Prize-winning news division was shut down in 2023 as parent company BuzzFeed Inc, which had gone public via SPAC, cut costs after its stock collapsed.
lesson: Prestige journalism can win awards while still being the least profitable division a public company sacrifices first.

name: By Miles
summary: UK pay-per-mile car insurance startup ceased writing new business and sold its book after struggling to reach profitability in a thin-margin niche.
lesson: Usage-based insurance pricing innovation doesn't by itself solve the capital intensity of running an insurance carrier.

name: Byju's
summary: Once the world's most valuable edtech company, Byju's collapsed under aggressive debt-fueled acquisitions, accounting irregularities, and auditor/board resignations, leading to insolvency proceedings.
lesson: Aggressive debt-fueled M&A without financial controls can sink even category-leading startups.

name: Byliner
summary: A digital publisher of long-form nonfiction e-singles by notable authors failed to build a sustainable subscription business and effectively ceased operations by 2014.
lesson: Premium long-form digital content struggled to find enough paying subscribers before ad-free bundles like Kindle Singles matured.

name: Byte
summary: Vine co-creator Dom Hofmann's short-video app reboot launched to fanfare in 2020 but couldn't compete with TikTok's scale and was shut down in 2023.
lesson: Nostalgia for a beloved defunct product doesn't overcome a much larger incumbent's head start.

name: Byton
summary: Chinese-backed EV startup Byton filed for bankruptcy in 2023 after years of production delays and cash shortages prevented its M-Byte SUV from ever reaching meaningful sales.
lesson: Years of delayed production while burning investor capital on prototypes signals fundamental execution problems, not just bad luck.

name: bZx / Ooki DAO
summary: DeFi lending protocol suffered multiple exploits from 2020-2021 and its successor Ooki DAO was ordered shut down by the CFTC in 2023 for offering illegal leveraged trading, with the agency winning a first-of-its-kind default judgment against the DAO itself.
lesson: Rebranding a protocol as a DAO does not shield it from regulatory liability for products offered to US persons.
source: CFTC press release on Ooki DAO judgment, 2023

name: C3 Metrics
summary: Marketing attribution startup C3 Metrics became embroiled in a high-profile lawsuit with WPP alleging fabricated data and credentials, effectively destroying its credibility and business.
lesson: Attribution and measurement vendors live or die by data integrity; fabrication claims can be fatal even without a final verdict.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday coverage of C3 Metrics/WPP lawsuit 2018-2019

name: CaaStle (Gwynnie Bee)
summary: Clothing-rental-as-a-service platform (originally size-inclusive rental brand Gwynnie Bee) shut down all US operations in 2025 after founder Christine Hunsicker was accused of a $530M fraud misrepresenting the company's financials to investors.
lesson: Founder fraud can instantly vaporize a company regardless of its underlying product traction or years of investor goodwill.
source: Business of Fashion, Wikipedia coverage of CaaStle/Christine Hunsicker fraud case 2025-2026

name: Cadre
summary: Real estate investing platform for accredited investors laid off much of its staff and scaled back its marketplace ambitions after high interest rates froze commercial real estate deal flow.
lesson: Alternative real estate investing platforms are highly sensitive to macro credit cycles outside their control.

name: Calibrate
summary: Telehealth weight-loss startup that combined coaching with GLP-1 prescriptions shut down in 2024 after larger competitors and direct-to-consumer GLP-1 access eroded its differentiation.
lesson: A crowded, fast-moving category can commoditize a startup's advantage within a couple of years.

name: Call9
summary: A startup placing paramedics with telemedicine gear inside nursing homes to treat frail elderly residents on-site and avoid costly ER transfers; it shut down in June 2019 after failing to raise additional capital to scale.
lesson: Reducing ER transfers for nursing-home residents is genuinely valuable, but the staffing-heavy delivery model is expensive to scale faster than new funding rounds can support.

name: Cambridge Analytica
summary: Political marketing and data firm Cambridge Analytica shut down in 2018 after revelations it harvested Facebook user data without consent for political ad targeting, triggering global regulatory backlash.
lesson: Data-targeting businesses that skirt consent rules face existential regulatory and reputational risk when exposed.
source: The Guardian, New York Times Cambridge Analytica investigation 2018

name: Cano Health
summary: A Medicare Advantage-focused senior primary care chain that went public via SPAC at a multi-billion-dollar valuation, then collapsed under debt from aggressive clinic acquisitions and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2025.
lesson: Debt-fueled roll-ups of senior primary care clinics can outrun the slow, capital-intensive economics of value-based Medicare reimbursement.

name: Canoo
summary: EV startup Canoo, known for its pod-like designs, ceased operations and began liquidation proceedings in early 2025 after years of production delays, executive turnover, and mounting debt.
lesson: Chronic executive turnover and shifting strategy are often leading indicators of an EV startup's inability to reach production at scale.
source: Reuters, January 2025, Canoo operations ceasing coverage

name: Capital New York
summary: A New York City politics and media trade publication was acquired by Politico in 2014 and folded into Politico New York in 2015, ending its standalone brand.
lesson: Regional trade publications with a niche but small audience are natural acquisition and consolidation targets rather than standalone survivors.

name: Car2Go / Share Now
summary: Daimler's free-floating car-sharing service merged with BMW's DriveNow to form Share Now in 2019, exited North America the same year, and the whole venture shut down in early 2024.
