lesson: Free-floating car-sharing fleets require fleet-scale capital that even automakers found unsustainable long term.

name: CareMax
summary: A Medicare-focused senior primary care and value-based care company operating dozens of Florida clinics; it filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy in November 2024 with $693M in debt after an ill-fated bet on Steward Health Care's Medicare business.
lesson: Taking on a struggling partner's Medicare risk contracts to fuel growth can import that partner's insolvency risk directly onto your own balance sheet.

name: CareSync
summary: A Tampa-based chronic care management platform that coordinated care for patients (mostly elderly Medicare beneficiaries) with multiple chronic conditions; it abruptly ceased operations in June 2018 after a rescue deal fell through, laying off nearly 300 employees.
lesson: Coordinating chronic/elderly care is valuable but reimbursement lags badly behind the cost of delivering the service, so burn rates must be matched to realistic payer timelines.

name: CareZone
summary: A consumer app that helped families track an aging relative's medications, notes and contacts; Walmart bought its core technology for roughly $200M in 2020 and quietly sunset the original consumer app by 2023.
lesson: An acquisition can look like a win on paper while the product that mattered to loyal caregiving families still disappears.

name: Cargo Chief
summary: US digital freight brokerage and trucking marketplace startup that wound down around 2019 after failing to differentiate its brokerage technology against larger competitors.
lesson: Freight brokerage technology alone is not a durable moat without proprietary carrier density or shipper relationships.

name: Cargomatic
summary: Trucking capacity marketplace for local and regional freight that shrank sharply from its early ambitions, with reports of major staff reductions and stalled growth in a highly competitive freight-tech market.
lesson: Local trucking marketplaces face brutal price competition that makes take-rate economics hard to sustain at scale.
source: Freight industry trade press coverage of Cargomatic's struggles

name: CarLotz
summary: Consignment-based used-car marketplace CarLotz sold its operating assets to Shift Technologies in 2023 after failing to reach profitability, and was later delisted.
lesson: A novel consignment model doesn't overcome the same operational cost structure problems that sink capital-intensive marketplaces.
source: CarLotz 8-K filings, 2023 asset sale to Shift

name: Casper
summary: Bed-in-a-box mattress D2C pioneer IPO'd in 2020 at a fraction of its earlier private valuation, then was taken private in 2022 by Durational Capital at a steep discount.
lesson: A category creator can still lose once dozens of well-funded copycats commoditize the product.

name: Cassava Sciences
summary: Alzheimer's drug developer faced sustained allegations of manipulated preclinical data behind lead candidate simufilam, triggering FDA and DOJ investigations and a stock collapse.
lesson: Data integrity questions raised by independent scientists can unravel years of investor confidence even before a pivotal trial reads out.

name: CastAR
summary: CastAR, founded by former Valve engineers to build retroreflective AR glasses, shut down in 2017 after running out of funding and failing to ship its long-delayed Kickstarter-backed product.
lesson: Kickstarter crowdfunding validates initial interest but does not guarantee sufficient capital to solve remaining hard engineering problems.

name: Castlight Health
funding: IPO 2014 (NYSE: CSLT); sold to Vera Whole Health 2022
summary: Corporate health navigation and wellness platform Castlight Health went public in 2014 at a high valuation but its stock collapsed over 95%, and it was sold for a fraction of its value to Vera Whole Health in 2022.
lesson: An enterprise health-tech IPO priced on hype can face years of value destruction if sales cycles and retention underperform projections.

name: Catalia Health
summary: Maker of Mabu, an AI companion robot that coached chronically ill and elderly patients through medication adherence and care plans in partnership with Pfizer and Kaiser Permanente; the company shut down after over-ordering robot hardware it couldn't sell through.
lesson: Hardware companion robots carry brutal unit economics — manufacturing commitments made on optimistic sales forecasts can sink a company even with strong pharma partnerships.

name: Catalog
summary: Music NFT marketplace for full-length song releases that ceased active operations in 2023 as the music-NFT trend collapsed alongside the wider crypto downturn.
lesson: First-mover platforms in a hype-driven niche market can be wiped out when the underlying speculative demand evaporates.

name: Cavirin
summary: Cloud security posture management startup was quietly acquired by ColorTokens in 2021 for an undisclosed, reportedly modest sum in a crowded CSPM market dominated by larger platform vendors.
lesson: Entering a hot security category late, without a clear differentiator, often ends in a small tuck-in sale rather than independent growth.

name: Cazena
summary: Managed data-lake-as-a-service startup that shut down in 2021 as cloud providers' native data lake offerings made its managed layer redundant.
lesson: A managed-service wrapper around cloud infrastructure can be commoditized once hyperscalers build the same capability natively.

name: Cazoo
summary: UK online used-car retailer Cazoo shrank from full vehicle retailing to a marketplace-only model in 2023 and was delisted from NYSE in 2024 after massive losses.
lesson: Rapid multi-country expansion of a capital-heavy retail model without proven unit economics accelerates cash burn rather than growth.

name: Celsius Network
summary: Crypto lending platform promising high-yield interest on deposits froze withdrawals and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after risky DeFi bets left it insolvent.
lesson: Yields that outpace any plausible underlying return are usually funded by new depositors, not real profit.

name: Cerebral
summary: Fast-growing telehealth startup for ADHD and mental health prescriptions faced a DOJ investigation into controlled-substance prescribing practices, was cut off by pharmacy partners, and laid off the large majority of its staff in 2022–2023, surviving only in drastically reduced form.
lesson: Growth built on liberal controlled-substance prescribing invites regulatory intervention that can gut a company almost overnight.

name: Championship Gaming Series
summary: DirecTV's ambitious city-franchise esports league folded after just two seasons when high production costs vastly outpaced television viewership and sponsorship revenue.
lesson: Traditional sports broadcast economics don't transfer directly to esports before mainstream audiences and infrastructure are ready.

name: Chari
summary: Morocco/Africa-focused B2B retail distribution marketplace for small shops that carried out significant layoffs and market retrenchment in 2023 amid tightening funding conditions.
lesson: Fast multi-country rollout of low-margin retail distribution outpaces the funding runway once venture capital cools.
source: African/MENA tech press coverage of Chari's 2023 layoffs

name: Chariot
summary: Crowdsourced commuter shuttle-van startup acquired by Ford in 2016 that was shut down in 2019 as Ford Smart Mobility cut underperforming units amid a broader restructuring.
lesson: Shuttle-van services face brutal per-seat economics that scale poorly even under automaker ownership.

name: Charlotte Russe
summary: Fast-fashion women's retailer filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2019 and liquidated all stores after failing to find a buyer, weighed down by debt from its 2009 LBO and declining mall traffic.
lesson: Heavy LBO debt loads leave little room for retailers to fund the digital transformation needed to survive.
source: Reuters coverage of Charlotte Russe Chapter 11 filing, 2019

name: Chartio
summary: Self-service BI and dashboarding tool acquired by Atlassian in late 2021; Atlassian shut it down almost immediately in 2022, giving customers only months to migrate.
lesson: An acquihire can result in near-immediate shutdown of the acquired product, leaving customers with very short migration windows.

name: Chef'd
funding: $35M+ (Campbell Soup was an investor and retail partner)
summary: Chef'd sold à la carte, retailer-branded meal kits including a partnership with Campbell's, but it abruptly ceased operations in 2018 after Campbell's exited its investment and no buyer emerged in time.
lesson: Dependence on a single large strategic partner for both funding and distribution creates existential risk if that partner exits.

name: Chegg
summary: Homework-help platform's stock collapsed over 99% from its 2021 peak after the CEO disclosed that ChatGPT was materially eroding subscriber growth, triggering mass layoffs.
lesson: Generative AI can rapidly commoditize a business built on giving students step-by-step answers.

name: Chitika
summary: Contextual ad network Chitika shut down its publisher ad network in 2020 after nearly two decades, citing an inability to compete with the scale of Google's and Amazon's ad networks.
lesson: Independent contextual ad networks eventually get squeezed out as duopoly platforms capture ad-buyer budgets.
source: Company announcement, AdExchanger coverage of Chitika shutdown 2020

name: CipherCloud
summary: Early cloud access security broker (CASB) pioneer was acquired by Lookout in 2022 for an undisclosed, reportedly low valuation after losing ground to competitors like Netskope and Skyhigh.
lesson: Being first to a security category is not enough; slower iteration than well-funded fast-followers can erase an early leader's advantage.

name: Circ
summary: Berlin-based e-scooter startup (rebranded from Flash) acquired by Bird in 2020 as the European scooter market consolidated rapidly.
lesson: In a saturated market, being acquired by a larger rival is often the only viable exit.

name: Circa
summary: A mobile-first news app that broke stories into structured, updatable facts shut down in 2015 after parent company Sinclair Broadcast Group declined to continue funding it.
lesson: An innovative story format is not enough if the corporate parent funding it doesn't see a path to a sustaining business model.

name: CircleUp
summary: Equity crowdfunding platform for consumer brands pivoted away from its investing marketplace to become a data analytics company after the crowdfunding model underdelivered.
lesson: Crowdfunding platforms can generate deal flow without generating a durable revenue model.

name: Civic
summary: Blockchain-based identity verification platform that raised $33M in a 2017 token sale; repeatedly pivoted its product but never achieved mainstream identity-verification adoption.
lesson: Token-funded startups can raise large sums without validating real product demand, delaying the reckoning rather than avoiding it.

name: Civilised Bank
summary: UK challenger bank aimed at small businesses and consumers withdrew its banking license application and folded in 2020 without ever launching to the public.
lesson: Winning a full banking license is a multi-year, capital-intensive process that many aspiring challenger banks never complete.

name: ClearEdge Power
summary: ClearEdge Power made stationary fuel cells for distributed power generation and filed for bankruptcy in 2014, with assets acquired by Doosan.
lesson: Distributed fuel cell generation needs a clear cost-per-kWh advantage over grid power to justify continued capital infusions.

name: Clearspire
summary: An innovative hybrid law firm and technology company built around splitting legal service delivery from technology infrastructure collapsed amid a bitter lawsuit between its co-founders.
lesson: A structurally novel business model can be undone by ordinary founder disputes over control and equity.

name: Clearview AI
summary: Facial recognition company used by law enforcement has been banned or fined in Canada, the UK, France, Italy, and several US states over its unconsented scraping of billions of photos.
lesson: Building a surveillance database from scraped personal data without consent invites regulatory bans across multiple jurisdictions.

name: Clearwire
summary: Built a nationwide WiMAX-based 4G wireless network, burning over $10 billion, before being acquired by Sprint in 2013 having never reached profitability.
lesson: Betting an entire national network build on a wireless standard that loses the industry standards war is an existential risk.

name: Close5
summary: eBay's local classifieds marketplace app aimed at competing with letgo and OfferUp was shut down in 2017 after failing to gain sufficient market share.
lesson: Launching a me-too marketplace app into an already-crowded category rarely overtakes established leaders.

name: CloudBoost
summary: Mobile backend-as-a-service startup (an early Parse alternative) that shut down its service in 2018 after struggling to build a sustainable customer base.
lesson: Building a replacement for a shuttered platform (Parse) does not guarantee demand if the underlying market has already moved on.

name: Cloudera
summary: Big data platform pioneer that IPO'd in 2017 but never delivered the growth investors expected, ultimately taken private by KKR and CD&R in 2021 at a price reflecting years of stock decline.
lesson: A well-funded IPO cannot offset a slow-growing core product being bypassed by cloud-native data warehouses.

name: Cloudmark
summary: Email and messaging security pioneer was acquired by Proofpoint in 2017 for a price well below its earlier private valuations, reflecting years of slowing growth in a maturing anti-spam market.
lesson: First-generation anti-spam/messaging security technology can become commoditized, eroding pricing power and exit value over time.

name: CloudPassage
summary: Cloud workload security pioneer that raised over $90M was acquired by Fidelis Cybersecurity in 2021 for undisclosed terms, well after larger competitors had captured most of the cloud security posture management market.
lesson: Being an early cloud security category creator provides no lasting moat if larger, better-capitalized rivals out-execute on go-to-market.

name: Clover Health
summary: Medicare Advantage insurtech taken public via a Chamath Palihapitiya SPAC in 2021; its stock collapsed over 90% amid short-seller allegations, an SEC inquiry, and persistent medical-cost losses.
lesson: SPAC hype cycles can inflate a health-insurance startup's valuation well beyond what its loss ratios can support.

name: Clovis Oncology
summary: Developer of PARP inhibitor Rubraca filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy after the approved ovarian cancer drug underperformed commercially against competitors.
lesson: Being second-to-market in oncology against an entrenched competitor drug can doom even an approved therapy.

name: ClusterHQ
summary: Maker of Flocker, a container data volume manager for Docker, shut down abruptly after failing to find a sustainable revenue model in the fast-moving container ecosystem.
lesson: Infrastructure tooling built for a rapidly consolidating ecosystem can be obsoleted before it reaches revenue scale.

name: CNN+
summary: WarnerMedia's standalone CNN streaming news service launched with major star hires, then was shut down after just three weeks amid the Warner Bros. Discovery merger and low subscriber numbers.
lesson: Launching a major product days before a change of corporate ownership invites it being killed regardless of its own merits.

name: CoBank/AgriDigital blockchain grain platform
summary: Blockchain-based grain commodity management platform AgriDigital scaled back significantly around 2021 after failing to achieve broad industry adoption of its blockchain settlement layer.
lesson: Blockchain-based supply chain tools often struggle when the underlying industry lacks the digital-trust problem the technology is meant to solve.

name: Coda Automotive
summary: EV maker Coda Automotive filed for bankruptcy in 2013 after its sedan received poor reviews and it could not compete with better-funded rivals like Tesla and Nissan.
lesson: Entering the capital-intensive EV market with an uncompetitive product against well-funded incumbents is a losing combination.
source: Reuters, May 2013, Coda Automotive bankruptcy filing

name: Coda Automotive / Coda Energy
summary: Coda pivoted from selling an EV sedan to focusing on its lithium-ion battery and energy storage division, but the automotive arm filed for bankruptcy in 2013 after weak sales.
lesson: A late pivot to energy storage couldn't rescue a company after its core EV product failed commercially.

name: Code Spaces
summary: Hosted source code and project management service that was forced to shut down within hours after an attacker gained access to its AWS control panel and deleted most of its infrastructure and backups.
lesson: Relying on a single cloud console without offline, immutable backups turns a security breach into an instant company-ending event.

name: Cofense
summary: Phishing-simulation and reporting vendor (formerly PhishMe) was bought by a private-equity consortium for $400M in 2018, then underwent repeated rounds of layoffs in subsequent years as growth slowed under PE ownership.
lesson: Private-equity ownership can squeeze cost structure hard when growth stalls, leading to repeated layoffs rather than reinvestment.

name: Coin
summary: Made a single card that could store and emulate multiple credit/debit cards, was acquired by Fitbit in 2016 for its payments tech, then Fitbit discontinued it in 2017.
lesson: Payment-replacing hardware faces card-network and bank pushback that can undermine the product even after a successful launch.

name: Coincheck
summary: Japanese exchange suffered one of the largest crypto hacks in history in January 2018, losing $530M in NEM tokens, and was forced into acquisition by Monex Group to survive and repay customers.
lesson: Storing hundreds of millions in customer assets in a hot wallet without multisig protections is an unforced catastrophic risk.
source: Japan FSA administrative action against Coincheck, 2018

name: CoinFLEX
summary: Crypto derivatives exchange halted withdrawals in June 2022 after a large counterparty defaulted on a debt obligation, and later pursued a recovery token and restructuring.
lesson: Extending large uncollateralized credit to individual counterparties on an exchange can freeze the whole platform when it defaults.
source: CoinFLEX statement on withdrawal halt, June 2022

name: Color (Color Labs)
summary: Photo-sharing app that raised a massive seed round on founder pedigree alone, launched to widespread criticism, and shut down within about a year.
lesson: Large funding rounds based on founder reputation cannot substitute for validated product-market fit at launch.

name: Commerce One
summary: Pioneering B2B e-procurement marketplace platform whose valuation and revenue collapsed with the dot-com crash; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2004.
lesson: A hot enterprise-software category can still implode if the customer base cannot fund adoption fast enough to match investor expectations.
source: Wikipedia / contemporary business press coverage of Commerce One bankruptcy (2004)

name: Como
summary: Israeli DIY app-builder platform for small businesses that shut down its service in 2018 despite substantial venture funding.
lesson: Consumer-facing SMB app builders need a low-cost, high-retention model since churn among small-business customers is inherently high.
source: Como shutdown coverage, Israeli tech press 2018

name: CompleteFarmer
summary: Ghanaian agtech marketplace CompleteFarmer scaled back operations significantly around 2023 amid broader African agtech funding contraction and unit-economics pressure.
lesson: Early-stage agri-marketplaces in nascent funding ecosystems are highly exposed to broader venture capital pullbacks.
source: TechCabal, African agtech funding downturn coverage 2023

name: Compound Photonics
funding: $150M+ raised across venture and debt rounds
summary: Compound Photonics, developer of microdisplay technology for AR glasses, struggled for over a decade to commercialize its display chips at scale, resulting in significant investor write-downs.
lesson: Deep-tech display component companies serving the AR industry face extremely long, capital-intensive paths to commercialization that can exhaust investor patience.
source: Industry reporting on Compound Photonics funding history and struggles

name: Compute North
summary: Bitcoin mining data-center operator filed for bankruptcy in September 2022 amid the broader mining downturn triggered by falling BTC prices and rising energy costs.
lesson: Hosting providers carrying heavy fixed infrastructure costs are acutely exposed when miner customers can't pay during downturns.

name: comScore
summary: Digital measurement firm comScore restated years of financial results after an accounting scandal involving its non-cash trade transactions, wiping out most of its market value and triggering leadership turnover and lawsuits.
lesson: Aggressive non-cash revenue recognition practices can unravel a media measurement company's credibility overnight.
source: SEC filings, Wall Street Journal coverage of comScore accounting scandal 2016-2017

name: Concirrus
summary: UK marine and specialty insurtech providing IoT-driven risk analytics for underwriters went into administration after failing to secure further investment.
lesson: Selling analytics into slow-moving specialty insurance markets can take longer than venture timelines allow.

name: Conergy AG
summary: German solar company Conergy filed for insolvency in 2013 after years of losses from the same Chinese-driven module price collapse that felled Q-Cells and Solon.
lesson: An entire national industry cluster can be wiped out together when a structural oversupply shock hits a commoditized product.

name: Contactually
summary: Relationship-focused CRM for real estate agents was acquired by Compass in 2019 and fully shut down in 2020 as Compass built its own internal tools.
lesson: Vertical CRM tools acquired by a customer in their core market are often absorbed into internal build efforts rather than kept alive.
source: Compass internal announcement of Contactually shutdown, 2020

name: Convergys
summary: Large outsourced customer contact-center operator Convergys was acquired by rival Synnex/Concentrix in 2018 after years of margin pressure in the traditional BPO customer service business.
lesson: Traditional labor-intensive customer service outsourcers face structural margin compression as automation and offshore competition both squeeze pricing.

name: Convey
summary: Post-purchase delivery experience and last-mile exception-management software company that wound down in 2023, with some technology assets acquired by FedEx.
lesson: Middleware layers on top of carrier data can be squeezed out once carriers build the same visibility features natively.
source: FreightWaves, Convey wind-down and FedEx asset deal 2023

name: Convoy
summary: Digital freight brokerage marketplace connecting shippers and truckers that shut down abruptly in October 2023 after a downturn in freight rates and a failed search for further funding or acquisition.
lesson: A capital-intensive marketplace built during a freight boom can be wiped out fast once rates and volumes normalize.

name: Coord
summary: Curb-space and parking-data startup spun out of Sidewalk Labs that wound down in 2022 after failing to build a large enough commercial base for city curb-management data.
lesson: City infrastructure data products can have long sales cycles that outlast a startup's runway.

name: Copia Global
summary: Kenyan B2B/B2C last-mile goods marketplace serving rural agents that entered administration in 2024 after prolonged cash burn and failure to reach profitability.
lesson: Last-mile distribution to low-density rural markets requires extremely disciplined logistics costs to ever break even.
source: African tech press coverage of Copia Global's 2024 administration filing

name: Copper
summary: Teen banking and debit card startup shut down its app in 2023 after failing to raise further funding in a shrinking niche crowded with better-capitalized rivals like Greenlight.
lesson: Entering a niche already dominated by a well-funded leader leaves little room for a smaller challenger to raise follow-on capital.

name: Core Scientific
summary: One of the largest US Bitcoin miners filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in December 2022 after equipment-financing debt and falling BTC prices made its balance sheet unsustainable; it emerged from bankruptcy in 2024.
lesson: Debt-financed hardware expansion in a commodity-mining business is extremely exposed to price and hash-rate cycles.
source: Core Scientific Chapter 11 filings, 2022; emergence plan 2024

name: CorePower Yoga
summary: National yoga chain CorePower Yoga filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2020 and permanently closed numerous studios after pandemic shutdowns eliminated in-person revenue.
lesson: Studio-based fitness formats with long real estate leases carry structural risk during prolonged closures.

name: Corinthian Colleges
summary: For-profit college chain with large online programs that collapsed in 2015 amid federal investigations into deceptive enrollment and job-placement claims.
lesson: Aggressive recruiting built on inflated outcome claims eventually triggers regulatory collapse.

name: CoTweet
summary: Social-media customer service tool CoTweet was acquired by ExactTarget in 2010 and discontinued as a standalone product by 2012 as social features were folded into ExactTarget's marketing suite.
lesson: Early social-customer-service tools were often acquired for their user base and then dissolved into a broader marketing platform roadmap.

name: Counter Logic Gaming
funding: Privately funded, later owned by NRG
summary: One of the oldest North American LoL organizations, CLG folded its LCS franchise operations in 2023 as the economics of the franchised league became untenable for smaller-market teams.
lesson: First-mover longevity in esports doesn't protect a brand once league-wide cost structures outrun its revenue base.

name: Cover
summary: Accessory dwelling unit (ADU) prefab manufacturer in Los Angeles that shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Small-scale prefab housing startups often can't achieve manufacturing economies of scale fast enough before capital runs out.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Cover ADU shutdown

name: Coverfox
summary: Indian online insurance marketplace for auto, health and life policies wound down its core consumer brokerage after failing to raise further funding, with remaining assets absorbed by smaller players.
lesson: Early leadership in an insurance marketplace category erodes quickly once better-capitalized rivals enter with subsidized acquisition.

name: Covisint
funding: Founded by GM, Ford, DaimlerChrysler as a joint venture
summary: Automotive-industry B2B procurement exchange founded by the Big Three automakers; failed to become the dominant transaction hub they envisioned and was eventually sold for a small fraction of its original valuation before being absorbed into OpenText.
lesson: Consortium-owned marketplaces struggle when founding members compete with each other and cannot agree on shared governance.

name: Coya
summary: German digital insurer offering home, liability and legal protection policies exited the direct-to-consumer retail insurance business in 2022 after underwriting losses proved unsustainable.
lesson: Full-stack European digital carriers face brutal capital requirements that can outpace even well-funded venture rounds.

name: Craftsy
summary: Online marketplace and classes platform for crafting supplies and instruction was sold off in pieces starting in 2018, with the original platform ceasing operations by 2019.
lesson: Bundling ecommerce and content/education products can dilute focus on either business.

name: Cream Finance
summary: DeFi lending protocol suffered at least five major exploits between 2021-2022 totaling over $130M in losses, and its team effectively discontinued active development and shut down its front end by 2023.
lesson: Repeated smart-contract exploits without decisive remediation erode user trust until a protocol becomes economically unviable.
source: Rekt.news coverage of Cream Finance exploits, 2021-2023

name: Cred Inc.
summary: Crypto lending platform filed for bankruptcy in November 2020 after risky investments and an alleged fraud by a business partner drained its reserves.
lesson: Outsourcing treasury management to unvetted partners can expose a lender to fraud it cannot detect until it's too late.

name: CropOne (Crop One Holdings)
summary: Crop One's joint venture with Emirates Flight Catering, Bustanica-precursor Crop One Dubai facility, wound down its original operations in 2022 amid financial strain in the vertical farming sector.
lesson: Vertical farming joint ventures still must clear the same energy and labor cost hurdles as standalone operators.

name: Cruise
summary: GM's robotaxi subsidiary suspended all driverless operations nationwide in late 2023 after a pedestrian dragging incident in San Francisco, leading to major layoffs, leadership departures, and GM later halting robotaxi development entirely in 2024.
lesson: A single serious safety incident can unravel years of regulatory goodwill and force a strategic retreat from autonomy.

name: Cruise (GM)
summary: GM's robotaxi subsidiary Cruise suspended all driverless operations nationwide in late 2023 after a pedestrian-dragging incident and safety-reporting scandal, and GM ended its robotaxi funding in 2024.
lesson: A single serious safety incident combined with a slow or misleading regulatory response can end years of technology investment almost overnight.
source: GM press release, December 2024, ending Cruise robotaxi development

name: CubicFarm Systems
summary: Publicly traded vertical farming and cattle-feed technology company CubicFarms saw its stock delisted from major exchanges and underwent severe financial distress by 2024.
lesson: Diversifying across multiple unproven agtech product lines can spread limited capital too thin to prove out any single one.

name: Cue Health
summary: Maker of at-home molecular COVID-19 test devices boomed during the pandemic on government contracts but collapsed as testing demand evaporated, laying off nearly all staff and winding down operations in 2024.
lesson: Demand pulled forward by a crisis can vanish just as fast, stranding capacity built for peak conditions.

name: Cupid plc
summary: UK-listed online dating operator whose stock collapsed after revelations that some of its sites used fake profiles and chatbots to entice paying subscribers into conversations.
lesson: Using fake engagement to juice subscription revenue is a fraud risk that can destroy a public company's valuation almost instantly once exposed.

name: Curbside
summary: Mobile curbside pickup and local commerce platform partnering with retailers shut down in 2019 after failing to build a sustainable standalone business.
lesson: Middleman commerce infrastructure is vulnerable once retailers build the same capability in-house.

name: CureVac
summary: Early mRNA vaccine pioneer's COVID-19 candidate showed only ~47% efficacy, losing the vaccine race to Moderna and Pfizer/BioNTech despite two decades of platform development.
lesson: Being first to a technology platform does not guarantee being first with a winning clinical product.

name: Current Health
summary: A remote patient monitoring platform for hospital-at-home care, heavily used for elderly post-discharge patients; Best Buy took a $475M goodwill impairment on its home-health bet and sold Current Health back to its founder in 2025 after major losses.
lesson: Even a well-funded corporate parent will divest a health-tech unit once losses outpace strategic patience, regardless of the underlying clinical promise.

name: Curves International
summary: Women's fitness franchise Curves shrank from a peak of roughly 10,000 locations worldwide to a small fraction of that as low-cost gyms and boutique studios outcompeted its format.
lesson: A once-dominant franchise format can be structurally outcompeted as the market shifts toward newer fitness models.

name: Cyberathlete Professional League (CPL)
summary: One of the earliest professional esports leagues, the CPL ceased operations in 2008 amid mounting financial losses and failed attempts to license its format internationally.
lesson: Being first to professionalize a competitive gaming scene doesn't matter if the sponsorship market isn't mature enough to fund it.

name: Cyberbit
summary: Israeli OT/ICS security and cyber-range training company underwent significant layoffs and strategic retrenchment in the early 2020s as the specialized OT security market proved smaller and slower-growing than projected.
lesson: Specialized security niches (like OT/ICS) can have a much smaller addressable market than the broader enterprise security narrative suggests to investors.

name: Cybereason
summary: EDR vendor once valued near $3B underwent repeated rounds of layoffs from 2022-2024, and by early 2025 its CEO sued major investors SoftBank and Liberty Strategic Capital alleging a board deadlock was pushing the company toward imminent bankruptcy.
lesson: Investor-board conflict over follow-on funding can bring a once-thriving company to the brink of collapse even when its product still has customers.

name: CyberSponse
summary: Security orchestration and automated response (SOAR) vendor was hit by co-founder litigation and governance disputes before being acquired by FireEye in 2019 for a modest sum.
lesson: Internal founder disputes and governance breakdowns can force a distress sale even for a company in a growing product category.

name: Cygilant
summary: Managed detection and response / SOC-as-a-service provider (formerly EIQnetworks) wound down operations around 2020 after years of subscale growth in the managed security services market.
lesson: Managed security services businesses need real scale to cover 24/7 SOC costs; subscale providers can quietly run out of runway.

name: Cylance
summary: AI-driven endpoint protection pioneer was bought by BlackBerry for $1.4B in 2019, but BlackBerry sold the Cylance business to Arctic Wolf in 2024-2025 for just $160M in cash plus stock, a fraction of the original purchase price.
lesson: An acquirer overpaying for a hot AI-security narrative can end up divesting the asset years later for a fraction of the price, destroying value along the way.

name: Cyphort
summary: Advanced threat-detection vendor was acquired by Juniper Networks in 2017 for an undisclosed but reportedly modest sum after raising roughly $65M, well below investor expectations.
lesson: A crowded threat-detection market can force even well-funded, technically credible vendors into an underwhelming acqui-hire exit.

name: CyPhy Works (Aria Insights)
summary: Founded by iRobot co-founder Helen Greiner to build tethered and autonomous drones for security and industrial use, the company rebranded as Aria Insights in 2018 and wound down in 2019.
lesson: Repeated repositioning between defense, industrial, and consumer drone markets can exhaust runway before any segment matures.
source: Boston Business Journal / TechCrunch coverage of Aria Insights closure

name: Cyren
summary: Email and web security/threat-intelligence vendor (formerly Commtouch) was delisted from Nasdaq and commenced insolvency proceedings in Israel in February 2023 after failing to find a buyer or new financing.
lesson: Legacy security vendors that fail to modernize their product and cost structure eventually lose to better-funded, cloud-native competitors.

name: Cyveillance
summary: Early cyber threat-intelligence and brand-protection vendor lost momentum against newer entrants and was acquired by LookingGlass Cyber in 2015 for an undisclosed, reportedly modest sum.
lesson: First-mover threat-intelligence vendors can be leapfrogged by better-productized competitors and end up as tuck-in acquisitions.

name: D2iQ (Mesosphere)
summary: Pioneer of the Mesos-based DC/OS platform that rebranded as D2iQ as Kubernetes overtook its core technology bet, eventually being acquired by Nutanix in a modest deal.
lesson: Betting the company on one orchestration technology left no way back once the market coalesced around a rival standard.

name: Daily Burn
summary: On-demand workout video service Daily Burn, owned by IAC, was shut down in 2022 as the crowded streaming fitness market made it uncompetitive against newer entrants.
lesson: A first-mover in streaming fitness content can still be squeezed out once bigger platforms and hardware bundles enter the space.

name: Damballa
summary: Network-based malware detection pioneer failed to meet revenue expectations and was sold to Core Security for under $10M in 2016, a fraction of the capital it had raised.
lesson: Being an early technology pioneer offers no protection if the company cannot convert that lead into sustainable revenue before better-funded rivals catch up.

name: Daqri
summary: Daqri built industrial AR smart helmets and headsets for manufacturing but shut down in 2019 after failing to reach commercial scale, selling off patents.
lesson: Heavy hardware R&D costs for niche industrial AR devices can outpace a slow-forming enterprise sales cycle.

name: DataHero
summary: Self-service BI and data visualization startup that shut down in 2016 after failing to gain enough traction against bigger BI incumbents.
lesson: Self-serve BI tools need a wedge beyond charts and dashboards to avoid being outgunned by Tableau-class incumbents.

name: Deadspin (original)
summary: After G/O Media ordered the sports-and-culture site to 'stick to sports,' the entire editorial staff resigned in protest in 2019, effectively gutting the brand under its private-equity owner.
lesson: Editorial staff who built a brand's voice can walk away en masse when new ownership tries to override that identity.

name: Decentraland
summary: Decentraland, a blockchain-based virtual world with tokenized land parcels, saw its MANA token and land values collapse after the 2021-2022 metaverse hype cycle, with reports showing very low daily active users relative to its market capitalization.
lesson: Speculative land/token value in a metaverse platform can vastly outpace actual user engagement, exposing the gap once hype-driven capital recedes.
source: DappRadar and Bloomberg reporting on Decentraland low daily active users, 2022

name: deCODE genetics
summary: Icelandic population-genomics pioneer that mapped disease genes filed for bankruptcy after its drug discovery efforts failed to generate products.
lesson: A world-class genomics dataset does not automatically convert into a marketable drug pipeline.

name: Deep Space Industries
summary: Asteroid-mining and in-space propulsion startup was quietly acquired by Bradford Space in 2019 after failing to raise the capital needed for its resource-prospecting roadmap; the asteroid-mining business unit was effectively shuttered.
lesson: Asteroid mining requires capital and timelines far beyond what venture funding cycles will tolerate absent near-term revenue.

name: Deliv
summary: Crowdsourced same-day delivery marketplace serving retailers and malls that shut down in 2020 after losing key retail partnerships and failing to reach profitability.
lesson: A delivery marketplace dependent on a handful of large retail partners is highly exposed if those partners build in-house alternatives.

name: Demand Media (eHow)
summary: A content farm that used algorithms to mass-produce SEO-optimized articles (eHow, Cracked) went public in 2011, then saw its stock collapse after Google's Panda algorithm update devalued its content, leading to a split into Leaf Group.
lesson: A business model built entirely around gaming a third-party search algorithm is fundamentally at that platform's mercy.

name: Dendreon
summary: Developer of Provenge, the first FDA-approved cancer immunotherapy vaccine, collapsed into Chapter 11 bankruptcy because manufacturing costs and a $93,000 price tag made commercial uptake unsustainable.
lesson: FDA approval is not commercial viability if the cost structure cannot support market adoption.

name: Denial Esports
summary: The North American multi-title esports org folded in 2018 after years of roster instability and an inability to secure a franchise slot in major leagues.
lesson: Mid-market esports orgs without a franchised league seat are highly exposed to sponsorship market downturns.

name: Denizen
summary: Neobank built for globally mobile expats and immigrants shut down in 2022 after failing to find a sustainable path to scale in a narrow niche.
lesson: A well-defined niche audience can still be too small to support the fixed costs of running a licensed banking product.

name: Deserve
summary: Credit-card-as-a-service platform's planned SPAC merger with Ajax Capital, valuing it near $1.2 billion, collapsed in 2022 amid a broader fintech valuation slump.
lesson: Fintech infrastructure providers dependent on a single exit path are exposed when that market window closes.

name: Desk.com (Assistly)
summary: Assistly launched a lightweight helpdesk product, was acquired by Salesforce in 2011 and rebranded Desk.com, then Salesforce shut the product down in 2018 to push customers to Service Cloud.
lesson: Being acquired is not an exit for a product roadmap — an acquirer can sunset a beloved tool once it conflicts with its flagship platform strategy.

name: Desktop Metal
summary: Industrial metal 3D-printing manufacturer went public via SPAC, made a string of acquisitions, then saw its stock collapse and a planned merger with Stratasys collapse amid mounting losses.
lesson: Rolling up multiple acquisitions to build a manufacturing platform can multiply integration and cash-burn risk instead of synergies.

name: Diamond Sports Group
summary: Owner of the Bally Sports regional sports networks carrying dozens of MLB, NBA, and NHL teams, filed Chapter 11 bankruptcy after cord-cutting gutted subscriber revenue.
lesson: Leveraging up to buy legacy cable sports-rights assets was catastrophic timing just as cord-cutting accelerated.

name: Diem (Facebook Libra)
summary: Facebook-led global stablecoin project, rebranded from Libra to Diem, was shut down in 2022 after sustained opposition from US and international regulators, with its assets sold to Silvergate Bank.
lesson: A stablecoin backed by a Big Tech platform with billions of users triggers systemic-risk scrutiny that can override even massive resources.
source: Diem Association wind-down announcement; Silvergate asset purchase, 2022

name: Digg
summary: The pioneering social news aggregator was overtaken by Reddit and Facebook after a disastrous 2010 redesign, and was sold for parts for about $500,000 in 2012.
lesson: A poorly executed redesign can destroy years of community trust almost overnight.

name: Digital Guardian
summary: Data loss prevention (DLP) vendor that raised over $200M and shelved earlier IPO ambitions was ultimately sold to HelpSystems (Fortra) in 2021 for terms reportedly well below its cumulative venture investment.
lesson: Data loss prevention is a capital-intensive, long-sales-cycle category where even well-funded vendors can struggle to generate venture-scale returns.

name: Digital Media Solutions
summary: Performance marketing company Digital Media Solutions went public via SPAC in 2020 but its stock collapsed over 90% amid heavy debt load and slowing ad revenue, leading to major restructuring.
lesson: SPAC-driven public listings in performance marketing exposed heavily-leveraged businesses to severe market corrections.
source: SEC filings, Digiday coverage of Digital Media Solutions stock collapse

name: Directly
summary: Directly built a crowdsourced customer-support expert network for enterprise brands, but shut down operations in 2020 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Marketplace models for customer support require enormous supply-side liquidity, and gig-expert quality is hard to guarantee at enterprise scale.
source: Crunchbase News coverage of Directly's 2020 shutdown

name: Discord Store (game storefront)
summary: Discord shut down its game storefront and Nitro Games subscription library in 2019 after just over a year, citing low usage relative to its investment.
lesson: A large user base for one product doesn't automatically transfer demand to an adjacent product bundled alongside it.

name: Dispatch
summary: Early sidewalk delivery-robot startup (Carry) wound down operations in 2018 as larger-funded competitors like Starship and Kiwibot scaled faster in the same niche.
lesson: Being an early mover in a capital-intensive robotics category doesn't help if better-funded rivals scale faster.
source: TechCrunch coverage of last-mile delivery robot startups, 2018

name: Disperse
summary: UK-based computer-vision construction progress-monitoring startup that ceased operations around 2023 after struggling to raise further funding.
lesson: Construction progress-monitoring software faces a crowded competitive field, making it hard for smaller players to reach scale before funding dries up.
source: UK tech press coverage of Disperse closure

name: Divvy Homes
summary: Rent-to-own real estate startup was acquired by Brookfield Properties in 2024 at roughly half its peak $2.3B valuation, after rising rates hurt its model.
lesson: Balance-sheet-heavy rent-to-own models are highly exposed to interest rate cycles.

name: DNAinfo
summary: Billionaire Joe Ricketts abruptly shut down hyperlocal news sites DNAinfo and Gothamist just a week after their newsrooms voted to unionize, laying off around 115 staffers.
lesson: A media outlet fully dependent on one wealthy owner's goodwill can be shut down for reasons unrelated to its underlying business performance.

name: Docker Inc
summary: Container technology pioneer whose developer tool became an industry standard, but whose enterprise sales business failed to generate proportional revenue; sold its enterprise unit to Mirantis.
lesson: Ubiquitous open-source adoption doesn't guarantee a company can capture commensurate enterprise revenue.

name: Domain Money
summary: Fee-only financial planning startup offering fixed-price advisor sessions shut down after concluding the human-advisor-plus-software model could not scale profitably.
lesson: Blending human financial advice with software economics is hard to scale profitably at consumer price points.

name: Domo
summary: Business intelligence and dashboarding platform that IPO'd in 2018 but has seen its stock price fall over 90% since, with persistent unprofitability despite the massive pre-IPO capital raised.
lesson: A successful IPO does not offset years of extreme cash burn if the underlying unit economics never turn sustainably profitable.
source: Domo SEC filings and public market performance

name: DoNotPay
summary: The self-styled 'robot lawyer' chatbot was fined $193,000 by the FTC in 2025 for deceptively marketing its AI as equivalent to a human lawyer.
lesson: Overstating an AI product's legal competence invites both regulatory penalties and professional-practice liability.

name: Doorman
summary: Last-mile delivery scheduling startup letting consumers pick evening delivery windows that shut down in 2019 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Consumer-facing delivery scheduling layers struggle without owning enough of the underlying delivery economics.

name: Dot & Bo
summary: Curated furniture and home goods ecommerce startup shut down in 2017 after failing to overcome the high shipping costs and returns typical of furniture ecommerce.
lesson: Furniture ecommerce margins are easily wiped out by freight and return logistics.

name: Douugh
summary: Australian-founded neobank targeting US customers with automated savings tools wound down operations in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: Launching a neobank in a foreign, already-crowded market multiplies customer acquisition costs beyond what small raises can sustain.

name: Draganfly
summary: One of the oldest commercial drone makers, Draganfly has traded as a public penny stock for years with persistent losses and going-concern-level cash constraints despite diversifying into agriculture and public-safety drones.
lesson: Longevity in a hardware niche does not guarantee profitability if the category commoditizes faster than the company diversifies.
source: Draganfly SEC filings and small-cap market coverage

name: Drive.ai
summary: Self-driving software startup Drive.ai ran out of funding and was acquired by Apple in an acqui-hire in 2019 after failing to close a larger financing round.
lesson: Autonomous vehicle R&D burns cash for years before commercialization, making later-stage funding gaps fatal.
source: Axios, June 2019, Drive.ai acquisition by Apple

name: DroneUp
summary: Walmart's primary drone-delivery partner underwent major layoffs and scaled back store-based delivery hubs in 2024 as Walmart pulled back on its drone delivery ambitions amid weak unit economics.
lesson: Depending on a single retail anchor partner for drone-delivery volume leaves a startup vulnerable to that partner's changing priorities.
source: Trade press coverage of DroneUp layoffs and Walmart drone program scale-back, 2024

name: Dubsmash
summary: Lip-sync and short video creation app acquired by Reddit in 2020 that was ultimately shut down in 2023 as Reddit deprioritized standalone video products.
lesson: An acquisition can still end in shutdown if the acquirer's strategic priorities move away from the acquired product.

name: e-Steel
summary: Online steel trading marketplace that raised over $100M during the dot-com boom but folded within two years as transaction volume never materialized at scale.
lesson: Heavy industry buyers with entrenched relationship-based purchasing are slow to move volume onto a new digital channel.
source: Contemporary trade press on e-Steel's 2001 shutdown

name: E8 Security
summary: Behavioral analytics/UEBA startup was acquired by VMware's Carbon Black in 2018 and its standalone product was subsequently discontinued and folded into the broader Carbon Black platform.
lesson: Niche analytics point products are often acquired mainly for talent and IP, with the original product line quietly retired.

name: eBags
summary: Online luggage and bags retailer was acquired by Samsonite in 2017 and shut down in 2020 amid pandemic-driven travel collapse and parent-company struggles.
lesson: A niche ecommerce category tied closely to travel is acutely exposed to macro demand shocks.

name: Echo Fox
funding: Privately funded by Rick Fox and investors
summary: The multi-title esports org co-founded by Rick Fox forfeited its League of Legends franchise slot and dissolved after a bitter ownership dispute involving co-owner Amit Raizada.
lesson: Multi-owner esports orgs need clean cap-table governance before scaling, or internal disputes can void the franchise itself.

name: Eclipse Senior Living
summary: A senior living operator created by REIT Ventas to manage over 100 communities; the pandemic devastated occupancy and margins so severely that Ventas transitioned all communities to other operators and shut down Eclipse's corporate operations by the end of 2021.
lesson: A new operating company built around distressed senior living assets is extremely exposed to macro shocks like a pandemic that hits occupancy across an entire portfolio at once.

name: Editas Medicine
summary: One of the original CRISPR gene-editing companies discontinued its lead in vivo program after disappointing trial results, forcing a strategic pivot and repeated leadership changes.
lesson: Being a CRISPR pioneer with strong IP does not guarantee the first clinical program succeeds.

name: eGain
summary: Customer engagement software vendor eGain has remained a public micro-cap for over two decades with a persistently depressed stock price and stagnant growth relative to cloud-native CX rivals.
lesson: Surviving as a public company for decades is not the same as thriving — stagnant growth against faster-moving SaaS rivals can trap a vendor in permanent decline.
source: eGain SEC filings and long-term stock performance history

name: EHang
summary: Chinese autonomous aerial vehicle and drone maker's stock collapsed over 60% in a single day in 2021 after short-seller Wolfpack Research alleged its flagship contracts and revenue were largely fabricated.
lesson: Rapid pre-revenue valuation growth in an aerial-robotics story stock warrants heavy scrutiny of underlying contract and revenue claims.
source: Wolfpack Research report on EHang and subsequent Reuters coverage, 2021

name: eHealth Inc
summary: Publicly traded online health insurance marketplace saw its stock crash more than 90% from its 2020 highs as Medicare Advantage policy churn and rising acquisition costs battered profitability.
lesson: Even an established online insurance marketplace can be destabilized by shifts in carrier commission economics.

name: Electric Last Mile Solutions
summary: Commercial EV maker Electric Last Mile Solutions filed for bankruptcy in 2022, less than a year after its SPAC merger, following an accounting investigation into its founders' prior venture.
lesson: Founder history and internal accounting controls matter enormously; unresolved governance red flags can sink a company within months of going public.
source: Reuters, June 2022, Electric Last Mile Solutions bankruptcy

name: Element Insurance
summary: German digital insurance carrier and B2B2C insurance-as-a-service provider filed for insolvency after major partners pulled back and losses mounted.
lesson: Full-stack insurance carriers need enormous reserves of capital to survive underwriting losses while scaling.

name: Element Robotics / Agrist
summary: Early-stage harvesting robotics venture Element Robotics wound down around 2023 after its autonomous picking hardware failed to reach commercial reliability targets.
lesson: Harvesting robots that cannot consistently match human-level speed and accuracy in variable field conditions will struggle to attract paying grower customers.

name: Elementum
summary: Supply-chain visibility and risk-management software platform backed by Flex that quietly wound down operations around 2020 after failing to differentiate against larger visibility platforms.
lesson: Being spun out of a large strategic backer does not guarantee independent commercial traction in a crowded category.
source: Industry reporting on supply-chain visibility consolidation, 2020

name: Elio Motors
summary: Elio Motors, developer of a low-cost three-wheeled autocycle, never reached production after over a decade of delays and effectively wound down operations by 2021.
lesson: Repeatedly delaying vehicle production while continuing to raise money from retail investors erodes credibility and eventually funding access.
source: SEC filings and Jalopnik coverage of Elio Motors' prolonged non-production, 2020-2021

name: Elysium Space
summary: Space-burial startup that flew symbolic portions of cremated remains to orbit or the Moon ceased new bookings around 2022 after its main satellite ride-share partnerships wound down and it failed to scale beyond a niche customer base.
lesson: A novelty niche product riding on ride-share launch capacity has limited total addressable market and thin unit economics.

name: eMagin Corporation
funding: Public company (NYSE American: EMAN) prior to acquisition
summary: eMagin, a maker of OLED microdisplays used in military and AR/VR headsets, was acquired by Samsung Display in 2024 in a going-private deal after years of financial losses and delisting risk.
lesson: Component suppliers to the AR/VR hardware industry can face chronic unprofitability even when their technology is used across multiple downstream headset makers.

name: Embark Trucks
summary: Autonomous trucking company Embark shut down operations in 2023 and sold its intellectual property to Applied Intuition after burning through its SPAC-era funding without commercial deployment.
lesson: Deep-tech autonomy ventures need multi-year runway; going public via SPAC does not substitute for a viable path to revenue.

name: Emerge
summary: Digital freight marketplace startup that abruptly ceased operations in 2023 amid allegations of financial misrepresentation to investors and lenders.
lesson: Freight marketplaces built on opaque financial reporting collapse quickly once lenders and investors lose trust.
source: FreightWaves, Emerge shutdown and fraud allegations 2023

name: Empty Set Dollar
summary: Algorithmic stablecoin protocol lost its peg and was effectively abandoned in 2021, one of several seigniorage-model stablecoins that failed to sustain value during volatility.
lesson: Reflexive tokenomics that rely on continuous new demand cannot survive a sustained loss of confidence.
source: DeFi post-mortem coverage of algorithmic stablecoin collapses, 2021

name: Endlesss
summary: Social live music-jamming and collaboration app that shut down in 2023 after failing to raise further funding to sustain operations.
lesson: Novel collaborative music formats need a large committed user base fast enough to justify continued investor funding.

name: Endomondo
summary: Fitness tracking app Endomondo was acquired by Under Armour in 2015 and shut down in 2021 as the company retreated from connected fitness.
lesson: A crowded fitness-tracking app market makes it difficult to justify running multiple overlapping acquired products.

name: Ener1
summary: Ener1 made lithium-ion batteries for EVs through subsidiary EnerDel and filed for Chapter 11 in 2012 after its main customer, Think Global, went bankrupt and demand fell short of projections.
lesson: Concentrating revenue on a single early-stage EV customer creates existential risk if that customer fails.

name: Enron Corporation
summary: Energy trading giant Enron, once a pioneer of deregulated energy markets, collapsed into bankruptcy in December 2001 after systemic accounting fraud using off-balance-sheet special purpose entities was exposed.
lesson: Innovative energy trading models built on hidden debt and fraudulent accounting eventually collapse regardless of market position.
source: SEC enforcement records, Wall Street Journal

name: Envia Systems
summary: Envia Systems claimed breakthrough high-energy-density lithium-ion battery chemistry and signed a GM development deal, but the technology failed to meet cycle-life and safety targets, and the company was effectively wound down after litigation with GM.
lesson: Overstating lab-scale battery performance to a major automotive partner can trigger both technical failure and legal collapse.

name: Escape Dynamics
summary: Startup developing a beamed-microwave-propulsion reusable launch vehicle shut down in 2016 after concluding the ground-based microwave beaming infrastructure required was not commercially viable at the scale investors would fund.
lesson: A propulsion concept that requires building an entirely new ground infrastructure network faces two capital-intensive technology risks instead of one.

name: eSolar
summary: eSolar developed modular solar thermal tower technology but wound down operations around 2016 as utility-scale photovoltaic panels became far cheaper than solar thermal.
lesson: A technically elegant solar thermal architecture lost out once conventional photovoltaic costs fell faster than anyone predicted.

name: Esperanto Technologies
summary: Built a 1,000-core RISC-V AI accelerator chip but landed too few customers after years of development and wound down its silicon business.
lesson: A technically impressive chip with no anchor customer cannot outrun the cash burn of custom silicon development.

name: Essential Products
summary: Android co-founder Andy Rubin's company shipped the Essential Phone but sold poorly against Apple/Samsung; shut down in 2020.
lesson: Star founders and huge funding rounds can't overcome a saturated market and a single failed hero product.

name: eStara
summary: Click-to-call and web customer contact vendor eStara was acquired by Oracle in 2011, and the standalone product was discontinued within a year as it was merged into Oracle's marketing cloud stack.
lesson: Niche communication-bridge technologies acquired by large enterprise vendors are frequently absorbed into a broader suite and lose their brand identity.

name: Esurance
summary: Early direct-to-consumer online auto insurer was acquired by Allstate for roughly $1 billion in 2011 but was fully discontinued as a brand in 2023 after Allstate decided to consolidate its distribution strategy.
lesson: A strong acquisition price doesn't guarantee a brand's long-term survival once it no longer fits the parent's strategy.

name: eToys.com
summary: Online toy retailer that briefly outvalued Toys R Us during the dot-com boom filed for bankruptcy in 2001 after overspending on marketing and logistics infrastructure.
lesson: A high stock valuation is not a substitute for a profitable operating model.

name: Euclid Analytics
summary: In-store retail analytics company using Wi-Fi signals to track shopper foot traffic was acquired by WeWork in 2016 and subsequently wound down as WeWork abandoned the retail-analytics product line.
lesson: An analytics startup acquired by a company outside its core market can be shut down when the acquirer's strategic priorities shift.

name: Even (Even Responsible Finance)
summary: Earned-wage-access and budgeting app, later acquired by Walmart to power its employee financial wellness app, was discontinued as a standalone brand after Walmart in-housed the technology.
lesson: Being acquired by your largest enterprise customer can still mean losing your independent brand identity.

name: Evergreen Solar
summary: Massachusetts solar wafer manufacturer that filed for bankruptcy after Chinese competitors undercut its production costs despite state subsidies.
lesson: Domestic manufacturing subsidies could not offset a structural cost disadvantage versus lower-cost overseas producers.

name: Evernote
summary: Pioneering notes app once reached a $1B+ valuation but suffered years of stagnation and declining relevance against Notion, culminating in its 2023 sale to Bending Spoons.
lesson: Early category leadership can erode without continued product innovation and focus.

name: Everoad
summary: French digital road-freight platform that struggled to reach profitability and was absorbed/restructured in 2019 after failing to compete against larger, better-capitalized freight-tech rivals.
lesson: A crowded freight-tech field with well-funded incumbents leaves little room for undercapitalized regional platforms.

name: Everpix
summary: Highly praised photo storage and organization service that shut down in 2013 after running out of money and failing to raise a follow-on round or find an acquirer.
lesson: Even a product users love can die if storage costs and funding runway do not line up with revenue growth.

name: Everyday Robots (Alphabet)
funding: internally funded by Alphabet's X moonshot factory
summary: Alphabet's general-purpose household and office robot project, incubated for years inside X, was shut down in July 2023 after failing to demonstrate a clear near-term commercial path.
lesson: Even a well-funded corporate moonshot will be cut if it cannot show a believable path to commercialization within a reasonable horizon.
source: IEEE Spectrum / Wired coverage of Everyday Robots shutdown, 2023

name: Evil Geniuses (LCS exit)
summary: Facing unsustainable annual franchise fees and player costs relative to League of Legends revenue, Evil Geniuses sold its LCS slot back to Riot Games in 2023 and exited League esports entirely.
lesson: Paying a large upfront franchise fee only pays off if league-wide viewership and sponsorship growth keeps pace with rising team costs.

name: Excalibur Almaz
summary: Private space tourism venture that acquired Soviet-era Almaz space station modules and reentry capsules planned crewed lunar and orbital tourism flights but dissolved in 2015 having never flown a paying customer.
lesson: Refurbishing legacy hardware for a new commercial market still requires the full capital and certification cost of a new vehicle program.
source: Isle of Man company filings / SpaceNews

name: Exos Aerospace
summary: Reusable suborbital rocket company ceased operations in 2023 after its SARGE vehicle suffered a launch failure and it could not secure further financing.
lesson: A single high-profile flight failure can be fatal when a company has no cash cushion to fund a return-to-flight investigation.

name: F45 Training
summary: Franchise fitness chain F45 Training went public in 2021 backed by celebrity investors, but its stock collapsed over 95% amid franchisee struggles before being taken private in 2023 at a fraction of its IPO value.
lesson: Rapid franchise unit growth without validating franchisee-level profitability can unravel quickly once growth slows.

name: Fab.com
summary: Design-focused flash-sale commerce site (fashion, home goods) burned through over $300M chasing hypergrowth, pivoted its business model multiple times, and was sold for parts (to PCH International) in 2015 after a failed strategy reset.
lesson: Chasing growth-at-all-costs on a flash-sale model without proven repeat-purchase economics leads to a valuation collapse when funding tightens.
source: TechCrunch, Fast Company coverage of Fab.com collapse 2013-2015

name: Fabric (CommonSense Robotics)
summary: Micro-fulfillment robotics company that raised huge rounds at a unicorn valuation but underwent mass layoffs and effective collapse in 2023 amid a shrinking market for automated micro-fulfillment.
lesson: Hype-driven mega-rounds can push valuations far ahead of real commercial traction in capital-intensive automation.
source: Business Insider, Fabric layoffs and collapse 2023

name: Fair.com
summary: Flexible car-subscription and leasing startup Fair laid off most of its staff and sold assets in 2021 after its capital-intensive vehicle-holding model proved unsustainable.
lesson: Owning and depreciating a large vehicle fleet to offer flexible subscriptions requires balance-sheet strength that most startups lack.
source: TechCrunch, May 2021, Fair layoffs and restructuring

name: Fancy
summary: Social commerce and product-discovery retail platform that let users curate shoppable collections shut down in 2018 after years of declining engagement and failure to build a sustainable retail-transaction business.
lesson: Social product-discovery platforms need a durable transaction and retention model, not just aesthetic appeal, to justify large funding rounds.

name: Faraday Future
summary: China-backed luxury EV startup that promised to rival Tesla but has produced only a tiny number of its FF 91 vehicles amid years of near-bankruptcy, executive turnover, and fraud allegations from the SEC.
lesson: Grandiose promises without manufacturing discipline erode investor and consumer trust long before a product ships at scale.

name: Farfetch
funding: IPO 2018 (NYSE: FTCH), ~$2.8B in debt obligations by 2023
summary: Global luxury fashion marketplace came within days of bankruptcy in December 2023 after its stock collapsed over 95%; Coupang acquired its assets in a $500M rescue deal completed January 2024, wiping out public shareholders.
lesson: Aggressive debt-funded acquisitions (Farfetch bought New Guards Group, Browns, etc.) can turn a market leader into a forced fire sale when growth slows.
source: Business of Fashion, Puck, Forbes coverage of Farfetch-Coupang rescue deal 2023-2024

name: Farmcrowdy
summary: Nigerian agtech marketplace Farmcrowdy, once Africa's first Y Combinator-backed agtech startup, laid off most staff and scaled back sharply in 2020 after its crowdfunding-for-farms model failed to scale.
lesson: Agricultural crowdfunding models depend on managing thousands of smallholder farmer relationships, which is far more operationally complex than the fintech layer alone.

name: Farmers Edge
summary: Canadian precision-agriculture data company Farmers Edge, which went public in 2021, saw its stock collapse and filed for creditor protection under CCAA in 2024.
lesson: Going public does not resolve a data-services business model that hasn't proven it can convert acreage under contract into profit.
source: BNN Bloomberg, Farmers Edge CCAA filing 2024

name: Farmigo
summary: Online farmers-market and CSA delivery marketplace Farmigo shut down its consumer marketplace business in 2017 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Local food delivery marketplaces face brutal last-mile logistics costs that are hard to offset with typical grocery margins.

name: Farmshare Inc.
summary: Community-supported agriculture management platform Farmshare shut down around 2019 after failing to build a sustainable subscription revenue base among small farms.
lesson: Software serving small, low-budget farm operations often cannot generate enough per-customer revenue to sustain a dedicated venture-backed company.

name: Farmstead
summary: AI-powered online grocery delivery startup Farmstead shut down in 2021 after failing to raise a follow-on funding round.
lesson: Grocery delivery startups without differentiated logistics advantages struggle to raise follow-on capital in a crowded space.

name: FarmWise Labs
summary: Autonomous weeding-robot startup FarmWise laid off a significant portion of its staff in 2023 as it struggled to scale robot deployment profitably across specialty-crop farms.
lesson: Field robotics for niche specialty crops face a smaller addressable market that limits the scale needed to spread hardware R&D costs.

name: Fast
summary: One-click checkout startup for ecommerce sites shut down abruptly in 2022 after burning through funding faster than it could sign up merchants, despite a high-profile launch.
lesson: Infrastructure startups need real merchant adoption metrics, not just hype, before scaling spend.

name: Fast (Fast.co)
summary: One-click checkout startup that shut down abruptly in April 2022 despite recent large fundraising, after reports surfaced of minimal revenue, poor merchant adoption, and unsustainable spending.
lesson: Impressive fundraising totals can mask an absence of real merchant demand for a product.

name: Fast Radius
summary: On-demand digital manufacturing company went public via SPAC then filed for bankruptcy after failing to control costs.
lesson: SPAC-funded manufacturing companies with heavy fixed costs need real revenue traction to survive as public companies.

name: Fauna
summary: Serverless, globally-distributed database built by ex-Twitter engineers that shut down its commercial cloud service in 2025 after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: A technically ambitious database can lose to simpler, cheaper alternatives even with strong engineering pedigree.

name: FaZe Clan
summary: The influencer-driven esports and content org went public via SPAC at a billion-dollar valuation, then saw its stock collapse over 90% amid heavy losses before going private.
lesson: A large creator and fan following doesn't automatically translate into a business with sustainable margins.

name: Federated Media Publishing
summary: An early blog-advertising network that represented influential bloggers for brand deals wound down operations around 2015 as native advertising shifted to in-house platform tools.
lesson: Ad-network intermediaries between advertisers and independent creators get squeezed out once platforms build direct monetization tools.

name: FedNat Holding Company
summary: Publicly traded Florida homeowners insurer ceased writing new policies and had its primary subsidiary placed into liquidation in 2023 after years of hurricane losses and litigation costs eroded its capital base.
lesson: Rising reinsurance costs and litigation exposure can force even long-established regional carriers into insolvency.

name: Fei Protocol / Tribe DAO
summary: Algorithmic stablecoin protocol suffered an $80M hack in 2022 and, after a governance vote, the Tribe DAO chose to shut down and return remaining treasury funds to holders.
lesson: A major smart-contract hack combined with governance paralysis can be a faster death sentence than slow financial decline.
source: Fei/Tribe DAO governance shutdown vote, September 2022

name: Fernish
summary: Furniture rental startup for apartment dwellers shut down in 2022 after failing to raise a new funding round amid tightening venture capital.
lesson: Asset-heavy rental commerce models need continuous capital access that dries up quickly in downturns.

name: Fetchr
summary: Middle East logistics and delivery marketplace that ceased operations around 2020 after running out of cash amid intensifying regional competition.
lesson: Regional logistics marketplaces need durable cost advantages, not just funding, to survive competition from larger regional players.
source: MENA tech press coverage of Fetchr's 2020 shutdown

name: Fidor Bank
summary: Pioneering German digital bank was acquired by France's BPCE Group in 2016, and BPCE announced a full wind-down of Fidor Bank's German banking operations in 2023.
lesson: Being an early digital-banking pioneer does not guarantee long-term survival once an acquirer decides the business no longer fits its strategy.

name: Fifth Season
summary: Pittsburgh-based robotic vertical farming startup Fifth Season abruptly shut down operations in late 2022, laying off its entire staff.
lesson: Automation-heavy vertical farms still face the same energy-cost economics that sink less automated peers.
source: AgFunder News, Fifth Season shutdown coverage 2022

name: FinanceFox
summary: Swiss digital insurance folder app that let users manage all their policies in one place shut down its consumer service in 2021 after pivoting unsuccessfully to an enterprise licensing model.
lesson: A policy-management app without a brokerage or underwriting revenue engine has a structurally weak business model.

name: Finless Foods
summary: Finless Foods was developing cultivated bluefin tuna, but it laid off the vast majority of its staff in 2023 as investment in cultivated seafood collapsed well before regulatory approval was reached.
lesson: Companies pursuing multi-year regulatory pathways need capital reserves that outlast investor sentiment cycles.

name: FireEye Products (Trellix)
summary: FireEye's core network/endpoint security products business, once a market leader after its 2013 IPO, was sold off in 2021 for $1.2B to Symphony Technology Group and merged with McAfee Enterprise into Trellix, while the higher-margin Mandiant incident-response unit was separated and later sold to Google.
lesson: A pioneering security product can still lose its independent standing once growth slows and margins compress against nimbler cloud-native rivals.

name: Firefly Space Systems (original entity)
summary: Small-launch rocket startup lost a key investor after a lawsuit from Virgin Galactic over an alleged stolen engine design and filed for bankruptcy in 2017; assets were bought out of bankruptcy and relaunched as Firefly Aerospace.
lesson: IP disputes with a well-funded competitor can sever a startup's funding lifeline overnight regardless of the underlying merits.

name: First Republic Bank
summary: Private bank serving high-net-worth clients that was seized by regulators and sold to JPMorgan in May 2023 after a wave of uninsured deposit withdrawals exposed large unrealized losses on low-rate mortgages.
lesson: Long-duration, low-yield assets funded by short-term deposits become a solvency risk once interest rates rise sharply.

name: Fisker Automotive
summary: Luxury plug-in hybrid maker, backed by a $529M DOE loan, filed for bankruptcy after battery supplier A123's collapse and recalls.
lesson: Startups building complex hardware are exposed to their suppliers' failures, not just their own execution.

name: Fisker Automotive (1st company)
summary: Henrik Fisker's first EV company built the Karma plug-in hybrid but collapsed into bankruptcy in 2013 after battery supplier A123's bankruptcy, manufacturing delays, and a government loan default.
lesson: Overreliance on a single battery supplier creates existential single-point-of-failure risk for an EV startup.

name: Fisker Inc
summary: EV maker of the Fisker Ocean SUV filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2024 after quality complaints, recalls, and inability to scale production or secure additional financing.
lesson: Automotive manufacturing requires operational discipline and capital reserves far beyond what most software-style fundraising cycles provide.
source: Reuters, June 2024 Fisker Chapter 11 filing coverage

name: Fisker Inc. (2nd company)
summary: Henrik Fisker's second EV venture launched the Ocean SUV but filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in June 2024 after severe production quality issues, plunging sales, and inability to raise further capital.
lesson: Rushing a complex vehicle to market without a mature manufacturing and quality process leads to a death spiral of recalls and returns.

name: Fitness First (UK)
funding: Private equity owned (BC Partners, later Oaktree)
summary: Once Europe's largest gym chain, Fitness First underwent a debt restructuring in 2012 after private-equity leverage left it unable to service its debt amid falling membership.
lesson: Aggressive private-equity leverage on a physical fitness chain can force restructuring even without a single external shock.

name: Fitocracy
summary: Gamified fitness social network Fitocracy shut down its consumer app in 2018 after failing to convert an engaged early community into a sustainable business.
lesson: Strong early community engagement does not guarantee a viable monetization path for a fitness social app.

name: Fitplan
summary: Celebrity-trainer workout app Fitplan wound down operations in 2023 after failing to sustain subscriber growth against larger fitness content platforms.
lesson: A content-licensing model built around individual trainer personalities can struggle to scale against platforms with broader content libraries.

name: Fits.me
summary: Estonian virtual fitting-room robot and software company for online apparel retailers was acquired by Rakuten in 2014, then its technology was discontinued and the standalone service shut down by 2018.
lesson: A virtual fitting-room technology can be acquired for its IP yet still be shut down if the acquirer deprioritizes the product line.

name: Flickr
summary: Pioneering photo-sharing site that declined for years under Yahoo's neglect before being sold off to SmugMug in 2018 in a much-diminished state.
lesson: A once-dominant product can lose all relevance if an acquirer starves it of investment for years.
source: The Verge, Flickr sale to SmugMug 2018

name: FlightCar
summary: Airport car-sharing startup letting travelers rent out their cars while away that shut down in 2017 amid insurance disputes, lawsuits, and unsustainable operating losses.
lesson: Insurance and liability complexity in peer-to-peer vehicle sharing can create legal exposure that outpaces revenue.

name: Flirtey
