summary: Drone-delivery pioneer that conducted some of the first FAA-approved delivery flights in the US ceased most commercial operations by 2022 as larger-funded rivals like Zipline and Wing captured the emerging market.
lesson: Being first to prove regulatory feasibility doesn't guarantee commercial survival against better-capitalized later entrants.
source: Industry press coverage of Flirtey's operational wind-down

name: Fluidic Energy (NantEnergy)
summary: Fluidic Energy (later rebranded NantEnergy) built zinc-air batteries for microgrids in emerging markets but wound down after failing to reach sustainable commercial scale despite heavy investment from Patrick Soon-Shiong.
lesson: Emerging-market microgrid battery deployments face slow sales cycles that can outlast even well-funded patient capital.

name: Flywheel Sports
summary: Boutique indoor cycling chain Flywheel Sports permanently closed all studios and shut its at-home bike business in 2020 amid competition from Peloton and pandemic closures.
lesson: Boutique studio chains with high fixed costs are highly vulnerable to a well-funded at-home competitor and demand shocks.

name: Forever 21
summary: Fast-fashion giant filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in September 2019 after aggressive global overexpansion left it with too many oversized stores as mall traffic and fast-fashion competition (Zara, H&M, Shein) intensified.
lesson: Overexpansion into large-format mall leases becomes a fatal liability once shopping habits and competitors shift online.
source: Wall Street Journal, Reuters coverage of Forever 21 Chapter 11, 2019

name: Forge Global
summary: Private-company secondary share marketplace went public via SPAC in 2022 but its stock fell over 90% as trading volumes and late-stage valuations declined sharply.
lesson: Marketplaces tied to speculative asset trading volumes suffer badly when the broader risk appetite cools.

name: Forto
summary: German digital freight forwarder (formerly FreightHub) that cut roughly half its workforce and restructured operations in 2023 after aggressive expansion outpaced sustainable revenue.
lesson: Rapid geographic expansion in freight forwarding without matching operational efficiency leads to painful restructuring when funding tightens.

name: ForUsAll
summary: 401(k) provider for small businesses drew a Department of Labor lawsuit over a partnership letting participants allocate retirement savings into cryptocurrency, damaging its enterprise reputation.
lesson: Retirement plan providers face outsized regulatory risk when offering speculative investment options to plan participants.

name: Forward Health
summary: Concierge primary-care startup founded by ex-Google exec Adrian Aoun built high-tech physical clinics and later automated 'CarePods'; it shut down operations after failing to reach sustainable scale.
lesson: High-touch, capital-intensive physical clinics are extremely expensive to scale nationally.

name: Fove
summary: Fove built the first commercially available eye-tracking VR headset via Kickstarter, but failed to scale beyond a niche developer audience before quietly ceasing consumer hardware sales.
lesson: Introducing an advanced feature (eye tracking) years before mainstream headsets adopted it left too small a market to sustain a standalone hardware company.
source: Road to VR reporting on Fove headset and company trajectory

name: Frank
summary: Financial-aid application startup sold to JPMorgan Chase for $175M in 2021; JPMorgan later sued founder Charlie Javice alleging she fabricated millions of fake user accounts to inflate the sale price.
lesson: Acquirers must independently verify user metrics rather than relying on target-provided data during diligence.

name: Frank And Oak
summary: Montreal-based menswear subscription and e-commerce brand filed for bankruptcy protection in 2018 after overexpanding into retail stores, and was later acquired out of restructuring by La Maison Simons.
lesson: Subscription-box brands that pivot into costly brick-and-mortar retail can outrun their cash runway.
source: Retail Dive, Fashion Network coverage of Frank And Oak bankruptcy 2018

name: Freetrade
summary: UK commission-free trading app closed its EU operations and took a steep down-round valuation cut after failing to reach profitability amid intense competition.
lesson: Commission-free trading apps need massive scale or ancillary revenue to survive against better-capitalized rivals.

name: FreeWire Technologies
summary: FreeWire Technologies made battery-integrated EV fast chargers designed to avoid costly grid upgrades but underwent major layoffs in 2024 amid a broader EV charging slowdown and funding difficulties.
lesson: Clever engineering to sidestep grid infrastructure costs still needs matching EV adoption timing to sustain the business.

name: Freight Farms
summary: Container-farm maker Freight Farms, a pioneer of shipping-container hydroponic farms, ceased operations in 2024 after failing to raise further financing.
lesson: Small-format containerized farming struggles to reach the scale economics needed to compete with larger indoor operators.

name: FreightHub
summary: Berlin-based digital freight forwarder that merged with competitor Forto in 2019 after struggling to scale independently in the crowded European freight-tech market.
lesson: Undifferentiated freight-forwarding platforms often end up as consolidation targets rather than standalone winners.

name: Freshly
summary: Freshly sold prepared, ready-to-heat healthy meals by mail and was acquired by Nestlé in 2020, but Nestlé shut the business down in 2023 after it failed to become profitable.
lesson: A large strategic acquisition does not guarantee survival if the acquired unit's core economics never improve.

name: Freshly Picked meal delivery (Territory Foods)
summary: Territory Foods delivered chef-prepared, dietitian-approved meals sourced from local chefs across US metros, but scaled back significantly and effectively wound down by 2023 amid weak unit economics in prepared-meal delivery.
lesson: Distributed local-chef sourcing adds supply-chain complexity that undermines the cost advantages meal delivery needs to survive.

name: Freshworks Studio (Farmshelf)
summary: Small-scale in-store and in-restaurant vertical farming unit maker Farmshelf ceased independent operations around 2022 after failing to scale beyond niche hospitality installations.
lesson: Micro-scale vertical farming units serving niche hospitality customers rarely generate enough volume to sustain a standalone hardware company.

name: FREYR Battery (now T1 Energy)
summary: Norwegian-founded FREYR Battery cancelled its planned $2.6B Georgia battery gigafactory and rebranded as T1 Energy in 2025, abandoning its original battery cell manufacturing mission to pivot to solar module production.
lesson: When battery cell economics and demand don't materialize on schedule, even well-capitalized gigafactory plans can be abandoned entirely.

name: Friday Health Plans
summary: Tech-enabled ACA marketplace health insurance startup expanded into multiple states quickly, then became insolvent in 2023, with regulators in several states placing it into liquidation.
lesson: Underpricing insurance risk while expanding into new state markets can trigger simultaneous, catastrophic losses.

name: FRIDAY Insurance
summary: German digital car insurer backed by Baloise ceased writing new retail auto policies in 2023 and sold its existing policy book after concluding the direct-to-consumer underwriting model could not be made profitable.
lesson: A strong balance-sheet backer can fund years of underwriting losses but won't indefinitely subsidize a structurally unprofitable model.

name: Frontback
summary: French dual-camera photo-sharing app that gained early buzz but shut down in 2016 after failing to grow its user base sustainably.
lesson: Photo-sharing apps entering a market already dominated by Instagram face an uphill network-effects battle.

name: FTX
summary: Once the world's second-largest crypto exchange, FTX collapsed in November 2022 after Alameda Research's balance sheet was exposed as built on FTX's own illiquid token.
lesson: Commingling customer funds with a trading affiliate is fraud, not a business model.

name: Full Stack Modular
summary: Modular high-rise construction company, successor to the Forest City modular tower program in Brooklyn, that wound down after cost overruns and quality issues on its flagship project.
lesson: Unproven modular manufacturing techniques applied to complex high-rise projects can produce cost overruns that erase the promised savings.
source: The Real Deal, New York YIMBY coverage of modular tower issues

name: FutureAdvisor
summary: Early robo-advisor acquired by BlackRock in 2015 for roughly $150M was quietly shut down six years later after failing to gain traction as an embedded advice product.
lesson: An acquisition exit doesn't guarantee survival if the acquirer can't find a working distribution model for the product.

name: Fyde
summary: Zero-trust remote access startup was quietly acquired by Barracuda Networks in 2019 in a small, largely unpublicized deal after a short independent run.
lesson: Being early to the zero-trust access category doesn't guarantee independent survival if funding runs out before reaching scale.

name: Fyodor Biotechnologies
summary: Wellness-diagnostics startup Fyodor Biotechnologies failed to gain sufficient commercial traction for its at-home diagnostic products and wound down operations.
lesson: Diagnostic-adjacent wellness products face slower regulatory and adoption timelines that can exhaust startup runway.

name: Fyre Media (Fyre Festival)
summary: Luxury music festival venture collapsed spectacularly when the promised Bahamas festival turned out to be built on fraudulent marketing, leading to the founder's imprisonment.
lesson: Marketing hype divorced from operational reality in event/travel experiences can constitute outright fraud, not just failure.

name: Gaana
summary: One of India's earliest major music streaming apps that laid off most of its staff and scaled down operations dramatically in 2024 after years of losses.
lesson: Being an early market leader in streaming is not enough if free-tier economics never convert to sustainable paid conversion.

name: GameFly (rental business)
summary: The Netflix-style DVD and game-disc-by-mail rental pioneer wound down its physical rental business in 2019 as digital distribution and downloads made mail-order rental obsolete.
lesson: A rental logistics business built around physical media has no defense once its industry moves natively to digital distribution.

name: Garden.com
summary: Online gardening supplies retailer shut down in 2001 after the dot-com downturn dried up funding for its niche, low-margin category.
lesson: Niche ecommerce categories with thin margins are especially exposed when capital markets tighten.

name: Gawker Media
summary: A network of irreverent blogs (Gawker, Deadspin, Jezebel, Gizmodo) built a large audience but was forced into bankruptcy after a $140M jury verdict in Hulk Hogan's Peter Thiel-funded invasion-of-privacy lawsuit.
lesson: A single unresolved legal liability, especially one bankrolled by a hostile billionaire, can bankrupt an otherwise profitable media company overnight.

name: GE Digital / Predix
summary: GE's ambitious industrial IoT software platform, meant to make GE a top-10 software company, was drastically scaled back and largely divested after 2018 amid huge losses.
lesson: An industrial conglomerate cannot force software-company economics onto a platform built primarily to serve its own hardware ecosystem.

name: Generation Orbit Launch Services
summary: Air-launched small-satellite rocket developer (GOLauncher) shut down commercial launch operations in 2022 after a failed hypersonic test-vehicle flight and inability to secure further funding for its orbital vehicle.
lesson: Pivoting between hypersonic test-vehicle contracts and an orbital launch product split focus and capital too thin to succeed at either.

name: Genesis Global Capital
summary: Major institutional crypto lender owned by Digital Currency Group suspended withdrawals after the FTX collapse exposed its exposure and filed for Chapter 11.
lesson: Institutional lenders are only as sound as the riskiest counterparty in their loan book.

name: Genesis Global Trading
summary: One of the largest crypto prime brokers and lenders filed for bankruptcy in January 2023 after exposure to Three Arrows Capital and FTX left it unable to meet withdrawal obligations to Gemini and other partners.
lesson: Wholesale lenders that don't match loan durations to counterparty risk can be brought down by a handful of bad loans.

name: Genius (Rap Genius)
summary: A crowdsourced lyrics-annotation platform raised significant venture funding at a media-company valuation but repeatedly laid off staff from 2023 onward after failing to build a business matching that valuation.
lesson: A community-driven content platform with huge engagement can still fail to convert that engagement into venture-scale revenue.

name: Genocea Biosciences
summary: T-cell-directed herpes vaccine and later cancer immunotherapy programs failed to show sufficient efficacy, leading to bankruptcy after burning through hundreds of millions.
lesson: A differentiated immunology platform still needs a clinical winner within a finite cash runway.

name: Geofeedia
summary: Social media surveillance tool sold to police departments collapsed after an ACLU investigation revealed it was used to monitor Black Lives Matter activists, prompting platforms to cut off its data access.
lesson: Building a business entirely dependent on social platforms' data APIs is fragile if the use case violates those platforms' policies.

name: Get Satisfaction
summary: Community-based customer feedback and support platform acquired by Sprinklr in 2015, after which the standalone product was discontinued and customers migrated off the platform.
lesson: A differentiated community-support niche can still lose to broader all-in-one CX suites that bundle the same capability.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Sprinklr's 2015 acquisition of Get Satisfaction

name: Getaround
summary: Peer-to-peer car-sharing marketplace that went public via SPAC in 2022 at a rich valuation but saw its stock collapse over 99%, leading to a Nasdaq delisting in 2024.
lesson: A SPAC listing can mask unresolved unit-economics problems that eventually surface in public markets.

name: Getir
summary: Istanbul-founded rapid grocery delivery pioneer that expanded aggressively into the US, UK and Germany, acquiring Gorillas and FreshDirect, then retreated from all three markets in 2023.
lesson: Aggressive international expansion in quick-commerce outpaced the ability to reach profitability anywhere.

name: Getsurance
summary: German digital insurance broker targeting freelancers and gig workers with gadget and liability policies shut down in 2019 after failing to reach profitability in a crowded niche.
lesson: Niche insurance products for underserved segments still need enough addressable market to justify the acquisition spend.

name: Gfinity plc
summary: The British publicly listed esports company saw its share price collapse more than 95% from its 2014 listing amid repeated losses, forcing multiple restructurings and a much-diminished business.
lesson: Going public before an esports business model is proven exposes a company to punishing, permanent capital-market discipline.

name: Gigaom
summary: A respected tech-industry news and research site abruptly shut down in 2015 after failing to raise further financing to cover mounting debts.
lesson: A trusted editorial brand can still collapse suddenly if working capital runs out faster than a financing round can close.

name: Gigster
summary: Software-development freelance/agency marketplace that collapsed around 2020 after mass layoffs and failure to deliver consistent project quality at scale.
lesson: Marketplaces for complex, high-touch services like custom software struggle to standardize quality without heavy internal project management.

name: Gilt Groupe
summary: Flash-sale luxury fashion site was sold to Hudson's Bay Company for just $250M in January 2016 (roughly 76% below peak valuation) as the flash-sale model lost momentum, and was resold/wound down by 2018.
lesson: A first-mover advantage in a trendy e-commerce format erodes fast once the underlying model (flash sales) stops generating repeat demand.
source: CNN Money, CBC coverage of Gilt Groupe sale to Hudson's Bay 2016

name: Glamsquad
summary: On-demand beauty services app (hair, makeup, manicures at home) shut down in 2020 after the COVID-19 pandemic eliminated demand for in-home beauty appointments and the gig-labor unit economics never reached profitability.
lesson: On-demand service marketplaces with high labor costs per transaction are especially vulnerable to demand shocks that hollow out volume.

name: Global Eagle Entertainment
funding: publicly traded, hundreds of millions in debt
summary: Satellite-based in-flight connectivity and entertainment provider filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in July 2020 after the COVID-19 pandemic collapsed airline traffic, its core customer base, on top of an already heavy debt load.
lesson: A satellite connectivity business tied to a single cyclical customer industry (aviation) has no hedge when that industry stops flying.

name: Globalstar (original entity)
summary: Satellite phone operator built by Loral and Qualcomm filed Chapter 11 in 2002 after mobile phone subscriptions came in far below projections, though the network and brand survived reorganization.
lesson: Satellite phone economics collapse quickly when cheap terrestrial cellular coverage expands faster than the satellite rollout.

name: GlowRoad
summary: Indian social-commerce reseller marketplace connecting small resellers with wholesale suppliers that scaled back operations significantly in 2023 amid funding constraints and heavy competition from larger platforms.
lesson: Reseller marketplaces built on thin per-order margins are vulnerable once larger platforms subsidize the same customer segment.
source: Indian tech press (Entrackr) coverage of GlowRoad's 2023 struggles

name: GoBank
summary: Green Dot's mobile-first checking account brand was discontinued in 2018 as the company consolidated its banking products under other brands.
lesson: Large incumbents can retire a promising consumer sub-brand once portfolio consolidation looks more efficient.

name: GoHealth
summary: Medicare insurance marketplace went public via a $1.2B-plus IPO in 2020 but its stock collapsed over 95% amid high customer acquisition costs and regulatory scrutiny of Medicare Advantage marketing.
lesson: Lead-generation-heavy insurance brokers are exposed to acquisition cost inflation and increased regulatory oversight.

name: Gold's Gym
summary: Gold's Gym International filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2020, closing dozens of corporate-owned locations after pandemic-driven revenue loss.
lesson: Franchise-heavy gym brands can still face bankruptcy at the corporate level when owned locations can't cover fixed costs during a shock.

name: Golden Spike Company
summary: Startup announced plans to offer commercial crewed missions to the Moon using existing launch vehicles, but never secured a customer or the capital to begin hardware development and quietly dissolved.
lesson: Announcing an ambitious mission architecture without committed customers or funded hardware development is marketing, not a company.

name: Good Catch
summary: Good Catch (Gathered Foods) sold plant-based tuna and seafood alternatives at major retailers but significantly scaled back operations in 2023 as plant-based category sales growth slowed across the board.
lesson: Even retail-shelf presence cannot save a plant-based brand once overall category growth stalls and funding tightens.

name: Good Eggs
summary: Online local-food grocery delivery startup Good Eggs shut down its New York and Los Angeles operations in 2015 and scaled back sharply, with the broader business winding down further by 2019.
lesson: Expanding a low-margin perishable-goods delivery model to multiple cities simultaneously can outrun the operational maturity needed to sustain it.

name: Good Technology
summary: Mobile device and data security vendor withdrew a planned IPO after its valuation cratered, and was ultimately sold to BlackBerry in 2015 for $425M — far below the $1B+ it had once expected.
lesson: Delaying an IPO while market sentiment and competitive position deteriorate can permanently destroy the exit value investors were counting on.

name: GoodData
summary: Embedded analytics and BI platform that underwent major layoffs and a distressed sale/restructuring around 2023 after years of struggling to match cloud-native BI competitors.
lesson: Being an early mover in cloud BI is not enough if the product fails to keep pace with newer, more self-service analytics platforms.

name: Google Daydream
summary: Google's Daydream mobile VR platform and headset were discontinued in 2019 after just three years, as smartphone-based VR failed to gain lasting traction against standalone headsets.
lesson: Smartphone-tethered VR was quickly outcompeted by cheaper, more convenient standalone headsets like Oculus Go and Quest.

name: Google Glass (consumer and enterprise editions)
summary: Google Glass launched to consumers in 2013 to privacy backlash and was discontinued for consumers in 2015; the pivoted Enterprise Edition was also discontinued in 2023 after failing to reach broad adoption.
lesson: Wearable camera-equipped AR devices faced social and privacy backlash that even a full B2C-to-enterprise pivot could not fully overcome.
source: Google Glass Enterprise Edition discontinuation reporting, 2023

name: Google Jump
summary: Google's Jump VR camera rig and cloud stitching service for 360-degree video production was shut down in 2019 as demand for cinematic VR content production tools failed to materialize at scale.
lesson: Building production infrastructure for a content format before consumer demand is proven risks the tooling becoming obsolete before adoption occurs.

name: Google Stadia
summary: Google's cloud-gaming platform launched with heavy marketing but failed to build a large enough library or user base, leading Google to shut it down in 2023 and refund purchases.
lesson: Even a company with immense infrastructure and capital can misjudge whether the market is ready for a new distribution model.

name: Google Tilt Brush
summary: Google's Tilt Brush, a pioneering 3D VR painting app, was discontinued as a commercial product in 2021 and open-sourced, as Google deprioritized VR content investment.
lesson: Creative VR tools acclaimed by early adopters can still be shelved when the parent company's broader VR strategy shifts away from content investment.

name: Google+
summary: Google's multibillion-dollar bid to challenge Facebook never achieved meaningful organic engagement and was shut down in 2019 following a data-exposure bug.
lesson: Forcing adoption across a company's other products cannot manufacture genuine social engagement.

name: GoPro Karma
summary: GoPro's first consumer drone, Karma, was recalled shortly after launch in 2016 due to a power-loss defect, relaunched in 2017, and discontinued entirely in 2018 as GoPro exited the drone market amid DJI's dominance.
lesson: Entering an established hardware category late with an unreliable first product compounds competitive disadvantage.
source: TechCrunch coverage of GoPro's exit from the drone market, 2018

name: Gorillas
summary: Berlin rapid grocery delivery pioneer that scaled to dozens of cities in under two years before being acquired by rival Getir in a distressed down-round deal.
lesson: Hypergrowth in 10-minute grocery delivery outran the ability to reach profitable dark-store economics.

name: GoStudent
summary: Austrian tutoring unicorn that reached a $3B valuation but laid off roughly a third of staff across 2023-2024 amid a funding-environment slowdown and cash-burn concerns.
lesson: Rapid multi-country expansion funded by growth-stage capital is fragile when funding markets tighten.

name: Gotcha Mobility
summary: South Carolina-based micromobility and car-share operator serving smaller college towns that ceased operations in 2023 as funding dried up.
lesson: Serving smaller secondary markets doesn't insulate a mobility startup from the same capital constraints as big-city rivals.

name: Gothamist (original, DNAinfo era)
summary: A well-regarded New York City news and culture blog was acquired by Joe Ricketts' DNAinfo in 2017, then shut down alongside DNAinfo just months later after the newsroom unionized, before being revived by New York Public Radio in 2018.
lesson: Acquisition by an owner hostile to unionization can end a media brand's operation almost as quickly as the deal that created it.

name: Grabango
summary: Cashierless checkout-vision company founded by Pandora co-founder Will Glaser, piloted with 7-Eleven, Circle K and Aldi, ceased operations in October 2024 after failing to secure additional funding.
lesson: Capital-intensive computer-vision retail infrastructure needs a clear path to profitability before funding markets tighten, since cheaper self-checkout kiosks can win on cost alone.

name: Grantland
summary: Bill Simmons' ESPN-backed sports and pop-culture site built a devoted following but was shuttered after Simmons' contract with ESPN was not renewed.
lesson: A media brand built tightly around one star founder's voice is existentially exposed to that founder's relationship with the parent company.

name: GreenSky
summary: Point-of-sale home-improvement lender acquired by Goldman Sachs in 2021 for $2.24B as part of its consumer banking push; Goldman sold it to Sixth Street in 2023 for roughly a quarter of that price as it retreated from consumer lending.
lesson: Even a well-established fintech can become a write-down when its acquirer's own strategic priorities change.

name: Griddy
summary: Texas retail electricity startup Griddy sold power at wholesale prices to consumers but collapsed in 2021 when Winter Storm Uri sent prices to the market cap, saddling customers with thousands of dollars in bills and triggering its shutdown by regulators.
lesson: Passing raw wholesale price volatility directly to consumers without a cap or hedge is a business model one extreme weather event away from disaster.

name: Griswold Home Care
summary: One of the largest 1099-contractor home care franchise networks in the US; it shut down all California locations in 2016 after a Department of Labor ruling required home care workers to be classified as W-2 employees.
lesson: An entire home care business model built on contractor classification can become instantly unviable in a single state the moment labor law changes.

name: Grokker
summary: Corporate wellness video platform Grokker ceased operations in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable scale amid competition from larger wellness benefit platforms.
lesson: A content-only wellness offering can be squeezed out by broader benefits platforms that bundle more services for employers.

name: Grooveshark
summary: Ad-supported streaming site that let users freely upload and share tracks; shut down in 2015 as part of a settlement with major record labels over copyright infringement.
lesson: Growth built on unlicensed content is not sustainable against well-resourced rights holders.

name: Grouparoo
summary: Open-source reverse ETL startup that wound down operations in 2023, with its team and technology joining Airbyte.
lesson: Being early to a hot category (reverse ETL) is not sufficient if better-funded competitors capture the market faster.

name: Groupon
summary: Daily deals pioneer IPO'd in 2011 at a multi-billion-dollar valuation but its stock lost over 99% of its value as the deals model proved unsustainable and merchants churned.
lesson: A viral growth model built on one-time deal customers rarely produces durable repeat revenue.

name: Grow (Grin + Yellow)
summary: Latin American bike and scooter-sharing giant formed by the merger of Brazil's Yellow and Mexico's Grin that shut down operations across the region in 2020 amid the COVID-19 demand collapse and cash shortages.
lesson: Merging two cash-burning competitors doesn't fix the underlying unit economics, especially heading into a demand shock.

name: Grubhub
summary: Early online food ordering and delivery pioneer IPO'd in 2014 but was sold by Just Eat Takeaway to Wonder Group in 2024 for about $650M, a steep loss from the $7.3B acquisition price paid in 2021.
lesson: Market share leadership in food delivery erodes fast without continual reinvestment against better-funded rivals.

name: Guardhat
summary: Connected safety-wearables (smart hardhat) company for construction and industrial sites that faced significant financial difficulty and scaled back operations in the early 2020s.
lesson: Wearable hardware for construction safety faces long enterprise sales cycles that can outlast a startup's runway.
source: Trade press coverage of Guardhat's financial struggles

name: Guevara
summary: UK peer-to-peer car insurance startup that let groups of drivers pool premiums and share surplus shut down in 2016 after struggling to get regulatory approval and reach meaningful scale.
lesson: Peer-to-peer insurance models face steep regulatory and actuarial hurdles that are hard to clear with early-stage capital.

name: Guvera
summary: Australian music streaming startup that collapsed after a scandal-plagued attempted IPO was rejected by regulators over its financial disclosures.
lesson: Aggressive fundraising narratives that outpace real financial fundamentals eventually collide with regulatory scrutiny.
source: Australian Financial Review, Guvera IPO scandal coverage

name: Habit
summary: Habit offered personalized nutrition plans based on DNA and blood-marker testing and was acquired by DSM in 2016, but DSM shut the consumer service down in 2019 after it failed to reach mainstream adoption.
lesson: Personalized-nutrition testing services require repeat consumer engagement that many one-time DNA test buyers never provide.

name: Handy
summary: Home services marketplace that merged into ANGI Homeservices in 2018 at a valuation far below its earlier venture funding rounds, after years of high customer-acquisition costs and worker-classification legal battles.
lesson: Chronic worker-classification lawsuits and high paid-acquisition costs can force even a well-known consumer/business services brand into a distress sale.
source: TechCrunch coverage of the Handy-ANGI merger (2018)

name: Happn
summary: Location-based French dating app that let users see people they'd crossed paths with in real life; underwent mass layoffs in 2022 and shrank dramatically.
lesson: A distinctive location mechanic doesn't guarantee retention once bigger apps copy the core idea with more resources.

name: Harvest CROO Robotics
summary: Strawberry-picking robot developer Harvest CROO Robotics faced major funding shortfalls and slowed development significantly by 2023 after years of delayed commercial deployment.
lesson: Complex multi-armed harvesting robots for delicate fruit require far longer and more expensive development cycles than initial timelines suggest.
source: AgFunder News, Harvest CROO Robotics coverage

name: HauteLook
summary: Flash-sale fashion ecommerce site was acquired by Nordstrom in 2011 for $180M and was folded into Nordstrom Rack before being shut down in 2021.
lesson: Flash-sale ecommerce formats can lose relevance even under a well-capitalized retail parent.

name: Haven
funding: Backed by Maersk and McKinsey Ventures
summary: Ocean freight booking and supply-chain visibility joint venture backed by Maersk and McKinsey that was shut down in 2020 as Maersk consolidated digital efforts internally.
lesson: Corporate-backed joint ventures can be wound down quickly once the parent's strategic priorities shift internally.

name: Health IQ
summary: Life insurance marketer targeting 'health-conscious' consumers with lower premiums shut down after struggling to convert its niche audience into a sustainable underwriting book.
lesson: A clever marketing wedge does not guarantee the underlying insurance economics will work at scale.

name: Hedgeable
summary: One of the earliest robo-advisors offering alternative-asset exposure and hedged portfolios shut down after failing to reach the scale of Betterment and Wealthfront.
lesson: Being first to market in robo-advising wasn't enough without the capital to out-market better-funded rivals.

name: Helbiz
summary: NYC-based micromobility company that went public via SPAC in 2021, acquired multiple failing scooter rivals, but was delisted from Nasdaq in 2024 amid steep losses.
lesson: Rolling up other struggling scooter companies compounds losses rather than fixing the underlying economics.

name: HelloWallet
summary: Employee financial wellness platform acquired by Morningstar in 2014 was later shut down as Morningstar consolidated its workplace retirement offerings.
lesson: Being folded into a larger acquirer's roadmap can mean quiet discontinuation rather than growth.

name: Helprace
summary: Small independent helpdesk and community-support software vendor Helprace ceased active development and support around 2019 amid a saturated low-end helpdesk market.
lesson: Bootstrapped helpdesk tools without a distribution advantage struggle to survive once free and freemium alternatives dominate the entry-level market.
source: Product review site archives noting Helprace's discontinued updates

name: High Fidelity
summary: High Fidelity, founded by Second Life creator Philip Rosedale to build a next-generation social VR platform, pivoted away from consumer VR to spatial audio and eventually wound down its VR ambitions entirely.
lesson: Even an experienced virtual-world founder can struggle to repeat past success when the underlying hardware market isn't ready.
source: VentureBeat/UploadVR reporting on High Fidelity pivot and shutdown

name: Highrise
summary: 37signals' simple contact-management CRM Highrise was spun out as an independent company in 2014 but stagnated against modern CRMs and was effectively wound down by 2018.
lesson: A once-innovative simple CRM can be leapfrogged if it stops shipping features while competitors integrate messaging, automation, and analytics.
source: 37signals blog posts on Highrise's spinout and later stagnation

name: Hippo Insurance
summary: Home insurance startup that went public via SPAC at a roughly $5B valuation saw its stock collapse over 90% after severe underwriting losses forced it to exit several state markets and restructure its business.
lesson: Rapid geographic expansion in property insurance can outpace a young carrier's ability to price catastrophe risk correctly.

name: Hitbox.tv / Smashcast
funding: Privately funded, merged with Azubu in 2017
summary: The livestreaming platform built around fighting-game and esports communities merged with Azubu to form Smashcast in 2017, but the combined entity still shut down in 2020, unable to compete with Twitch and YouTube Gaming.
lesson: Merging two struggling challengers rarely creates enough combined scale to overcome a dominant platform's network effects.

name: Hodlnaut
summary: Singapore crypto lender halted withdrawals in August 2022 and applied for creditor protection after suffering losses from the Terra/LUNA collapse.
lesson: Concentrating lending yield strategies in a single volatile asset class leaves no margin for systemic shocks.
source: Monetary Authority of Singapore statements on Hodlnaut, 2022

name: Hointer
summary: Automated apparel retail concept using RFID, robotics and a mobile app to let shoppers try on clothes without browsing racks closed its Seattle and Yakima stores in 2016 after failing to prove the model scaled to more retail categories.
lesson: An innovative in-store technology experience must fit how a specific retail category's shoppers actually behave, not just be technically impressive.

name: Homejoy
summary: On-demand home cleaning marketplace backed by Google Ventures shut down after burning through cash and facing worker-classification lawsuits.
lesson: Subsidized on-demand marketplaces can collapse quickly when customer acquisition costs outpace retention and legal risk mounts.

name: Hometeam
summary: A NY/NJ home care marketplace for hiring vetted caregivers that lost its founding CEO and president in 2018 and was forced to abandon its direct-to-consumer model, pivoting to a B2B Medicaid dual-eligible business.
lesson: A 1099-marketplace pricing advantage evaporates the moment regulators require W-2 employment, and pivoting away from the original vision often signals the first business never found true product-market fit.

name: Homicide Watch
summary: An award-winning hyperlocal crime-reporting site covering every homicide in Washington, D.C. shut down in 2014 after its founders could not build a financially sustainable independent operation.
lesson: Critically acclaimed, civically valuable local journalism can still fail commercially without a scalable revenue model.

name: Hopin
summary: Virtual-events SaaS platform rocketed to a $7.75B valuation during the pandemic, then saw demand collapse as in-person events returned; sold its core product to RingCentral for a fraction of capital raised.
lesson: Pandemic-driven demand spikes can reverse just as fast, punishing companies that scaled to match the peak.

name: Hortonworks
summary: Hadoop distribution company that IPO'd in 2014 but saw its stock collapse as cloud data warehouses replaced on-prem Hadoop, ultimately merging into Cloudera in 2019 from a position of weakness.
lesson: Public listing doesn't validate a business model if the underlying technology category is being displaced by cloud-native alternatives.

name: Houseparty
summary: The group video-chat app spiked during COVID lockdowns but usage collapsed once Zoom and FaceTime added similar features; Epic Games shut it down in 2021.
lesson: A pandemic-driven usage spike can mask the absence of durable competitive advantage.

name: HQ Trivia
summary: The live mobile trivia game show was a viral 2017-18 phenomenon but usage crashed after co-founder disputes and it failed to build a sustainable business, shutting down in 2020.
lesson: A live scheduled format that depends on constant novelty struggles to retain users once the fad fades.

name: Huddle
summary: Australian peer-to-peer insurance startup offering community-pooled coverage for niche groups like motorcyclists shut down in 2021 after failing to scale beyond small affinity communities.
lesson: Peer-to-peer insurance pools work best at a scale that most affinity-group startups never reach.

name: Hukkster
summary: Browser-extension shopping tool that alerted consumers to price drops and sales on watched retail items shut down in 2015 after failing to build a sustainable retailer-revenue or affiliate model.
lesson: Consumer shopping tools that save users money need a monetization model that doesn't conflict with the retailers whose margins they erode.

name: Human Longevity Inc
summary: Craig Venter's ambitious longevity and whole-genome sequencing company faced co-founder lawsuits, leadership turnover, and business-model struggles, eventually selling off major assets.
lesson: A visionary founding team is not sufficient without a validated near-term revenue model in genomics.

name: Humane
summary: Ex-Apple designers built the AI Pin, a screenless wearable AI assistant that launched to scathing reviews; the company sold its assets to HP in 2025.
lesson: Star pedigree and hype cycles don't protect a product from fundamentally unsolved hardware and UX problems at launch.

name: Hungry Planet
summary: Hungry Planet made plant-based meat alternatives targeted at foodservice and institutional buyers but quietly wound down around 2022 amid intensifying competition from Beyond Meat and Impossible Foods.
lesson: Smaller plant-based meat producers without major retail distribution get squeezed out once category leaders scale.

name: Hyzon Motors
summary: Hydrogen fuel-cell truck maker Hyzon Motors wound down US commercial operations in 2025 after an SEC investigation into its revenue recognition practices and years of heavy losses.
lesson: Overstating early revenue in a nascent hydrogen trucking market invites regulatory action that can end operations even with substantial funding.
source: SEC filings and Reuters, 2024-2025, Hyzon Motors investigation

name: IAM Robotics
summary: Pittsburgh-based warehouse item-picking robot maker (Swift) ceased operations in 2023 after failing to secure additional funding in a tightened venture climate for robotics.
lesson: Warehouse robotics requires deep, patient capital; a funding-market downturn can be fatal even with working technology.
source: The Robot Report coverage of IAM Robotics closure, 2023

name: IBM Watson Health
summary: IBM's flagship healthcare AI division failed to deliver on promises of AI-driven cancer treatment recommendations and was sold off in pieces for about $1B.
lesson: Overpromising AI capability in high-stakes domains like healthcare invites credibility collapse when results disappoint.

name: ICO Global Communications
summary: Mobile satellite communications operator building a medium-Earth-orbit constellation filed Chapter 11 in 1999 after the rise of terrestrial cellular networks undercut its business case before launch.
lesson: A multi-year satellite build cycle can be overtaken by faster-moving terrestrial competitors solving the same problem more cheaply.

name: iflix
summary: A Netflix-style streaming service targeting emerging markets raised over $300M but was sold off for parts to Tencent in 2020 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Emerging-market streaming requires enormous capital to build content and infrastructure against well-funded global rivals.

name: IFTTT
summary: Consumer workflow-automation pioneer ('If This Then That') that underwent severe layoffs and near-collapse of its team in 2023 after failing to convert its huge user base into a sustainable business.
lesson: A large free user base does not guarantee a viable business model without a clear path to monetization.
source: Reporting on IFTTT layoffs and business struggles 2023

name: IGA Worldwide
summary: In-game advertising network IGA Worldwide struggled to generate sufficient advertiser demand and sold its assets in 2009 during the broader collapse of the in-game ad sector.
lesson: New ad inventory formats need proven advertiser demand, not just novel supply, to sustain a standalone business.
source: VentureBeat, GamesIndustry.biz coverage of IGA Worldwide 2009

name: IGN Pro League
summary: IGN's attempt to run a dedicated professional esports league and studio was discontinued after just a year due to high production costs and limited traction.
lesson: Media companies entering esports production need a sustained multi-year commitment, not a one-season experiment.

name: Ike
summary: Autonomous trucking startup founded by former Google and Otto engineers that was acquired by Nuro in 2022 primarily for its engineering talent, ending as an independent company.
lesson: Talent-acquisition exits in capital-intensive AV trucking are common when independent scaling proves too costly.

name: imeem
summary: Social music and video sharing site that struck licensing deals with major labels but burned through cash and sold its assets to MySpace Music in 2009.
lesson: Expensive content-licensing deals can outpace ad-supported revenue, making unit economics unsustainable.

name: Imergy Power Systems
summary: Imergy Power made vanadium redox flow batteries for microgrids and grid storage but shut down in 2016 after running out of capital.
lesson: Vanadium flow battery economics remained uncompetitive against falling lithium-ion prices during the mid-2010s.

name: Imperfect Foods
summary: Imperfect Foods sold surplus and "ugly" produce boxes to reduce food waste, but rising costs and slowing subscriber growth forced it to shut down in 2024 despite years of growth.
lesson: Mission-driven subscription grocery models must reach profitability before investor patience for growth-at-a-loss runs out.

name: Improbable
summary: The SpatialOS cloud-simulation platform failed to gain broad developer adoption for large-scale multiplayer games, forcing a pivot to metaverse tooling and deep layoffs in 2023.
lesson: Infrastructure-layer bets on how games 'should' be built can stall if the developer ecosystem doesn't adopt the new paradigm.

name: Improbable (SpatialOS metaverse business)
funding: $600M+ raised, including a SoftBank-led round valuing it at $2B+
summary: UK-based Improbable built the SpatialOS platform for large-scale multiplayer/metaverse worlds and raised over $600M, but wrote down its metaverse (M2) business and cut significant staff in 2023 after failing to find commercial traction.
lesson: Massive infrastructure investment for a speculative future market (the metaverse) can outpace actual paying enterprise or developer demand.
source: Sifted/TechCrunch reporting on Improbable layoffs and writedown, 2023

name: Inbound Health
summary: A hospital-at-home startup spun out of Allina Health that treated complex, often elderly patients in their homes instead of hospital beds; it shut down in December 2025 after a funding round fell through amid uncertainty over renewal of the federal Acute Hospital Care at Home waiver.
lesson: Hospital-at-home economics depend entirely on a temporary federal waiver — building a venture-scale business on a policy that could lapse at any Congressional session is a fundamental risk investors eventually price in.

name: Infarm
summary: Berlin-based in-store vertical farming company Infarm collapsed in 2023, filing for insolvency in Germany and the Netherlands after mass layoffs and unit withdrawals from retailers.
lesson: Distributed micro-farm networks in retail stores add logistics complexity that can outweigh the benefits of proximity to consumers.

name: InnovAge
summary: The largest for-profit PACE (Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly) operator; its 2021 IPO was followed within months by CMS enrollment freezes over care-quality failures, a CEO ouster, shareholder fraud suits and a stock collapse of over 80%.
lesson: Rapid for-profit scaling of a Medicare-regulated care model without matching investment in clinical quality and compliance invites regulatory sanctions that can crater a public listing.

name: Inovio Pharmaceuticals
summary: DNA vaccine developer's COVID-19 candidate was repeatedly delayed by FDA holds and its device-based delivery approach, missing the vaccine race entirely.
lesson: A vaccine delivery technology that requires specialized hardware faces a much slower regulatory and manufacturing path.

name: Instafreight
summary: German digital road-freight forwarder that merged into rival Forto in 2022 after failing to independently reach profitability amid intense competition among European freight-tech startups.
lesson: When multiple well-funded competitors chase the same freight-tech niche, consolidation often follows the funding downturn.

name: Insteon
summary: One of the longest-running smart-home platforms, it abruptly shut down all servers and operations in April 2022 with no warning, bricking devices for thousands of customers.
lesson: Even a mature, long-established smart-home brand can vanish overnight if it runs out of cash.

name: Instill Corporation
summary: Foodservice supply-chain B2B marketplace that raised heavily during the dot-com boom and shut down operations after failing to reach profitable transaction volume with restaurant distributors.
lesson: Fragmented, low-margin distribution industries are hard to digitize profitably without owning logistics as well as the marketplace layer.
source: Trade press coverage of dot-com era foodservice marketplaces

name: InsWeb
summary: One of the earliest online insurance comparison sites went public in 1999 but sold its remaining lead-generation assets in 2013 after years of declining revenue and competition from newer aggregators.
lesson: Being first to digitize insurance shopping doesn't protect market share once better-funded aggregators enter the category.

name: Insys Therapeutics
summary: Maker of fentanyl spray Subsys collapsed into bankruptcy after founder John Kapoor and executives were convicted of bribing doctors to overprescribe the powerful opioid.
lesson: Aggressive off-label sales tactics for a controlled substance invite criminal liability that can end a company overnight.

name: Intel Vaunt
funding: Corporate-funded internal project at Intel (New Devices Group)
summary: Intel's Vaunt smart glasses project, designed to be an unobtrusive everyday AR display, was shelved in 2018 before commercial launch as Intel restructured its wearables division.
lesson: Internal corporate incubation projects can be cancelled due to shifting parent-company priorities even when the underlying prototype receives positive press.
source: The Verge reporting on Intel Vaunt shutdown, 2018

name: Interactions Corporation
summary: Intelligent virtual assistant provider Interactions withdrew its planned SPAC/IPO in 2022 amid weak public market appetite and has since faced restructuring pressure despite remaining privately operating.
lesson: Capital-intensive conversational AI companies can be stranded when public markets close just as they need a funding event to validate years of investment.
source: Reuters reporting on Interactions Corporation's withdrawn 2022 IPO

name: International Business Times (IBT Media)
summary: A global business-news outlet grew rapidly through an SEO-driven, high-volume publishing strategy but faced repeated fraud and traffic-manipulation allegations tied to its founders' connections to Olivet University, damaging its credibility.
lesson: An aggressive SEO-volume publishing strategy built on ties to controversial funders can generate persistent credibility and legal exposure that outlasts any traffic gains.

name: International Launch Services (ILS)
summary: Joint venture marketing Russian Proton rockets to commercial satellite customers effectively ceased operations around 2022 after sanctions following Russia's invasion of Ukraine cut off its only launch vehicle from the Western commercial market.
lesson: A launch services business built entirely around one nation's rocket is existentially exposed to that nation's geopolitics.

name: Interplay Entertainment
summary: The Fallout/Baldur's Gate publisher spiraled into serious financial distress in the mid-2000s, selling off its most valuable IP (Fallout) to survive.
lesson: Selling your crown-jewel IP to stay solvent is often a company's last move before effective collapse.

name: InVision
summary: Pioneering design collaboration and prototyping platform that was overtaken by Figma's real-time collaborative editor, eventually discontinuing its core product in 2024.
lesson: A prototyping tool without native real-time multiplayer editing became obsolete once a competitor built that from the ground up.

name: Invitae
summary: Genetic-testing company scaled rapidly through acquisitions to become a genomics giant but never reached profitability and filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2024, selling off its assets.
lesson: Rolling up acquisitions to chase scale doesn't fix a fundamentally unprofitable core business.

name: IPsoft (Amelia)
summary: IPsoft, maker of the Amelia enterprise AI virtual agent, underwent major layoffs and restructuring in 2023 amid mounting losses despite two decades of operation and heavy investment.
lesson: A long operating history and marquee enterprise logos do not protect an AI vendor from collapse if the cost of delivering the AI exceeds what customers will pay.
source: Business Insider reporting on Amelia/IPsoft layoffs, 2023

name: Iridium SSC (original)
summary: Motorola-backed satellite phone constellation launched 66 satellites but filed the largest bankruptcy of its time in 1999 after signing up a fraction of the subscribers needed to cover debt service; assets were later bought for $25M and relaunched as Iridium Communications.
lesson: Building the technically hardest version of a product first can bankrupt a company even when the underlying network eventually finds a market.

name: Iris Dating
summary: AI-driven dating app that claimed to learn users' 'type' from swipes; shut down after being exposed for using fabricated/bot profile photos to appear more populated than it was.
lesson: Faking network density to mask a cold-start problem destroys trust faster than slow organic growth would have cost you.

name: IRL
summary: The event-planning social app claimed to be the 'anti-Facebook' and reached unicorn status, then shut down in 2023 after investigations found most of its claimed users were bots.
lesson: Inflated user metrics eventually collide with reality, especially once regulators start asking questions.

name: Iron Finance
summary: Partially-collateralized algorithmic stablecoin project (TITAN/IRON) suffered a bank-run style collapse in June 2021, with the TITAN token price falling to near zero within hours.
lesson: Partial-collateral algorithmic stablecoins can enter irreversible death spirals once redemptions outpace the backing.
source: CoinDesk/The Block coverage of Iron Finance collapse, June 2021

name: Iron Ox
summary: Robotic indoor farming startup Iron Ox laid off a large share of its workforce in 2022-2023 and scaled back its greenhouse-robotics ambitions after failing to hit growth targets.
lesson: Fully autonomous greenhouse robotics adds significant capex on top of the already-thin margins of controlled-environment agriculture.
source: AgFunder News, Iron Ox layoffs coverage 2022-2023

name: IronNet
summary: Network threat-detection company founded by former NSA director Gen. Keith Alexander, went public via SPAC in 2021 but revenue fell far short of projections; it furloughed staff, defaulted on an AWS bill, and filed for Chapter 7 liquidation in October 2023.
lesson: A prestigious founder and a hot SPAC market cannot substitute for durable revenue growth against projections made to public investors.

name: Jabong
summary: Indian fashion ecommerce marketplace was sold to rival Myntra (Flipkart Group) in 2016 for roughly $70M, a steep discount to the $225M+ it had raised.
lesson: Fast-scaling fashion marketplaces in emerging markets can burn capital faster than the category can support.

name: JackThreads
summary: Men's flash-sale fashion e-commerce site was acquired by Thrillist Media Group in 2014 but was shut down/restructured in 2016 as flash-sale demand cratered and the combined company pivoted to media.
lesson: The flash-sale e-commerce category as a whole lost its differentiation once full-price sites added regular discounting.
source: Digiday, Business Insider coverage of JackThreads wind-down

name: Jaunt VR
summary: Jaunt built VR cinematic camera technology and content but pivoted multiple times before winding down operations and selling assets around 2020 amid weak demand for VR video.
lesson: Betting heavily on cinematic VR video content assumed consumer headset adoption that never materialized at the needed scale.
source: Road to VR and TechCrunch reporting on Jaunt wind-down, 2019-2020

name: Jawbone
summary: Wearable maker Jawbone (UP trackers) raised nearly $1B but collapsed under manufacturing defects, patent battles with Fitbit, and cash burn, liquidating in 2017.
lesson: Massive capital raised cannot compensate for recurring hardware quality failures and losing ground to a faster competitor.

name: Jenny Craig
funding: Private equity owned (North Castle Partners, later H.I.G. Capital)
summary: Weight-loss company Jenny Craig abruptly ceased U.S. operations in 2023 after being outcompeted by GLP-1 weight-loss drugs and digital alternatives.
lesson: An in-person, appointment-based weight-loss model became obsolete once a more effective medical alternative reached mass adoption.

name: Jet Airways
summary: Once India's largest private airline grounded its entire fleet under $1.2B of debt; a 2021 revival attempt also failed.
lesson: Heavily leveraged airline expansion without disciplined cost control eventually catches up with even market leaders.

name: Jet.com
summary: Membership-style ecommerce marketplace built to compete with Amazon was acquired by Walmart for $3.3B in 2016, then shut down in 2020 as Walmart consolidated its ecommerce efforts under its own brand.
lesson: Even a well-funded challenger can be absorbed and discarded once its acquirer no longer needs the separate brand.

name: Jetty
summary: Renters insurance and security-deposit-alternative startup wound down its core consumer insurance business in 2023 and sold off parts of its operations after failing to reach sustainable unit economics.
lesson: Bundling insurance with adjacent fintech products like deposit alternatives adds complexity without automatically adding profitability.

name: Jezebel
summary: A prominent feminist pop-culture site descended from Gawker Media was shut down by G/O Media in 2023 citing declining traffic and revenue, before being revived by Paste Magazine in 2024.
lesson: A recognizable, influential media brand can still be discarded by a cost-cutting owner if its standalone numbers don't clear the bar.

name: Jibo
summary: Social home robot billed as 'the world's first family robot' shipped late in a market Amazon Echo and Google Home had already claimed with cheaper voice assistants.
lesson: A charming but expensive robot can be leapfrogged by cheap smart speakers solving the same core need.

name: Joyent
summary: Early cloud infrastructure pioneer that was acquired by Samsung in 2016 in a deal seen as a fire sale after failing to compete with AWS.
lesson: Being early to cloud infrastructure wasn't enough once AWS's economies of scale became insurmountable for smaller IaaS providers.

name: Juicero
summary: Made a $400+ wifi-connected juicer that pressed proprietary produce packs; became a punchline when reporters showed the packs could be squeezed by hand just as fast.
lesson: Don't over-engineer a solution consumers can replicate with their bare hands.

name: Julep
summary: Beauty subscription and D2C nail/cosmetics brand ceased operations in 2018 after its assets were sold following a failed pivot to social selling.
lesson: Pivoting a subscription brand's business model late in its life rarely reverses declining fundamentals.

name: Julep Beauty
summary: Seattle beauty subscription brand was acquired by Glansaol in 2016; after Glansaol's Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2018, new owner AS Beauty abruptly discontinued Julep's Maven subscription box in February 2019.
lesson: Being folded into an overleveraged multi-brand roll-up can doom an otherwise viable brand when the parent collapses.
source: GeekWire, BeautyMatter coverage of Julep Maven shutdown 2019

name: Jumio
summary: Early identity-verification pioneer that collapsed into insolvency in 2016 after its CEO was fired for expense-account fraud; assets were later acquired out of bankruptcy and relaunched.
lesson: Founder-level financial misconduct can destroy a well-funded, technically sound company almost overnight, regardless of product quality.

name: Jump Bikes
summary: Electric bike-share startup (originally Social Bicycles) acquired by Uber in 2018; Uber shut down and sold off Jump's operations in 2020 amid COVID-driven cost cuts.
lesson: A promising acquisition can still be unwound when the parent company's own priorities shift.

name: Juno Finance
summary: Crypto-friendly neobanking app underwent mass layoffs and scaled back sharply after the 2022 crypto market crash eroded its core user base and revenue.
lesson: Banking products built around a single volatile asset class inherit that asset class's boom-bust cycles.

name: Juno Therapeutics
summary: CAR-T therapy JCAR015 caused five patient deaths from cerebral edema, forcing an FDA clinical hold before the company was acquired by Celgene in 2018.
lesson: Fatal neurotoxicity in a CAR-T trial can end a flagship program even amid an otherwise hot cell-therapy market.

name: Just Energy Group
summary: Retail energy and natural gas marketer Just Energy filed for creditor protection in Canada and Chapter 15 in the US in 2021 after also being hit hard by Winter Storm Uri's extreme price spikes in Texas.
lesson: Retail energy resellers carry hidden tail risk from extreme wholesale price events that can wipe out years of accumulated margin.

name: Kabbage
summary: SMB lending fintech whose core platform was acquired by American Express in 2020; the remaining legal entity (K Servicing) filed for bankruptcy in 2022 facing lawsuits over fraudulent PPP loans it had originated.
lesson: Loosening underwriting standards to move fast during a government relief program created liability that outlived the acquired business itself.

name: Kahuna
summary: Mobile marketing automation startup Kahuna raised tens of millions but was quietly acquired for its technology around 2021 after failing to achieve independent scale in the crowded martech space.
lesson: A crowded marketing-automation category punishes vendors without a clearly differentiated wedge.

name: Kalera
summary: Publicly traded vertical farming company Kalera filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in April 2023 and later sold its assets to Sunnyside Fresh.
lesson: Rapid multi-facility expansion without operational profitability accelerates cash burn to insolvency.

name: Kapost
summary: Content marketing platform Kapost struggled to differentiate in a crowded market and was effectively wound down, with its technology and customers folded into Uberflip around 2019.
lesson: Content marketing platforms without a defensible niche get absorbed by consolidating competitors.

name: Karhoo
summary: London-based ride-hailing marketplace aggregating taxis and private-hire cars that collapsed into administration in 2017 just months after a high-profile US launch.
lesson: Aggressive multi-country expansion funded by debt-like structures can implode quickly when investor confidence wavers.

name: Karmaloop
summary: Streetwear e-commerce pioneer filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in March 2015 after founder Greg Selkoe's side ventures drained cash and vendor payments fell months behind; sold to Shiekh Shoes in 2016.
lesson: Diverting core-business cash into unrelated side ventures starves the flagship company of the capital it needs to survive.
source: Boston Globe, Boston Magazine coverage of Karmaloop bankruptcy 2015

name: Katapult
summary: Rent-to-own e-commerce financing platform that went public via SPAC in 2021 at a ~$1.1B valuation; stock collapsed over 95% amid weak credit quality and was later delisted from Nasdaq.
lesson: SPAC valuations built on growth projections can mask fundamentally weak lending unit economics.

name: Katerra
summary: SoftBank-backed construction technology company tried to vertically integrate design, manufacturing, and building, but collapsed into bankruptcy.
lesson: Vertically integrating a capital-intensive industrial business too fast, funded by growth-stage capital, can outrun operational competence.

name: Kayako
summary: Long-running helpdesk software vendor Kayako suffered years of financial distress and mass layoffs before being acquired in a distressed sale by ReadMe in 2023.
lesson: Early movers in a category can still be starved of resources and out-innovated by better-funded, faster-iterating rivals over a long enough timeline.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Kayako's 2023 acquisition by ReadMe

name: Keecker
summary: French startup building a mobile home-projector robot ceased operations in 2020 after failing to reach profitable scale in the crowded smart-home category.
lesson: Novel multi-function home robots face a steep uphill climb proving a must-have use case over cheaper single-purpose devices.
source: French tech press (Les Echos/Maddyness) coverage of Keecker closure

name: Keen IO
summary: Event analytics API startup that shut down its service in 2021 after failing to reach sustainable scale against larger analytics platforms.
lesson: Developer-analytics infrastructure is a crowded market where undifferentiated APIs struggle to retain customers long-term.

name: Kespry
summary: Drone data analytics company serving construction, mining, and insurance industries that shut down in 2022, selling off its technology assets.
lesson: Hardware-plus-software drone startups face brutal margin compression as commodity drone hardware becomes cheap and analytics alone struggle to fund the business.
source: TechCrunch, Commercial UAV News coverage of Kespry shutdown

name: Kinvey
summary: Mobile backend-as-a-service platform acquired by Progress Software in 2017; Progress discontinued Kinvey in 2022 as part of a product portfolio rationalization.
lesson: Even a successful acquisition exit can end in product discontinuation years later if the category consolidates around the acquirer's core business.

name: KiOR
summary: Biofuel company that promised to turn wood chips into crude oil substitute but filed for bankruptcy after its Mississippi plant never hit production targets.
lesson: Overpromising production yields to investors and regulators eventually collides with physical and chemical reality.

name: Kissmetrics
summary: Customer analytics platform that struggled to differentiate against Mixpanel and Amplitude and shut down its product in 2018.
lesson: Analytics tools need a durable technical or distribution edge to survive well-funded direct competitors.

name: Kistler Aerospace
summary: Reusable launch vehicle developer (K-1 rocket) filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2003 after failing to complete its vehicle despite over a decade of development and a later NASA COTS award it could not fulfill.
lesson: Reusable launch hardware is a capital sink that punishes companies for slipping schedules by a decade rather than a year.

name: Kite
summary: AI-powered code completion tool for Python and JavaScript that shut down after concluding it could not build a sustainable business, just before the LLM-based coding assistant wave took off.
lesson: Being just ahead of the technology curve can be as fatal as being behind it if the underlying models aren't yet good enough.

name: Kittyhawk.io
summary: Drone fleet-management and airspace-compliance software company, used across construction and other industrial sectors, that shut down in 2021 after running out of funding.
lesson: Enterprise drone-operations software needs enough differentiated value to avoid being commoditized by larger platform players.
source: Commercial UAV News coverage of Kittyhawk.io shutdown

name: Klout
summary: Klout, which scored social media influence, was acquired by Lithium Technologies in 2014 and ultimately shut down in 2018 after failing to build a sustainable business model around its scores.
lesson: A viral engagement metric doesn't automatically translate into a durable monetizable product.

name: Knewton
summary: Adaptive-learning pioneer raised huge venture rounds on the promise of AI-personalized courseware but never found a durable business model, and sold its assets to Wiley.
lesson: Being early with impressive AI technology doesn't guarantee a viable go-to-market or revenue model.

name: Knightscope
summary: Public security-robot maker (K5) has never achieved profitability, relies on recurring equity raises to fund operations, and has faced persistent going-concern warnings despite years of deployments.
lesson: Going public does not solve a fundamentally unprofitable rental-hardware business model.
source: Knightscope SEC filings and going-concern disclosures

name: Knip
summary: Swiss insurtech offering a digital policy management app that let users track and switch personal insurance shut down its consumer product in 2018 after failing to build a durable brokerage revenue stream.
lesson: Helping consumers manage existing policies is a weaker business model than actually underwriting or brokering new ones.

name: Knotel
summary: Flexible office space provider positioned as an enterprise-focused WeWork alternative filed for Chapter 11 after the pandemic gutted office demand.
lesson: A lease-arbitrage business model is acutely vulnerable to sudden demand shocks like a pandemic.

name: Kobo360
summary: Nigerian trucking and logistics marketplace connecting shippers with truckers that underwent major restructuring and staff cuts around 2022-2023 amid funding shortages across African logistics-tech.
lesson: Emerging-market logistics marketplaces are especially exposed when global venture funding for the sector tightens.

name: Koding
summary: Cloud-based development environment and collaborative IDE that raised significant funding but never found a scalable business model before shutting down.
lesson: A slick collaborative dev-environment product needs enterprise workflow fit, not just individual developer delight, to sustain funding rounds.

name: Konarka Technologies
summary: Konarka developed flexible organic photovoltaic solar film but shut down and liquidated in 2012 after failing to achieve commercially viable efficiency and cost at scale.
lesson: Breakthrough materials science demos don't guarantee a manufacturable, cost-competitive product.

name: Kozmo.com
summary: Promised one-hour delivery of small goods in major cities and shut down in 2001 after the free delivery model proved impossibly expensive.
lesson: Free ultra-fast delivery for low-value orders destroys margins regardless of funding size.

name: Kymeta Corporation
summary: Flat-panel satellite antenna maker, once valued highly on Bill Gates-backed funding, underwent major layoffs and restructuring in 2023-2024 after failing to reach profitable volume manufacturing amid slower-than-expected market adoption.
lesson: Hardware companies that raise at high valuations on a novel antenna technology still need a manufacturing cost curve that reaches profitable unit economics.

name: Lacework
summary: Cloud security posture management (CNAPP) unicorn valued at $8.3B in 2021 was sold to Fortinet in 2024 for an estimated $200-230M, a roughly 97% collapse in valuation that wiped out venture returns.
lesson: A massive up-round valuation during a hype cycle can set a company up for a devastating down-round once growth expectations aren't met.

name: Lantern
summary: An end-of-life and estate planning platform for families dealing with aging or dying relatives; acquired by caregiving concierge Wellthy in 2023 and fully decommissioned as a standalone product in September 2024.
lesson: Niche categories like death and estate planning often can't sustain a standalone venture-scale company and end up as a feature folded into a broader platform.

name: LastPass
summary: Popular password manager that suffered a series of breaches in 2022 exposing encrypted vault data, causing a mass exodus of users and lasting reputational damage.
lesson: For a security product, a single serious breach can permanently erode the trust the entire business model depends on.

name: Laybuy
summary: New Zealand buy-now-pay-later provider was delisted from the London Stock Exchange and wound down its consumer operations in 2023 after losing market share to larger BNPL rivals.
lesson: Smaller BNPL entrants struggle to compete once dominant players like Klarna and Afterpay lock in merchant partnerships.

name: LDK Solar
funding: Publicly traded (NYSE: LDK), billions in debt
summary: Chinese silicon wafer maker LDK Solar filed for bankruptcy protection in 2014 after accumulating massive debt during the solar oversupply crisis of the early 2010s.
lesson: Debt-financed capacity expansion timed at the top of a commodity cycle leaves no room to survive the downturn.

name: Le Tote
summary: Clothing rental subscription startup acquired the bankrupt Lord & Taylor in 2019, then itself filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in August 2020 and shut down Lord & Taylor entirely, unable to sustain either business through the pandemic.
lesson: Acquiring a struggling legacy retailer to gain scale can drag down an otherwise-promising subscription model rather than strengthen it.
source: Retail Dive, CNBC coverage of Le Tote bankruptcy 2020

name: Leap Motion
funding: $94M raised across venture rounds; sold for a fraction of $200M+ prior valuation
summary: Leap Motion, a pioneer of hand-tracking sensors for VR/AR, was sold to UltraHaptics (later renamed Ultraleap) in 2019 in a fire-sale deal after burning through significant funding without reaching profitability.
lesson: Pioneering an input technology ahead of hardware demand can leave a company financially exhausted before the market matures enough to monetize it.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Leap Motion/UltraHaptics acquisition, 2019

name: Leap Transit
summary: San Francisco luxury commuter bus startup shut down by regulators in 2015 for operating without proper permits, less than a year after launch.
lesson: Skipping local transit permitting requirements to move fast can end a company almost overnight.

name: LearnVest
summary: Financial planning startup acquired by Northwestern Mutual for roughly $250M in 2015 was shut down three years later when the insurer discontinued the standalone product.
lesson: Strategic acquirers can sunset even well-funded fintech products when they don't fit the parent company's core distribution.

name: LendUp
summary: Online lender aimed at underbanked consumers that was repeatedly fined by the CFPB for deceptive practices and illegal fees, and shut down its lending operations in 2022.
lesson: Serving underbanked customers profitably requires compliance built in from day one, not bolted on after regulators intervene.

name: Leocare
summary: French insurtech offering app-based home and auto insurance was placed into receivership in 2023 after running out of cash amid a broader European insurtech funding pullback.
lesson: Multi-line insurance startups need enough runway to absorb underwriting volatility while still building brand trust.

name: LeoSat Enterprises
summary: Planned high-throughput laser-linked satellite constellation for enterprise data backhaul shut down in late 2019 after failing to secure the roughly $100M needed to move past the planning phase.
lesson: A constellation business plan requiring billions in eventual capital must first prove it can raise the much smaller amount needed to de-risk the design.

name: Letgo
summary: Secondhand goods marketplace app raised nearly $1B before merging with rival OfferUp in 2020, and the letgo brand was ultimately discontinued in 2022.
lesson: Two well-funded rivals fighting for the same local marketplace niche often end in a costly consolidation rather than a standalone win.

name: Level Money
summary: Personal finance and spending forecast app was acquired by Capital One in 2015 and shut down in 2018 as the bank redirected resources to its own apps.
lesson: Acqui-hires by large banks often prioritize the team over the product, and standalone apps get sunset.

name: LifePod Solutions
summary: A Boston voice-first proactive care assistant for aging-in-place seniors that used smart speakers for check-ins, reminders and emergency alerts; the company went dark after a single funding round and is no longer active.
lesson: Voice hardware for seniors requires continuous investment in caregiver trust and household adoption that a single funding round rarely covers.

name: Lightning eMotors
summary: Commercial EV and charging infrastructure company Lightning eMotors went public via SPAC in 2021 but filed for bankruptcy in 2024 after failing to scale production profitably in a slower-than-expected commercial EV market.
lesson: Small-scale commercial EV converters face brutal capital requirements that a SPAC raise alone can't guarantee will be sufficient.

name: LightSquared
summary: Attempted to build a hybrid satellite-terrestrial LTE network but the FCC blocked deployment after tests showed severe interference with GPS receivers, forcing Chapter 11.
lesson: Spectrum plans that interfere with critical safety infrastructure like GPS will be blocked no matter how much capital has already been spent.

name: Lily Robotics
summary: Promised a throw-and-shoot autonomous camera drone using promo footage shot with a different prototype; shut down amid an SEC/DOJ fraud investigation before ever shipping.
lesson: Faking demo footage to sell preorders is fraud, and it eventually catches up with founders.

name: Limeade
summary: Corporate wellness platform Limeade went public on the Australian exchange in 2019 but its valuation collapsed, leading to its acquisition by WebMD Health Services in 2023 at a steep discount.
lesson: An enterprise wellness SaaS company can still fail to reach profitability even with recurring corporate contracts if growth costs outpace revenue.

name: Limewire
summary: Popular peer-to-peer file-sharing client for music shut down by a federal court injunction after the RIAA won a copyright infringement lawsuit.
lesson: Facilitating widespread copyright infringement invites legal action that can end a business overnight.
source: Arista Records LLC v. Lime Group court records

name: Lion Electric
summary: Canadian electric school bus and truck maker that filed for bankruptcy protection after demand fell short of production capacity and cash reserves ran low.
lesson: Building manufacturing capacity ahead of firm, funded customer orders is a dangerous bet in commercial EVs.

name: LittleThings
summary: A lifestyle and feel-good content site that built enormous traffic through Facebook shut down abruptly in 2018 after a Facebook News Feed algorithm change collapsed its referral traffic overnight.
lesson: Building a media business almost entirely on a single platform's referral traffic leaves no cushion when that platform deprioritizes publishers.

name: Livemocha
summary: Early social-network-style language-learning pioneer that was acquired for parts by Rosetta Stone in 2013 and fully discontinued a few years later.
lesson: Being first to a category doesn't protect against faster-moving mobile-native competitors like Duolingo.

name: LivePerson
summary: Pioneer live-chat and conversational AI company LivePerson saw its stock collapse over 95% from its peak amid a founder-conduct scandal, executive departures, and going-concern doubts starting in 2023.
lesson: Even a category pioneer with decades of revenue can implode when governance failures and leadership scandal destroy market trust.
source: Wall Street Journal investigative reporting on LivePerson's 2023 governance crisis

name: Living Social
summary: Daily deals marketplace once valued near $6B raised nearly $1B before being sold to rival Groupon for a nominal $1 in 2016.
lesson: Deals marketplaces without repeat-purchase loyalty burn through capital chasing an ever-churning customer base.

name: Living.com
summary: Online furniture retailer shut down in 2000 after failing to solve the logistics and returns costs of shipping bulky furniture.
lesson: Category economics for bulky, high-return goods make naive ecommerce models unprofitable.

name: Livingly Media
