summary: A women's lifestyle content network (LoveToKnow, Lonny) built through SEO-optimized articles across a portfolio of niche sites was gradually wound down and sold off in pieces as programmatic-ad rates declined.
lesson: A content-farm-style portfolio strategy dependent on programmatic display advertising is highly exposed to declining CPM rates over time.

name: Local Bounti
summary: Publicly traded controlled-environment agriculture company Local Bounti, which went public via SPAC in 2022, underwent a severe debt restructuring in 2024 to avoid bankruptcy after mounting losses.
lesson: SPAC-funded agtech companies still face the same fundamental margin pressures as their privately held peers.

name: Local Motors
summary: 3D-printed and co-created vehicle manufacturer Local Motors, known for the Olli autonomous shuttle, abruptly ceased operations in 2022 after running out of cash.
lesson: Novel manufacturing approaches don't reduce the fundamental capital intensity of building and certifying vehicles.

name: Loggly
summary: Early cloud log management pioneer acquired by SolarWinds in 2016 at a valuation well below its funding, as Splunk and Datadog captured the larger enterprise observability market.
lesson: Being first to a category doesn't matter if better-funded, broader-platform competitors out-execute on enterprise sales.

name: LookingGlass Cyber Solutions
summary: Threat-intelligence platform vendor that raised over $100M was acquired by ZeroFox in 2023 for undisclosed terms after struggling to achieve the growth trajectory its funding had implied.
lesson: Large cumulative venture funding raises the bar for what counts as a successful exit; a modest acquisition after $100M+ raised is effectively a loss for investors.

name: Loot
summary: UK neobank targeting students and young people collapsed into administration in 2019 after running out of funding and failing to secure a rescue deal.
lesson: Targeting a low-revenue demographic like students makes it especially hard to reach profitability before funding runs out.

name: Loot Crate
summary: The gaming and geek-culture subscription box pioneer expanded too fast, mismanaged inventory and vendor payments, and filed for bankruptcy in 2019.
lesson: Subscription-box logistics and cash-flow management can sink a fast-growing consumer brand as easily as a lack of demand.

name: Lordstown Motors
summary: Electric pickup truck maker Lordstown Motors filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after a fraud investigation into overstated pre-orders and a collapsed partnership with Foxconn.
lesson: Overstating demand signals like pre-orders to public markets invites regulatory scrutiny that can destroy a company's credibility and financing.
source: Reuters, June 2023, Lordstown Motors Chapter 11 filing

name: Lori Systems
summary: Kenyan digital freight and trucking marketplace for East African supply chains that scaled back drastically and effectively ceased core operations in 2023 amid a funding crunch.
lesson: Digitizing fragmented trucking markets is capital-intensive and highly sensitive to macro funding cycles.
source: African tech press, Lori Systems retrenchment 2023

name: Loyal3
summary: Commission-free stock purchase platform that partnered directly with public companies shut down as brokerages moved to zero-commission trading industry-wide.
lesson: A commission-free niche disappears fast once the entire industry adopts the same pricing.

name: LucasArts
summary: Disney shut down the storied studio after acquiring Lucasfilm, cancelling Star Wars 1313 and shifting to a licensing-only model.
lesson: An acquisition can end a beloved studio overnight when the buyer's strategy doesn't include internal development.

name: Luko
summary: French home insurance unicorn known for fast digital claims was sold in a distressed acquisition after mounting underwriting losses and a stalled fundraising process left it unable to continue independently.
lesson: A slick claims experience can win customers while still losing money on every policy underwritten.

name: Luminary
summary: Podcast subscription platform that spent heavily on exclusive celebrity content but failed to build a large paying subscriber base, eventually laying off most staff and selling its show catalog.
lesson: Paying premium prices for exclusive content does not guarantee subscriber conversion in a market full of free alternatives.

name: Luminosity Gaming
funding: Privately funded, backed by OverActive Media
summary: Facing financial strain, the Canadian esports org sold its prized CS:GO roster to 100 Thieves in 2020 to raise cash and significantly downsized its remaining operations.
lesson: Selling off a marquee roster for liquidity is often a sign an esports org's broader business model isn't self-sustaining.

name: Luxe
summary: On-demand valet parking startup operating in several major US cities that shut down its consumer parking service in 2017, with Volvo licensing its technology afterward.
lesson: On-demand valet labor costs proved too high relative to what urban parkers were willing to pay.

name: Lytro
summary: Pioneered light-field cameras that let users refocus photos after the shot, but consumers didn't value the feature enough to justify the price; assets acquired by Google in 2018.
lesson: A technically brilliant camera innovation can still fail if it doesn't solve a problem consumers actually have.

name: Machine Zone
summary: The Game of War/Mobile Strike maker built a massive ad-spend machine but couldn't sustain user acquisition costs as growth slowed, shedding staff and selling off assets.
lesson: Growth fueled primarily by expensive paid user acquisition is fragile once acquisition costs rise or engagement dips.

name: Machinima
summary: The pioneering gaming and esports content network never found a sustainable ad-revenue model as YouTube's own creator ecosystem matured, and WarnerMedia shut it down in 2019.
lesson: A content network built on aggregating creators can be disintermediated once the underlying platform empowers those creators directly.

name: Machinima (media/blogging network)
summary: Originally a gaming-content blog and video network majority-owned by Warner Bros., it was shut down abruptly in 2019 with mass layoffs after failing to build a sustainable advertising model as YouTube's direct creator tools matured.
lesson: Intermediary content networks lose their reason to exist once the underlying platform gives creators direct monetization tools.

name: Magic Leap
summary: Raised the largest AR war chest ever for its Magic Leap One headset, then missed consumer sales targets by orders of magnitude and pivoted entirely to enterprise/defense.
lesson: Enormous funding and secrecy can't substitute for validating real consumer demand before shipping.

name: Mahalo.com
summary: Jason Calacanis' human-edited search and content site pivoted repeatedly (search, video courses, HowTo.com) after Google's Panda update devalued its SEO-driven content pages, eventually shutting down.
lesson: A content strategy optimized primarily for search ranking rather than reader value is fragile to search-algorithm changes.

name: Major League Gaming
summary: One of the earliest professional esports leagues, MLG burned through significant VC funding on live events and its GameBattles platform before selling its core assets to Activision Blizzard in 2016 for a fraction of the capital raised.
lesson: Being an early esports-league pioneer doesn't guarantee survival if broadcast and sponsorship revenue arrive years after your cash runs out.

name: MakeTime
summary: On-demand CNC manufacturing capacity marketplace that wound down independent operations after failing to reach the transaction density needed to match idle machine-shop capacity with buyer demand profitably.
lesson: Matching fragmented, illiquid industrial capacity with demand requires far higher transaction density than most manufacturing marketplaces achieve early on.
source: Manufacturing trade press coverage of MakeTime's 2019 wind-down

name: Managed by Q
summary: Office management and facilities-services marketplace acquired by WeWork; operations were wound down as WeWork retrenched during its own crisis.
lesson: An acquisition by a troubled parent company can doom an otherwise-functioning business.

name: MannKind Corporation
summary: Inhaled insulin product Afrezza faced multiple near-bankruptcies before winning approval, but adoption remained tiny for years against injectable insulin's entrenched market position.
lesson: A better delivery mechanism doesn't overcome physician and patient inertia around an established standard of care.

name: Manthan Systems
summary: India-founded retail analytics and merchandising-intelligence software company was acquired for a fraction of its funding by Aurea/ESW Capital in 2021 after struggling to convert its analytics platform into sustained profitable growth.
lesson: Analytics platforms that require lengthy, high-touch retailer implementations can burn through venture funding faster than they can prove renewal value.
source: Industry retail-tech and M&A press coverage, 2021

name: ManyPets (US)
summary: UK-founded pet insurer exited the US market and cut staff after underwriting losses from medical cost inflation made American expansion unsustainable.
lesson: International insurance expansion can outrun a carrier's ability to price risk accurately in a new market.

name: Maple
summary: NYC-based prepared meal delivery startup co-founded by a celebrity chef shut down in 2017, with its team acquired by Deliveroo.
lesson: A crowded meal-delivery market punishes startups that can't reach delivery density fast enough.

name: MapR
summary: Hadoop distribution vendor that raised hundreds of millions but lost ground to Cloudera/Hortonworks and cloud-native data platforms, eventually selling its assets to HPE in a fire sale.
lesson: Being one of three well-funded competitors chasing the same enterprise market left little room for anyone but the leader to survive.

name: Marble
summary: San Francisco sidewalk delivery-robot startup was acquired by Ford in 2018 primarily for its engineering talent, and its delivery-robot product was discontinued.
lesson: An acqui-hire outcome for engineering talent often means the original product vision is shelved entirely.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Ford's acquisition of Marble team, 2018

name: Marcus by Goldman Sachs
summary: Goldman Sachs' consumer banking venture racked up billions in losses on credit cards and personal loans, prompting the bank to retreat from mass-market consumer banking starting in 2023.
lesson: An institution without consumer banking DNA can underestimate the cost and risk of acquiring and servicing retail borrowers.

name: Marin Software
summary: Search and social ad management platform Marin Software went public in 2013 but saw its stock collapse over the following decade as it struggled to retain customers against Google and Facebook's own tools, repeatedly restructuring to avoid delisting.
lesson: Third-party ad-management layers are structurally at risk once the underlying ad platforms build competing native tools.
source: SEC filings, AdExchanger coverage of Marin Software decline

name: MaritzCX
funding: Formed from Maritz Research and Allegiance merger
summary: Customer experience management platform MaritzCX, itself a merger of legacy feedback vendors, was acquired by InMoment in 2018 after struggling to compete against Qualtrics and Medallia at scale.
lesson: Rolling up legacy market-research firms into a CX platform does not automatically produce the product velocity needed to compete with venture-scaled leaders.

name: MarketForce
summary: East African B2B retail distribution marketplace that collapsed in 2024, laying off nearly all staff after running out of funding amid a broader African tech funding downturn.
lesson: Retail distribution marketplaces in low-margin emerging markets are especially exposed when funding markets tighten.
source: African tech press (TechCrunch/Disrupt Africa) coverage of MarketForce's 2024 collapse

name: Massive Incorporated
summary: Massive Incorporated built an in-game advertising network and was acquired by Microsoft in 2006 for around $200M, but Microsoft shut the unit down in 2010 after the in-game ad market failed to scale as projected.
lesson: Emerging ad formats can be acquired at a premium before the underlying market has proven it can scale.
source: TechCrunch, Reuters coverage of Massive Inc shutdown 2010

name: Masten Space Systems
summary: Vertical-takeoff/vertical-landing rocket and lunar lander developer, a longtime NASA contractor, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in mid-2022 after cost overruns on its NASA CLPS lunar lander contract; assets were later acquired by Astrobotic.
lesson: Fixed-price government contracts can bankrupt a small contractor if technical costs run ahead of milestone payments.

name: MasterClass
summary: Celebrity-taught online course platform saw sharp subscriber growth during the pandemic reverse afterward, leading to layoffs and a roughly 75% internal valuation markdown.
lesson: Pandemic-boosted consumer subscription demand for enrichment content proved largely temporary.

name: Matches Fashion
funding: Acquired by Frasers Group for £52M in Dec 2023
summary: UK luxury fashion e-commerce retailer entered administration in March 2024, just three months after Frasers Group bought it, cutting over half its staff and fully ceasing operations by July 2024 after years of mounting losses.
lesson: A distressed acquisition target needs a credible turnaround plan and committed follow-on funding, not just a lower price, to survive.
source: Business of Fashion, Fashionista coverage of Matches Fashion administration 2024

name: Maven
summary: General Motors' car-sharing brand operating in over a dozen US cities was shut down in 2019 as GM redirected investment toward Cruise's autonomous vehicle efforts.
lesson: Corporate mobility experiments get cut quickly when a flashier bet (like autonomy) captures leadership's attention.

name: Mayfield Robotics
summary: Bosch-incubated startup behind the Kuri home robot discontinued the product in mid-2018 before wide shipment despite a successful CES 2017 launch and thousands of pre-orders.
lesson: Strong trade-show buzz does not always translate into a business case corporate backers will keep funding.

name: MediaMath
summary: MediaMath, an early independent demand-side platform, shut down operations in 2023 after running out of cash and failing to secure rescue financing or a sale.
lesson: Independent ad-tech infrastructure players face existential risk when scale economics favor a few dominant platforms.

name: Medium
summary: Evan Williams' blogging and publishing platform repeatedly pivoted its business model, from ad-supported to a metered paywall to a flat subscription, laying off staff multiple times while never matching its early venture-scale ambitions.
lesson: Repeatedly changing a publishing platform's core monetization model erodes trust with both the writers and readers it depends on.

name: MedMen
summary: Cannabis retail chain that branded itself as the 'Apple Store of weed' expanded aggressively across multiple states, then saw its stock collapse over 99% and default on debt.
lesson: Aggressive multi-state retail expansion can collapse fast when unit economics never catch up to store count.

name: Meerkat
summary: The live video-streaming app rode SXSW buzz but was crushed within weeks after Twitter cut off its social-graph access and launched rival Periscope.
lesson: Building a growth engine on top of a rival platform's API leaves you exposed to a single policy change.

name: Melinta Therapeutics
summary: Antibiotic maker with four approved drugs filed Chapter 11 in 2020 after revenue failed to cover debt and operating costs, the second antibiotic bankruptcy in a year.
lesson: Multiple approved antibiotics still couldn't overcome the structurally poor economics of the antibacterial market.

name: Meroxa
summary: Real-time data integration and change-data-capture platform that shut down in 2024 after failing to reach sustainable growth.
lesson: Real-time data infrastructure requires deep enterprise trust and long sales cycles that can outlast available runway.

name: Meta Company
summary: Meta Company (unrelated to Facebook's later rebrand), founded by Meron Gribetz, built AR glasses for spatial computing but filed for bankruptcy in 2019 after failing to achieve commercial scale.
lesson: Ambitious spatial-computing AR hardware startups faced the same capital-intensity and long development timelines that sank several peers in the same period.
source: TechCrunch reporting on Meta Company bankruptcy, 2019

name: MetalSite
summary: Steel-industry B2B trading exchange backed by large steel producers that shut down after failing to attract enough independent buyer volume beyond its founding sponsors.
lesson: A marketplace funded and dominated by incumbent suppliers rarely earns the independent buyer trust needed for real liquidity.
source: Industry trade press coverage of MetalSite closure (2001)

name: Metanautix
summary: Big-data query and integration startup acquired by Microsoft in 2016; its technology was folded into Microsoft's data platform and not sold as a standalone product.
lesson: An acqui-hire can be a positive outcome for founders while still ending the product as an independent entity.

name: Metromile
summary: Pay-per-mile auto insurance telematics startup Metromile was acquired by Lemonade in 2022 at a steep valuation discount after years of underwriting losses and a crashed stock price.
lesson: Usage-based insurance requires actuarial discipline; growth without underwriting profitability leads to distressed sale outcomes.
source: Lemonade press release, July 2022, Metromile acquisition

name: MFG.com
summary: Early online manufacturing sourcing marketplace connecting buyers with contract manufacturers that faded from prominence over the 2010s as newer, better-funded manufacturing marketplaces like Xometry overtook it.
lesson: Being first to a marketplace category provides no lasting advantage if the product experience stalls while competitors modernize.
source: Manufacturing sourcing industry trade press coverage of MFG.com's decline

name: Miasolé
summary: Thin-film solar panel maker that was sold at a steep discount to a Chinese conglomerate after burning through hundreds of millions without reaching profitability.
lesson: Being among the most heavily funded startups in a sector is no guarantee against a distressed fire-sale exit.

name: Mic
summary: A millennial-focused news site chased Facebook video pivots, laid off most editorial staff in 2017 after a traffic collapse, and was sold for parts in 2019.
lesson: Rebuilding an entire editorial strategy around a single platform's algorithm leaves no fallback when that algorithm changes.

name: Microsoft HoloLens
funding: Corporate-funded by Microsoft, including a $22B U.S. Army IVAS contract
summary: Microsoft's HoloLens mixed-reality headset line was effectively discontinued in 2024 after Microsoft scaled back HoloLens 2 production, cut the team, and the U.S. Army IVAS contract faced repeated soldier complaints and delays.
lesson: Even a well-capitalized incumbent can abandon an AR hardware line after years of investment if it fails to find a durable enterprise or consumer market beyond a single large government contract.
source: Business Insider/The Verge reporting on HoloLens discontinuation, 2024

name: Midway Games
summary: The Mortal Kombat publisher accumulated heavy debt and underperforming titles, filing Chapter 11 and selling assets piecemeal.
lesson: Carrying too much corporate debt while release quality declines is a slow-motion bankruptcy.

name: Mighty Buildings
summary: 3D-printed prefab home manufacturer that raised substantial venture funding but underwent repeated mass layoffs and scaled back operations sharply in 2023-2024.
lesson: Novel construction manufacturing technology (3D printing) still must clear the same unit-economics bar as traditional homebuilding to reach scale.
source: TechCrunch coverage of Mighty Buildings layoffs

name: Millennial Media
summary: Millennial Media, a mobile ad network that IPO'd in 2012, saw its valuation crater amid competition from Facebook and Google and was acquired by AOL in 2015 for about $238M, far below its peak market cap.
lesson: Ad networks without proprietary inventory or data are vulnerable when platform giants enter the same market.
source: TechCrunch, Fortune coverage of AOL-Millennial Media acquisition 2015

name: Mindshare Technologies
summary: Customer experience feedback and analytics vendor Mindshare Technologies was acquired by Service Management Group (SMG) in 2018, folding its product line into SMG's broader CX platform.
lesson: Mid-market CX feedback vendors are frequent consolidation targets as larger CX measurement platforms roll up market share.
source: SMG 2018 acquisition announcement for Mindshare Technologies

name: Mint
summary: Pioneering personal finance and budgeting app, acquired by Intuit for $170M in 2009, was shut down in 2024 with users pushed to migrate to Credit Karma.
lesson: Even category-defining products get sunset when a parent company decides consolidation serves its strategy better.

name: Mirror (fitness)
summary: Home-fitness mirror startup Mirror was acquired by Lululemon for $500M in 2020, but Lululemon discontinued the hardware and took a writedown in 2023 as demand collapsed.
lesson: An acquirer overpaying for a connected-fitness hardware trend can be forced into a costly reversal once category demand fades.

name: Misfit
summary: Made stylish fitness trackers and was acquired by Fossil Group in 2015, but Fossil shut down the Misfit wearables brand in 2018.
lesson: A successful design-led exit doesn't guarantee the product survives inside a larger portfolio company.

name: Mission Motors
summary: Electric motorcycle and powertrain maker Mission Motors shut down in 2015 after pivoting from consumer motorcycles to enterprise EV powertrains failed to generate sufficient revenue.
lesson: A late pivot from a struggling consumer product to enterprise sales rarely succeeds without a proven enterprise go-to-market motion.

name: Mixer
summary: Microsoft's live-streaming platform never closed the gap with Twitch despite exclusive deals with stars like Ninja, and was shut down in 2020.
lesson: Buying exclusive star creators can't overcome an entrenched platform's network effects and habit formation.

name: Mixin Network
summary: Cross-chain messaging and asset network suffered a $200M hack in September 2023 attributed to a cloud database compromise, severely damaging user trust and effectively ending the project's active usage.
lesson: Relying on centralized cloud infrastructure for custody of decentralized assets reintroduces the single point of failure blockchain aims to remove.

name: mOasis
summary: Soil-moisture-retaining polymer startup mOasis, which raised funding from prominent agtech investors, shut down around 2019 after its product failed to gain broad grower adoption.
lesson: Agronomic input products need extensive multi-season field validation before growers will pay a premium over established practices.

name: ModCloth
summary: Indie/vintage-inspired women's fashion e-commerce brand was acquired by Walmart in 2017, then sold at a steep loss to Go Global Retail in 2019 after failing to gain traction within Walmart's e-commerce portfolio.
lesson: A niche brand's distinct identity can get lost and its unit economics worsen when absorbed into a mass-market parent's operations.
source: TechCrunch, Retail Dive coverage of ModCloth sale to Go Global Retail 2019

name: Mode Media
summary: Formerly Glam Media, this lifestyle content and ad-tech network raised over $300M but shut down abruptly in 2016 after failing to find a buyer amid an unsustainable cash burn rate.
lesson: Raising hundreds of millions to build a content network doesn't guarantee an exit if the underlying ad-tech economics never worked.

name: Modern Age
summary: A longevity and healthy-aging clinic chain offering hormone therapy and wellness treatments for aging adults; it shut down in 2023 and was sold in a distressed deal after failing to reach sustainable unit economics.
lesson: Premium in-person longevity clinics face the same brutal real-estate and staffing cost structure as any medical practice, and novelty branding doesn't fix that math.

name: Modern Meadow
summary: Modern Meadow began as one of the first cultivated-meat startups before abandoning food entirely in 2019 to focus solely on lab-grown leather materials, effectively failing in the nutrition space it started in.
lesson: Cultivated-protein science that proves easier to commercialize in materials than in food signals a fundamental product-market mismatch for the original vision.

name: Modsy
summary: 3D interior design and furniture ecommerce startup shut down in 2022 after failing to convert design engagement into sustainable furniture sales margins.
lesson: Rich design experiences don't guarantee conversion rates high enough to cover furniture ecommerce costs.

name: Modulous
summary: UK-based modular building design software and platform company that entered financial administration in 2024 after failing to secure further funding.
lesson: Construction-tech platforms tied to a slowing housing development pipeline are highly vulnerable to funding-market downturns.
source: Construction News (UK) coverage of Modulous administration

name: MOG
summary: On-demand music streaming and internet radio service acquired by Beats Electronics in 2012 primarily for its technology, with its consumer service quickly discontinued.
lesson: Being acquired does not guarantee a product's survival if the acquirer values only the technology or team.

name: Mojio
summary: Connected-car platform Mojio, which powered carrier telematics programs, sold off assets and wound down in 2023 after carrier partners scaled back or ended their programs.
lesson: Over-reliance on a small number of large carrier partners for distribution creates existential risk if those contracts end.

name: Momentus Inc.
summary: In-space transportation (space tug) company went public via SPAC in 2021 but its founder was forced out over national-security concerns, and by 2024 it faced a Nasdaq delisting notice and severe cash shortfalls.
lesson: Founder background and regulatory scrutiny can unravel a SPAC-funded space company even after the deal closes and cash is in hand.

name: Monese
summary: UK-based neobank for underbanked and migrant customers underwent a severe down-round and distressed majority-stake sale around 2025 after years of losses and failed acquisition talks.
lesson: Serving an underbanked niche with low-margin customers requires reaching scale faster than most seed-stage neobanks manage.

name: Moov Inc.
summary: Wearable fitness coaching device maker Moov shut down in 2018 after failing to find a sustainable business model against larger wearable competitors.
lesson: A niche wearable feature (motion coaching) is hard to defend once major players add similar functionality.

name: Morphe (Forma Brands)
funding: Backed by Sagon Phior, funding undisclosed
summary: Influencer-driven cosmetics brand's parent Forma Brands filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in January 2023 after closing all US stores; sales had fallen from $500M (2020) to $295M (2021), and creditors took over via a $690M debt-for-equity deal.
lesson: Over-reliance on a small set of influencer partnerships (James Charles, Jeffree Star) for brand equity creates fragile demand when influencer reputations sour.
source: NBC News, Retail Dive, Cosmetics Business coverage of Forma Brands Chapter 11, 2023

name: Motif FoodWorks
summary: Motif FoodWorks developed plant-based protein and heme ingredients for food manufacturers as a spinout of Ginkgo Bioworks, but weakening demand for alt-protein ingredients led it to wind down and sell assets in 2024.
lesson: B2B ingredient suppliers to alt-protein are only as healthy as the consumer demand of the brands they supply.

name: Motif Investing
summary: Thematic investing platform that let users build baskets of stocks around ideas shut down and sold its technology and some assets to Charles Schwab.
lesson: Novel investing UX alone can't outrun zero-commission incumbents once trading fees go to zero industry-wide.

name: Moven
summary: One of the earliest US mobile banking apps pivoted from direct-to-consumer banking to licensing its technology to banks, and wound down after the pivot failed to gain traction.
lesson: Pivoting from a consumer product to enterprise licensing requires a fundamentally different sales motion that early consumer-focused teams often lack.

name: MoviePass
summary: A subscription service offering nearly unlimited movie theater tickets for a flat monthly fee; the company paid full price for every ticket redeemed, resulting in catastrophic losses.
lesson: A subscription price below your per-unit cost of goods sold is not a growth strategy, it's a countdown to insolvency.

name: Moxie Software
summary: Enterprise CRM and knowledge-management vendor Moxie Software (formerly Consona/Onyx CRM lineage) wound down after failing to compete with cloud-native CRM suites, with assets later absorbed by other vendors.
lesson: Legacy on-premise CRM vendors carrying private-equity debt struggled to fund the cloud transition fast enough to survive against SaaS-native competitors.
source: Industry trade press coverage of Moxie Software's decline

name: Msg.ai
summary: Enterprise chatbot and conversational AI platform Msg.ai (formerly Snaps' rival) wound down operations around 2019 after failing to secure follow-on funding.
lesson: First-generation enterprise chatbot vendors often underestimated how much NLP infrastructure and integration work was needed to satisfy brand customers.
source: Industry reporting and Crunchbase records on Msg.ai's wind-down

name: Mt. Gox
summary: Once handling over 70% of all bitcoin transactions, the Tokyo exchange collapsed after admitting roughly 850,000 BTC had been lost or stolen through years of undetected hacking.
lesson: Exchanges holding customer assets need auditable, professional-grade security from day one.

name: Multichain
summary: Cross-chain bridge protocol handling billions in TVL collapsed in mid-2023 after its CEO was arrested by Chinese authorities and hundreds of millions in user funds mysteriously moved out of its contracts.
lesson: Centralized control over supposedly decentralized bridge infrastructure creates a single point of catastrophic failure.
source: Multichain official statements and on-chain analysis, July 2023

name: Munchery
summary: On-demand prepared meal delivery startup shut down abruptly in 2019, leaving customers and vendors unpaid.
lesson: Food delivery logistics have thin margins that heavy funding cannot mask indefinitely.

name: Muve Music
summary: Mobile carrier-bundled unlimited music streaming service offered by Cricket Wireless that was discontinued in 2014 after Cricket's acquisition by AT&T.
lesson: Carrier-bundled media products are highly exposed to shifts in the parent telecom's ownership and strategic direction.

name: Muxtape
summary: Simple mixtape-sharing website that went viral quickly but was forced by the RIAA to shut down its music-hosting feature within a year of launch.
lesson: Viral growth without licensing agreements in place is a temporary state, not a strategy.

name: Myspace
summary: The dominant social network of the mid-2000s lost its lead to Facebook after clumsy redesigns, ad clutter, and management turmoil under News Corp ownership.
lesson: Acquisition by a media conglomerate can smother the product agility that made a startup win.

name: Namely
summary: HR and payroll software for mid-sized companies raised over $260M before being sold to Vensure Employer Services after struggling to grow into its valuation.
lesson: Raising at aggressive valuations can force an eventual down-market exit if growth doesn't keep pace.

name: Nanigans
summary: Facebook ad-buying platform Nanigans, once a fast-growing adtech firm, shut down in 2019 after losing customers to in-house tools and larger competitors.
lesson: Point-solution ad-buying tools are exposed when platform owners and holding companies build competing in-house capabilities.
source: AdExchanger, Digiday coverage of Nanigans shutdown 2019

name: Nanosolar
summary: Thin-film CIGS solar panel maker, once one of the most hyped cleantech startups, that quietly shut down after years of production delays and missed targets.
lesson: Heavy pre-revenue hype and funding cannot substitute for solving hard manufacturing yield problems.

name: Napster
summary: The pioneering peer-to-peer music-sharing service was shut down by court injunction in 2001-2002 after a landmark RIAA copyright infringement lawsuit.
lesson: A product built on unresolved legal liability can be shut down overnight regardless of user love.

name: Nasty Gal
summary: D2C vintage/fashion e-commerce brand that grew rapidly then filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in November 2016 amid inventory and management problems; assets were acquired by Boohoo Group in 2017.
lesson: Rapid top-line growth without inventory and operational discipline can mask an unsustainable cost structure.
source: Business of Fashion, TechCrunch coverage of Nasty Gal bankruptcy 2016-2017

name: Natero
summary: Customer success software startup Natero was acquired by Freshworks in 2019 and folded into Freshsuccess, discontinuing Natero as a standalone product.
lesson: Undersized customer-success vendors often end up as feature acquisitions rather than standalone winners once larger suites decide to compete directly.

name: National CineMedia
summary: The largest cinema advertising network in the US filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 due to a heavy debt burden and slower-than-expected post-COVID theater attendance recovery.
lesson: Even a market-leading position can't offset a debt structure built for pre-pandemic attendance levels.

name: NationSwell
summary: A media platform covering social innovation and civic solutions journalism wound down its consumer media operations around 2020, shifting fully to a private membership and consulting model.
lesson: Solutions-oriented journalism can struggle to build a large enough ad-supported audience, forcing a pivot away from public media entirely.

name: Neato Robotics
summary: Robot-vacuum maker was acquired by German appliance company Vorwerk in 2017, which discontinued the Neato brand entirely in 2023 after failing to keep pace with Roomba and Chinese rivals.
lesson: Being acquired by a strategic parent does not guarantee long-term survival if the parent later exits the category.
source: The Verge coverage of Neato Robotics brand discontinuation, 2023

name: Neighborhood Goods
summary: A 'department store of the future' startup that opened rotating-brand locations in Texas, New York, Austin and Newport Beach shut down all four stores in January 2024 after staff cuts and lease terminations, without filing for bankruptcy.
lesson: Novel retail-store concepts must prove unit economics at one location before signing additional high-rent leases.

name: Neoforma
summary: Healthcare-supply B2B marketplace that IPO'd at the peak of the dot-com bubble, saw its stock collapse over 99%, and was eventually acquired by GHX at a small fraction of its former value.
lesson: A marketplace built to IPO on hype rather than proven unit economics leaves shareholders exposed when sentiment turns.
source: SEC filings and GHX acquisition announcement (2006)

name: Neos
summary: UK smart-home insurer that used IoT sensors to prevent water and fire damage claims ceased underwriting new business in 2023 after its backers concluded the model could not scale profitably.
lesson: IoT-driven loss prevention can improve claims ratios but still needs enough scale to cover customer acquisition and hardware costs.

name: New Age Meats
summary: New Age Meats built cultivated pork sausage prototypes and ran early tastings but shut down in 2024 as investor appetite for pre-revenue cultivated-meat startups collapsed.
lesson: Cultivated meat startups without a clear regulatory approval and commercialization timeline struggle to raise follow-on capital.

name: New Wave Foods
summary: New Wave Foods developed plant-based shrimp alternatives made from algae and plant proteins but quietly ceased operations around 2022 amid weak retail traction and funding constraints.
lesson: Niche plant-based seafood alternatives face a much smaller addressable market than plant-based beef or chicken.

name: NewsCred
summary: A content-marketing platform that licensed news content and built enterprise content workflow tools sold its licensing business and shut down after burning through significant venture funding.
lesson: Pivoting from content licensing to enterprise SaaS marketing tools didn't resolve an underlying inability to reach profitability.

name: Newsvine
summary: An early citizen-journalism and social-news site was acquired by MSNBC, gradually deprioritized, and formally shut down in 2017.
lesson: Citizen-journalism platforms without a growth strategy were largely displaced once social networks absorbed that function.

name: Newsweek (IBT Media era)
funding: Sold for $1 in 2010, later relaunched under IBT Media
summary: After decades as a leading American newsweekly, Newsweek was sold by the Washington Post Company for $1 in 2010, ceased its print edition in 2012-2013, and its later owner IBT Media faced fraud investigations tied to Olivet University.
lesson: A storied legacy media brand can be run into serious financial and ethical trouble by owners chasing traffic and dubious ties over sustainable journalism.

name: Newsy
summary: A video-first news outlet aimed at cord-cutters was rebranded as Scripps News and effectively wound down as a distinct national news brand in 2023 amid Scripps' broader restructuring and cost-cutting.
lesson: An innovative video-news format still needs a sustainable distribution and revenue plan independent of a parent company's shifting strategic priorities.

name: Nexage
summary: Mobile ad exchange Nexage was acquired by AOL's Millennial Media in 2015 for around $107.6M, and its technology was subsequently absorbed and its independent brand discontinued as Millennial Media itself was later acquired.
lesson: Mobile ad exchanges without unique inventory access got consolidated into larger holding companies rather than scaling independently.
source: AdExchanger, Mobile Marketer coverage of AOL-Nexage acquisition 2015

name: NextVR
summary: NextVR, a VR live sports and events broadcasting company, was acquired by Apple in 2020 for a reported fraction of its prior valuation in what was widely viewed as an acquihire rather than a strategic win for investors.
lesson: Live VR broadcasting required continuous heavy production investment while paying audiences for VR sports content remained small.

name: Nikola Corporation
summary: Hydrogen and electric truck maker Nikola filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in February 2025 after founder Trevor Milton was convicted of fraud for exaggerating the company's technology.
lesson: Fabricating product demonstrations to inflate investor confidence eventually collapses the entire enterprise once exposed.
source: Reuters, February 2025, Nikola Corporation Chapter 11 filing

name: Nokia OZO
summary: Nokia's OZO, a professional 360-degree VR camera for cinematic content production, was discontinued in 2017 as part of Nokia's broader exit from the VR business after weak sales.
lesson: Even an established hardware manufacturer can misjudge demand for professional VR production tools when the downstream consumer VR content market fails to grow as projected.

name: Norse Corporation
summary: Threat-intelligence firm known for its live 'attack map' visualization imploded in early 2016 amid reports of a high cash-burn rate, an abrupt CEO ouster, and questions over the actual quality of its threat data.
lesson: Flashy demos and marketing cannot compensate for a threat-intelligence product that customers and analysts come to doubt is substantively accurate.

name: North (Focals)
summary: North, maker of the Focals smart glasses, was acquired by Google in 2020 in an acquihire deal that discontinued the Focals product line and shut down customer support shortly after.
lesson: Consumer smart glasses required both a mature display supply chain and mass consumer demand that had not yet arrived, despite heavy funding.
source: The Verge reporting on Google-North acquisition, 2020

name: North American Star League (NASL)
funding: Backed by parent company e-Sports Entertainment Association
summary: The StarCraft II and multi-title tournament league ceased operations in 2015 after failing to secure sustainable sponsorship and broadcast revenue.
lesson: Tournament leagues tied to a single dominant title are vulnerable when that game's competitive audience declines.

name: Northvolt
summary: Europe's flagship battery gigafactory startup filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection after production delays, quality issues, and a cash crunch despite billions raised.
lesson: Even massively funded gigafactory bets can fail if production ramp-up problems burn cash faster than customers can be delivered to.

name: NS8
summary: Fraud-prevention startup collapsed in September 2020 after founder/CEO Adam Rogas was charged by the SEC with fabricating customer revenue and bank statements to raise over $100M; he later pleaded guilty and was sentenced to five years in prison.
lesson: Fabricated metrics can inflate a funding round for a while, but fraud eventually collapses the company entirely once discovered.

name: Nuix
summary: The Australian e-discovery and forensic analytics company's 2020 IPO collapsed amid allegations of misleading investor forecasts, triggering a regulator investigation and executive departures.
lesson: Overpromising growth forecasts ahead of an IPO can trigger a governance crisis that outlasts the technology's actual value.

name: Nurx
summary: Direct-to-consumer telehealth startup for birth control and sexual health prescriptions was merged into Thirty Madison in 2022, with the standalone Nurx brand discontinued the following year after regulatory scrutiny over prescribing practices.
lesson: Consolidation is often the endpoint for DTC telehealth brands that can't sustain independent scale.

name: Oath Inc (Verizon Media)
funding: Verizon-owned, acquired AOL for $4.4B and Yahoo for $4.5B
summary: Verizon combined AOL and Yahoo into Oath/Verizon Media hoping to build an ad-tech and media rival to Google and Facebook, but wrote down billions in value and sold the unit to Apollo Global Management in 2021 for just $5B, far below the roughly $9B it had paid.
lesson: Bundling legacy media and ad-tech assets doesn't create a duopoly competitor without genuine platform advantages.
source: Wall Street Journal, CNBC coverage of Verizon Media sale to Apollo 2021

name: Oberlo
summary: Dropshipping tool that let Shopify merchants import products from AliExpress was acquired by Shopify in 2017 and discontinued in 2022 as Shopify shifted strategy toward other fulfillment partners.
lesson: Even category-defining tools can be discontinued once the parent platform's strategic priorities shift.

name: Ocean Hugger Foods
summary: Ocean Hugger Foods made tomato-based "Ahimi" tuna and eggplant-based "Unami" eel alternatives for sushi restaurants, but wound down operations in the early 2020s after failing to scale beyond niche foodservice accounts.
lesson: Plant-based seafood analogues aimed narrowly at foodservice lack the retail volume needed to reach sustainable scale.

name: Oculus Story Studio
summary: Facebook's in-house Oculus Story Studio, creators of award-winning VR shorts like Henry and Dear Angelica, was shut down in 2017 as Facebook redirected content investment toward interactive games over passive VR films.
lesson: Even a well-funded internal studio can be shut down when the parent company concludes the content format isn't driving hardware sales.
source: Facebook Oculus Story Studio closure announcement, 2017

name: Ofo
summary: Chinese dockless bike-share giant that expanded to 20+ countries but collapsed under debt, deposit-refund scandals, and cutthroat competition with Mobike, retreating from most markets by 2019.
lesson: Blitzscaling into dozens of countries simultaneously without a path to profitability invites collapse.

name: Ohi
summary: Distributed micro-fulfillment startup for D2C brands that shut down in 2023 after failing to reach sustainable unit economics in a soft e-commerce funding environment.
lesson: Asset-light distributed fulfillment networks still carry heavy fixed costs that punish thin margins.

name: Olive AI
summary: Healthcare AI automation startup once valued at $4B shut down and sold off business units after burning through nearly a billion dollars without delivering on its broad AI-automation promises.
lesson: Trying to automate too many disparate workflows at once can prevent an AI startup from proving value in any single one.

name: OlympusDAO
summary: DeFi 'reserve currency' protocol (OHM) known for its '(3,3)' staking meme saw its token collapse over 99% from late-2021 highs once the unsustainable staking APY model failed as new capital inflows slowed.
lesson: Tokenomics that depend on ever-increasing new buyer inflows to fund existing holder yields are structurally a Ponzi dynamic.
source: OlympusDAO token price history and DeFi post-mortem coverage, 2022

name: One Kings Lane
summary: Flash-sale home decor ecommerce site sold to Bed Bath & Beyond in a 2016 fire sale after raising over $200M, and its ecommerce operations were later wound down.
lesson: Flash-sale economics erode once novelty fades and customer acquisition costs rise.

name: Onerep
summary: Data-removal service that suffered a major reputational scandal in 2024 when it was revealed its founder had also created several of the people-search sites the service was meant to scrub users from.
lesson: A privacy service's credibility depends entirely on the integrity of its operators.

name: OneWeb
summary: LEO satellite broadband constellation builder filed Chapter 11 in March 2020 when a SoftBank-led funding round collapsed amid COVID-19 market turmoil; later rescued and merged into Eutelsat.
lesson: Capital-intensive satellite constellations need financing committed well before a downturn, not mid-buildout when investors can walk away.

name: OnLive
summary: A cloud-gaming pioneer years ahead of broadband infrastructure, OnLive burned through massive funding before selling its patents to Sony and shutting down.
lesson: Being technologically first doesn't matter if the underlying infrastructure isn't ready for mass adoption.

name: Onto
summary: UK all-inclusive EV subscription service Onto (formerly E-Car Club) collapsed into administration in 2023 after failing to raise a new funding round amid rising interest rates.
lesson: Subscription models built on fleet ownership are highly sensitive to financing costs and can fail fast when capital markets tighten.
source: Sky News, October 2023, Onto administration filing

name: Onvia
summary: B2G/B2B procurement marketplace whose stock cratered after the dot-com bust; survived for two decades as a smaller public company before being acquired by DemandStar at a fraction of its original valuation.
lesson: A marketplace can survive a bust and still never recover its original growth trajectory or valuation.

name: Onyx Software
summary: CRM vendor Onyx Software was acquired by Consona Corporation in 2010 after years of shrinking market share against Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics, and the product was eventually discontinued.
lesson: On-premise CRM vendors that were slow to move to cloud delivery lost ground rapidly once SaaS CRM alternatives matured.

name: Openbay
summary: Online auto repair marketplace Openbay, which connected drivers to repair shops for quotes, ceased consumer operations around 2019 after failing to reach scale against local competition.
lesson: Marketplace models in hyper-local, fragmented service categories need dense geographic liquidity that is expensive to build.
source: Auto repair industry trade press coverage of Openbay's 2019 wind-down

name: Openfit
summary: Digital fitness app Openfit, spun up by Beachbody to diversify beyond its MLM-style sales model, was shut down in 2023 as its parent company retrenched to its core BODi brand.
lesson: A parent company under financial pressure will cut newer diversification bets before addressing its core business problems.

name: Openpay
summary: Australian buy-now-pay-later company entered voluntary administration and was delisted in 2023 after failing to achieve profitability across its consumer and business lending books.
lesson: BNPL unit economics that depend on low default rates collapse quickly once credit conditions tighten.

name: Operator
summary: Conversational commerce startup Operator, founded by Robin Chan, shut down in 2018 after its human-plus-bot shopping concierge model failed to reach product-market fit.
lesson: Blending human agents with automation to reduce cost is easy to pitch but hard to make unit-economically sustainable at consumer scale.

name: OpTic Gaming (original entity)
summary: Financial disputes between founder Hector 'H3CZ' Rodriguez and investors led the original OpTic Gaming entity to cease operations in 2020, before the brand was relaunched under Immortals Gaming Club in 2021.
lesson: A beloved brand can survive its founding company's collapse only if a new owner is willing to relaunch it.

name: Osterhout Design Group (ODG)
summary: AR smart glasses pioneer ODG filed for bankruptcy in 2019 after failing to convert years of prototype development into sustainable enterprise or consumer sales; its patents were sold off.
lesson: Decades of hardware R&D without a clear path to commercial shipment can exhaust investor patience even with strong patent portfolios.

name: Otonomo
summary: Connected-car data marketplace Otonomo dissolved in 2024 after its SPAC-era valuation collapsed and it failed to find sustainable demand for aggregated vehicle data.
lesson: A data marketplace needs proven buyer demand at scale before building expensive data-aggregation infrastructure.

name: Otto
summary: Self-driving truck startup founded by ex-Google engineers, acquired by Uber in 2016; dissolved as a distinct unit by 2018 amid the Waymo trade-secrets lawsuit against Uber and its self-driving trucking pullback.
lesson: Acquiring technical talent under legal clouds can create massive downstream liability that ends the program entirely.

name: Outdoor Voices
summary: Athleisure D2C brand's founder Ty Haney was ousted as CEO in 2020 amid mounting losses (~$2M/month); the company later closed its stores and was acquired by Consortium Brand Partners in 2024 to avoid bankruptcy.
lesson: Public founder-board conflict combined with unchecked cash burn can force a distressed sale even for a beloved consumer brand.
source: Fortune, Inc., Retail Dive coverage of Outdoor Voices founder ouster and 2024 sale

name: Ouya
summary: The Android microconsole raised a then-record Kickstarter but shipped an underpowered device with a weak game library and was sold off to Razer in 2015.
lesson: Crowdfunding hype doesn't substitute for a compelling, differentiated hardware and software ecosystem.

name: Overwatch League
funding: Activision Blizzard-funded, ~$20M+ franchise fees per team
summary: Activision Blizzard's city-based franchised league charged teams tens of millions in entry fees but folded in 2023 after several team owners departed amid disputes over revenue sharing and unmet viewership projections.
lesson: Charging franchise owners large upfront fees based on projected media rights value is risky if that value never materializes.

name: own3d.tv
summary: The German esports livestreaming platform shut down in 2014, unable to compete with Twitch's scale and funding despite an early focus on the European esports scene.
lesson: A regional streaming platform needs either a defensible local moat or global scale to survive against a well-funded incumbent.

name: Ozmo
summary: AI-powered product support and troubleshooting platform Ozmo shut down in 2023 after failing to convert telecom and retail pilots into durable, profitable enterprise contracts.
lesson: AI support tools sold into telecom and retail enterprises face long sales cycles that can outlast a startup's runway before contracts scale.
source: Industry and Crunchbase reporting on Ozmo's 2023 shutdown

name: Ozy Media
summary: The digital media startup collapsed within weeks in 2021 after a New York Times investigation and a fraudulent investor conference call exposed inflated traffic and revenue claims.
lesson: Fabricating metrics to investors is an existential risk, not a growth hack.

name: PancakeBunny
summary: BSC-based yield-farming protocol was exploited via a flash loan attack in May 2021, crashing its BUNNY token by over 95% in a single day and effectively ending the project's relevance.
lesson: Yield protocols using token minting mechanisms tied to pool prices are vulnerable to flash-loan price manipulation.

name: Pantellos Group
summary: Utility-industry procurement marketplace consortium formed by major US utilities that was eventually shut down and its functions absorbed by other e-procurement vendors.
lesson: A marketplace owned collectively by its largest buyers has weak incentive to open the network to broader competition.
source: Utility industry trade press on Pantellos wind-down

name: Papa
summary: An on-demand companionship and errands marketplace pairing college students ('Papa Pals') with seniors, sold heavily through Medicare Advantage plans; a 2023 Bloomberg investigation into abuse allegations triggered mass contract cancellations from Humana, Aetna and other major payers.
lesson: When your entire revenue model depends on a handful of insurer contracts, safety and vetting failures can unwind years of growth almost overnight.

name: PaperExchange.com
summary: Paper-industry B2B trading exchange that wound down operations after failing to convert its user base of buyers and mills into a sustainable transaction fee business.
lesson: Listing and directory traffic is not the same as committed transaction volume in a marketplace business model.
source: Industry trade coverage of dot-com era paper marketplaces

name: Parature
summary: Customer service software provider Parature was acquired by Microsoft in 2014 for roughly $100M and folded into Dynamics 365, with the standalone product retired by 2018.
lesson: Point-solution helpdesk vendors acquired by platform giants are frequently absorbed and discontinued rather than scaled.

name: Parcelly
summary: UK click-and-collect parcel locker network startup that shut down in 2018 after struggling to build a large enough retail pickup-point network to be viable.
lesson: Network-effect logistics businesses need critical mass of physical pickup points before they become useful to consumers.

name: Pareteum Corporation
summary: Telecom connectivity software firm inflated revenue by over $40M from non-binding purchase orders; executives were later criminally charged and the company collapsed into Chapter 11.
lesson: Recognizing revenue on commitments customers never actually made is fraud, and it eventually destroys the company it was meant to prop up.

name: Parler
summary: The 'free speech' social network lost hosting and app-store access after the January 6, 2021 Capitol riot, and never recovered its user base.
lesson: Dependence on a handful of infrastructure and distribution gatekeepers is an existential single point of failure.

name: Parrot (consumer drones)
summary: French electronics company Parrot exited the consumer drone market around 2019-2020 after years of shrinking market share against DJI, laying off much of its drone division and pivoting to enterprise and defense drones.
lesson: A dominant incumbent can rapidly capture a hardware category, leaving even well-capitalized challengers no path to profitable share.
source: Reuters coverage of Parrot's consumer drone exit, 2019

name: Parse
summary: Popular mobile backend-as-a-service acquired by Facebook for roughly $85M in 2013, then shut down in 2017 despite serving hundreds of thousands of apps, as it no longer fit Facebook's strategic priorities.
lesson: Even a widely-used developer platform can be shut down by an acquirer if it doesn't align with the parent company's core business strategy.

name: Participant Media
summary: A social-impact film and TV studio (Spotlight, An Inconvenient Truth) ceased operations in 2023 after nearly two decades and dozens of Oscar-nominated films.
lesson: A prestigious, mission-driven content strategy can still be financially unsustainable as distribution economics shift under streaming.

name: Patch (AOL)
summary: AOL's ambitious network of hundreds of hyperlocal news sites was gutted in 2013-2014 with mass layoffs and site closures after failing to prove a sustainable local-ad revenue model at scale.
lesson: Scaling a local-news format to hundreds of towns simultaneously outpaces the ability to prove unit economics work in even one.

name: Path
summary: Intimate 'close friends' mobile social network never reached scale against Facebook; sold to Daum Kakao in 2015 and shut down in 2018.
lesson: A beautifully designed niche product can still lose to the incumbent's network effects.

name: Paxata
summary: Self-service data preparation platform acquired by DataRobot in 2019; the standalone product was discontinued shortly after as DataRobot integrated its technology.
lesson: Data-prep tools raised huge sums competing for a market that hyperscalers and BI vendors later absorbed natively.

name: PeachDish
summary: PeachDish was an Atlanta-based Southern-cuisine meal-kit company that partnered with regional retailers but shut down in 2019 as larger national players like HelloFresh and Blue Apron dominated the category.
lesson: Regional meal-kit players struggle to match the marketing and logistics scale of national competitors.

name: Peapod
summary: One of the earliest online grocery delivery services, owned by Ahold Delhaize, was shut down in most U.S. markets in 2021 amid competition from Instacart and retailer-owned delivery.
lesson: First-mover advantage in ecommerce grocery erodes if the technology platform isn't continually modernized.

name: Pear Therapeutics
summary: Pioneer of FDA-cleared prescription digital therapeutics for addiction and insomnia went public via SPAC but filed for bankruptcy in 2023 after failing to secure adequate insurance reimbursement.
lesson: Prescription digital therapeutics need a proven reimbursement pathway before scaling burn.

name: Pebble
summary: Pioneering smartwatch that ran two record-breaking Kickstarter campaigns but couldn't compete with Apple Watch and Fitbit; assets acquired by Fitbit in 2016.
lesson: Crowdfunding hype doesn't guarantee a sustainable business against deep-pocketed competitors.

name: Pebble Technology
funding: $35M+ VC raised plus record Kickstarter campaigns
summary: Smartwatch pioneer Pebble, popular for fitness and notification tracking, ran out of cash and sold its software assets to Fitbit in 2016, ceasing hardware production.
lesson: Crowdfunding success does not guarantee sustainable capital for ongoing hardware competition against deep-pocketed rivals.

name: Peeple
summary: 'Yelp for people' app letting users publicly rate and review other individuals; faced immediate, overwhelming public backlash over privacy and harassment concerns and never gained real traction.
lesson: Applying a review-platform model directly to human beings crosses a trust threshold that most of the public will reject outright.

name: PeerStreet
summary: Real estate debt investing marketplace that let retail investors buy fractions of mortgage loans wound down operations after loan defaults spiked and liquidity dried up.
lesson: Marketplaces built on credit assets are extremely exposed to interest-rate and default cycles.

name: Peloton
summary: Connected fitness hardware maker whose pandemic-era boom collapsed post-2021, wiping out over 90% of its stock value and forcing out its founder amid mass layoffs.
lesson: Scaling manufacturing and headcount for pandemic-level demand becomes a crippling liability the moment demand normalizes.

name: Peloton Interactive
summary: Peloton's valuation collapsed from roughly $50B to under $2B after pandemic-driven overexpansion in manufacturing and headcount met a sharp post-pandemic demand drop, triggering mass layoffs and a CEO change.
lesson: Scaling production and hiring to match a temporary demand spike can destroy value once the pull-forward reverses.

name: Peloton Technology
summary: Truck-platooning technology company Peloton Technology shut down in 2023 after pivoting away from platooning to driver-assist safety systems failed to find a sustainable customer base.
lesson: Abandoning a differentiated core technology for a crowded adjacent market rarely restores a struggling company's trajectory.

name: Penrose Studios
summary: Penrose Studios, an award-winning VR animation studio founded by Eugene Chung, shut down in 2019 after failing to build a sustainable business model around VR narrative films.
lesson: Critical acclaim for VR storytelling did not translate into a commercially sustainable studio absent a large paying VR audience.
source: Variety reporting on Penrose Studios closure, 2019

name: Percolata
summary: Retail workforce-scheduling and foot-traffic analytics startup backed by prominent Silicon Valley investors wound down operations around 2020 after a co-founder lawsuit and inability to scale beyond early retail pilot customers.
lesson: Workforce analytics tools face a difficult sale when they ask retail managers to justify staffing cuts, slowing enterprise adoption regardless of the data's accuracy.

name: Percolate
summary: Content marketing platform Percolate raised over $130M at a high valuation but was sold to Seismic in 2019 in what was widely reported as a distressed, fire-sale acquisition.
lesson: Heavily-funded enterprise martech startups can still fail if sales cycles and retention don't justify the burn rate.
source: TechCrunch, Business Insider coverage of Percolate/Seismic deal 2019

name: Persado
summary: AI marketing-language platform Persado, once valued near unicorn status, was acquired by WPP in 2024 in a deal reported to be well below its peak valuation after growth stalled.
lesson: AI-driven martech valuations built during a hype cycle can compress sharply once growth normalizes.
source: Wall Street Journal, AdExchanger coverage of WPP-Persado acquisition 2024

name: Petal
summary: Credit-card fintech using cash-flow underwriting to serve credit-invisible consumers; sold its card portfolio to Prosper in 2023 and wound down its core consumer card-issuing business after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Alternative-underwriting credit products still require balance-sheet scale and cheap funding that early-stage fintechs struggle to secure.

name: Pets.com
summary: Online pet supplies retailer famous for its sock-puppet mascot burned through IPO proceeds on marketing and cheap shipping of heavy, low-margin goods before closing in 2000.
lesson: Marketing spend cannot compensate for a fundamentally unprofitable shipping and margin structure.

name: Phase Four
summary: In-space electric propulsion (radio-frequency thruster) manufacturer shut down in 2023 after failing to close a new funding round amid a broader downturn in space-tech venture investment.
lesson: Component suppliers to the satellite industry are exposed to the same funding cycles as their customers, compounding downturn risk.

name: Photobucket
summary: Once one of the largest photo-hosting sites on the web, it alienated millions of users in 2017 by abruptly demanding payment to keep third-party image links working, triggering a mass exodus.
lesson: Monetization changes that ignore how customers actually use a free product can destroy years of accumulated user trust overnight.

name: Phrasee
summary: AI-powered marketing copywriting platform Phrasee shut down in 2023, citing an inability to raise further funding amid a tightening venture market and rising competition from general-purpose generative AI tools.
lesson: Narrow AI-copywriting tools can be rapidly commoditized once general-purpose large language models arrive.
source: TechCrunch, Marketing Week coverage of Phrasee shutdown 2023

name: Pillo Health
summary: A Boston home health robot that dispensed medication, tracked adherence and connected patients (many elderly) with caregivers via facial recognition; its technology was absorbed by Stanley Black & Decker in 2020 and the product line was discontinued.
lesson: Consumer health robotics is one of the most capital-intensive categories in aging tech, and even novel, well-reviewed products often end as an acqui-hire rather than a standalone business.

name: Ping (Apple)
summary: Apple's music-focused social network embedded in iTunes failed to gain traction against Facebook and Twitter and was quietly shut down after two years.
lesson: Bolting a social network onto an existing product does not create genuine network effects.

name: Pivotal CRM
summary: Pivotal CRM, an enterprise CRM suite with roots in several acquisitions, was sunset by owner Aptean around 2020 as customers were migrated to newer platforms.
lesson: CRM platforms passed between multiple private-equity owners over decades rarely receive the reinvestment needed to remain competitive.

name: Pivotal Software
summary: Enterprise PaaS spin-off from EMC/VMware that IPO'd in 2018 but saw its stock collapse within a year, leading VMware to reacquire it in 2019 at a steep loss to public investors.
lesson: An enterprise PaaS built for a pre-Kubernetes world struggled once the market standardized on container orchestration.

name: Pixvana
summary: Pixvana built cloud-based VR video production and streaming tools (SPIN Studio) but shut down in 2020 after failing to reach sustainable revenue as VR video demand stayed niche.
lesson: Tooling businesses built around a content format (VR video) fail if the underlying consumer audience for that format never scales.

name: Planetary Resources
summary: High-profile asteroid-mining company backed by Google and Virgin founders ran out of funding in 2018 and sold its assets to blockchain company ConsenSys in a fire sale.
lesson: A speculative decades-out mission narrative can attract celebrity investors but cannot substitute for a near-term revenue plan.

name: Plant Prefab
summary: California-based prefab home manufacturer that laid off most staff and halted production in 2023 amid financial struggles despite high-profile backers.
lesson: Marquee investors and press attention do not substitute for proven manufacturing unit economics in capital-intensive housing.
source: The Real Deal, Dwell coverage of Plant Prefab struggles

name: Plastiq
summary: Bill-payment platform that let businesses pay non-card-accepting vendors with a credit card; filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in May 2023 after burning through venture funding without a durable margin.
lesson: Interchange-fee arbitrage business models are vulnerable when partner banks or card networks tighten terms.

name: Plated
summary: Plated was one of the original meal-kit startups and was acquired by grocery chain Albertsons in 2017, but Albertsons shut the brand down in 2019 after failing to integrate it profitably.
lesson: Meal-kit acquisitions by grocers often fail when the acquirer cannot integrate a fundamentally different operating model.

name: Platfora
summary: Big-data visualization startup that raised over $100M but was acquired by Workday for a fraction of that in 2016; Workday discontinued the product in 2017.
lesson: Heavy capital consumption without proportional revenue growth in enterprise BI often ends in a distressed sale rather than an exit.

name: PledgeMusic
summary: Direct-to-fan music crowdfunding platform that collapsed in 2019 after failing to pay artists funds raised on its site amid severe financial mismanagement.
lesson: Platforms holding customer or artist funds must maintain rigorous financial controls or risk total collapse.

name: Plenty
summary: SoftBank-backed vertical farming company Plenty, once valued near $1 billion with backing from Jeff Bezos, filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in March 2025.
lesson: Even massive venture funding cannot substitute for a path to positive unit economics in indoor farming.

name: Pluralsight
summary: Tech-skills learning platform that IPO'd in 2018 but saw its stock collapse amid slowing enterprise growth, leading Vista Equity Partners to take it private in 2021 at a steep valuation discount.
lesson: Enterprise subscription growth must keep pace with public-market expectations or valuations reset sharply.

name: Podio
summary: Danish no-code work-management platform acquired by Citrix in 2012; Citrix formally sunset Podio in 2023 after years of limited investment.
lesson: Products acquired as adjacent bets inside a larger portfolio can be starved of investment and eventually retired.

name: Point (Point Technologies)
summary: US debit card and cash-back banking startup shut down in 2023, returning remaining customer funds after failing to secure additional funding.
lesson: Consumer neobanks funding rewards programs from interchange revenue alone often cannot sustain the model at scale.

name: Poloniex
summary: Once a top-five global crypto exchange by volume, Poloniex's relevance declined sharply after 2019 delistings and a 2023 hack that drained over $100M, though it continues to operate at diminished scale.
lesson: Losing product and compliance leadership to newer exchanges can turn a former top-five platform into an afterthought even without a single fatal event.
source: Poloniex hack disclosure and exchange ranking data, 2023

name: Powa Technologies
summary: Mobile commerce and marketing platform Powa Technologies collapsed into administration in 2016 after its billionaire founder's claims of a multi-billion-dollar valuation proved unsupported by actual revenue.
lesson: Valuations built on founder hype rather than verifiable revenue eventually collide with reality.
source: Financial Times, BBC coverage of Powa Technologies collapse 2016

name: Power2SME
summary: Indian B2B procurement marketplace for small and medium manufacturers that was folded into and rebranded under Moglix in 2022 after failing to scale independently.
lesson: A niche procurement marketplace can be absorbed by a better-capitalized rival once its independent growth stalls.
source: Indian tech press coverage of the Power2SME-Moglix merger (2022)

name: Preact
summary: Customer success software startup Preact was acquired by Aptean in 2018, after which the product was discontinued as a standalone offering.
lesson: Small, thinly-funded SaaS tools in a crowded segment often end their run as a quiet technology acquisition rather than an independent success.

name: PrecisionHawk
summary: Agricultural drone-data pioneer PrecisionHawk wound down its commercial operations and sold off assets in 2023 after years of pivoting business models.
lesson: Repeated business-model pivots in a capital-intensive category can exhaust runway before any version reaches product-market fit.

name: Precor
summary: Commercial fitness equipment maker Precor was acquired by Peloton for $420M in 2020 to build manufacturing capacity, then sold off in 2022 as Peloton retrenched from its overexpansion.
lesson: An acquisition made to solve a short-term capacity problem can be reversed at a loss once the acquirer's strategy changes.

name: PredPol / Geolitica
summary: Predictive policing software rebranded as Geolitica ceased operations at the end of 2023 after studies found it barely more accurate than random guessing and reinforced racial bias.
lesson: Predictive policing algorithms that reinforce existing patrol bias without proven crime-reduction results eventually lose government customers.

name: Prescient (AASI)
summary: Structural framing systems company for modular and multifamily construction that underwent significant financial restructuring around 2023-2024 amid a slowdown in multifamily construction starts.
lesson: Construction-technology suppliers tied to a single end-market (multifamily) are highly exposed to that market's cyclical downturns.
source: Trade press coverage of Prescient/AASI restructuring

name: Prime Trust
summary: Crypto custodian and infrastructure provider serving fintech and crypto firms filed for bankruptcy in 2023 after a shortfall in customer crypto holdings was discovered.
lesson: Custodians must reconcile customer asset balances continuously; even a plumbing company can implode from unmonitored shortfalls.
source: Prime Trust Chapter 11 filings; Nevada Financial Institutions Division order, 2023

name: Primus Power
summary: Primus Power developed zinc-bromide flow batteries for grid-scale storage but wound down operations around 2019 after failing to reach commercial scale profitably.
lesson: Flow battery chemistries promise long-duration storage but require enormous capital to reach cost parity with lithium-ion at scale.

name: Principle
summary: Popular Mac app for designing animated and interactive UI prototypes whose development was discontinued in 2022 after its solo creator moved on.
lesson: Tools reliant on a single maintainer face existential risk if that person's priorities or capacity change.

name: Prism Skylabs
summary: Video-analytics startup that helped retailers analyze in-store camera footage for customer behavior insights shut down in 2018 after failing to find a sustainable enterprise sales model.
lesson: Retail video analytics needs a clear, immediate ROI metric for store operators, or enterprise sales cycles will outlast the runway.

name: Prisma
summary: Berlin-based startup that shut down its hosted Prisma Cloud data-layer product in 2022 after a strategic pivot, laying off staff to refocus entirely on its open-source ORM.
lesson: When a startup's original hosted-platform business model fails to gain traction, refocusing on a narrower open-source niche can be the only path to survival.

name: Project Ara (Google)
summary: Google's modular smartphone project let users swap out camera, battery, and other modules, but was cancelled in 2016 after failing to solve reliability and cost engineering challenges.
lesson: Modular hardware sounds compelling but real-world engineering tradeoffs can defeat the concept.

name: Prosper Marketplace
summary: One of the original P2P lending marketplaces that underwent a major crisis in 2016 with mass layoffs and investor pullback, and has operated at sharply reduced scale in the years since.
lesson: Marketplace lenders depend on institutional investor confidence in loan quality, which can evaporate industry-wide after a single peer's scandal.

name: Proterra
summary: Electric bus and battery systems maker Proterra filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy in 2023 after supply chain costs and production delays outpaced its ability to raise further capital, later selling its bus unit.
lesson: Diversifying into multiple capital-intensive product lines simultaneously (buses, batteries, charging) can spread resources too thin.

name: Proteus Digital Health
summary: Pioneer of ingestible 'digital pill' sensors that tracked medication adherence failed to find commercial traction with payers and providers and filed for bankruptcy in 2020.
lesson: A technically impressive product still needs a clear reimbursement path to become a business.

name: Provident Financial
summary: UK sub-prime doorstep lender shut down its consumer credit division in 2021 after regulatory pressure and a surge in customer complaints made the business model unviable.
lesson: Legacy consumer lending models can become unprofitable once compliance costs from a wave of mis-selling claims rise sharply.

name: Pulse Secure
summary: Enterprise VPN vendor's flagship product became one of the most widely exploited attack vectors of 2019-2021 after critical vulnerabilities were used in major ransomware campaigns, badly damaging its reputation before it was folded into Ivanti in 2020.
lesson: Chronic, widely-exploited vulnerabilities in a security product can destroy customer trust faster than any competitive pressure.

name: PurchasePro.com
summary: B2B procurement marketplace that partnered with AOL; collapsed after an accounting fraud scandal involving inflated revenue from the AOL deal, leading to SEC charges and criminal convictions of executives.
lesson: Revenue recognition manipulated through side deals eventually surfaces and destroys marketplace credibility overnight.
source: SEC litigation release on PurchasePro.com fraud case

name: Purple Carrot
summary: Purple Carrot was a pioneering plant-based meal-kit company that was acquired in 2022, but its new parent ceased operations for the brand in 2024 amid the broader meal-kit industry contraction.
lesson: Being first to a plant-based niche within meal kits does not protect against category-wide demand decline.

name: Pypestream
summary: Enterprise conversational AI platform Pypestream underwent severe layoffs and effectively wound down most operations in 2023 after failing to convert large funding rounds into durable enterprise revenue.
lesson: Large enterprise AI contracts can mask weak underlying unit economics until the funding environment tightens.
source: Business Insider and industry reporting on Pypestream layoffs, 2023

name: Q-Bot
summary: UK robotics company building robots for under-floor insulation retrofits in construction that entered financial administration amid funding difficulties in the mid-2020s.
lesson: Construction robotics serving a niche retrofit market can struggle to reach volume before capital runs out, especially when government retrofit incentives are uncertain.
source: UK construction trade press coverage of Q-Bot administration

name: Q-Cells SE
summary: Germany's Q-Cells was once the world's largest solar cell manufacturer by output but filed for insolvency in 2012 after Chinese competitors drove panel prices down faster than it could cut costs.
lesson: Manufacturing scale in solar cells didn't protect a European incumbent from lower-cost Asian competition.

name: QuadrigaCX
summary: Canada's largest crypto exchange collapsed after founder Gerald Cotten died suddenly in 2018 allegedly holding the only keys to $190M in customer crypto, later found to have been running a Ponzi-like scheme.
lesson: Single-key custody with no institutional controls turns founder mortality into existential company risk.

name: Quartet Health
summary: Mental-health referral and care-coordination platform that connected primary care patients to behavioral health providers shut down operations in 2023 after failing to reach profitability.
lesson: Coordination platforms need a durable revenue model beyond thin per-referral fees.

name: Quartz
funding: Sold multiple times; last sold for reported ~$1
